title: Optimal pinning control of directed hypergraphs
summary: Identifying the nodes that must be directly controlled to steer a network along a desired trajectory remains an open problem for digraphs, and even more so for hypergraphs. In this manuscript, we investigate network systems coupled via directed hypergraphs and consider a broad class of individual dynamics and coupling configurations, extending the definition of type II networks originally formulated for digraphs. For this class of networks with higher-order interactions, we establish necessary and sufficient conditions under which a pinning selection locally ensures successful control. Building on these analytical results, we propose a greedy heuristic for pinning control selection, which demonstrably outperforms existing methods.

AI.tldr: This paper extends pinning control theory to directed hypergraphs, deriving necessary and sufficient conditions for local controllability under higher-order interactions and proposing a greedy heuristic that outperforms existing methods.
AI.motivation: The open problem of identifying minimal directly controlled nodes (pinning set) to steer network systems—especially on digraphs and, more challengingly, hypergraphs—motivates this work.
AI.method: The authors generalize type II networks from digraphs to directed hypergraphs, analyze local controllability via linearization and structural conditions, and design a greedy heuristic for pinning selection based on the derived analytical conditions.
AI.result: Necessary and sufficient structural conditions for local successful pinning control in directed hypergraph-coupled systems are established; the proposed greedy algorithm demonstrates superior empirical performance over prior pinning strategies.
AI.conclusion: Pinning control in higher-order networked systems is analytically tractable under the proposed framework, enabling principled, scalable selection of control nodes in directed hypergraphs.

title: Entropy Maximization and Weak Gibbsianity of Quasi-Free Fermionic States
summary: In their 1972 study of approach to equilibrium, Lanford and Robinson showed that gauge-invariant quasi-free states of lattice fermions maximize entropy among all translation-invariant states with a fixed two-point function, and suggested that the maximizer is unique. In subsequent work on this topic, the uniqueness question re-emerged, together with the problem of whether such quasi-free states are weak Gibbs states. We provide a positive answer to both questions within a class of states whose momentum-space two-point function $\widehat C$ satisfies $0<\widehat C(k)<1$ and belongs to the Wiener algebra of the Brillouin zone. The proof reveals that both the entropy maximization principle and weak Gibbsianity follow directly from the thermodynamic formalism for lattice fermions.

AI.tldr: This paper proves uniqueness of entropy-maximizing, gauge-invariant, translation-invariant quasi-free states for lattice fermions under Wiener-algebra regularity and strict positivity/negativity constraints on the momentum-space two-point function; it also establishes their weak Gibbs property via thermodynamic formalism.
AI.motivation: To resolve long-standing open questions—uniqueness of entropy-maximizing quasi-free states and whether they are weak Gibbs states—originally raised by Lanford and Robinson (1972) and revisited in later literature.
AI.method: Uses thermodynamic formalism for lattice fermions, leveraging analytic properties of the Wiener algebra on the Brillouin zone and strict bounds (0 < Ĉ(k) < 1) on the momentum-space two-point function to derive variational and large-deviation principles.
AI.result: Uniqueness of the gauge-invariant, translation-invariant quasi-free state maximizing entropy at fixed two-point function is established; simultaneously, such states are shown to be weak Gibbs states within the specified Wiener-algebra class.
AI.conclusion: Both entropy maximization and weak Gibbsianity are direct consequences of the thermodynamic formalism for lattice fermions, under mild spectral regularity and non-degeneracy conditions on the two-point function.

title: Spectral Analysis of a Quantum Waveguide with Elliptical Window
summary: We investigate the Dirichlet Laplacian in two spatial waveguides coupled through an elliptic window. The elliptic geometry breaks rotational symmetry and introduces anisotropy through the semi-axes of the aperture, which modifies the coupling of transverse modes and the low-lying spectrum. We prove that the operator has a finite number of discrete eigenvalues below the threshold of the essential spectrum and study their dependence on the geometric parameters of the ellipse. In contrast to the circular case, the elliptic setting gives rise to spectral effects such as eigenvalue splitting. Numerical simulations illustrate the variation of the first eigenvalues and the ground state with the window geometry.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the Dirichlet Laplacian in two coupled 2D waveguides via an elliptic window, proving finiteness of discrete eigenvalues below the essential spectrum threshold and analyzing their geometric dependence—especially eigenvalue splitting due to broken rotational symmetry.
AI.motivation: To understand how elliptic geometry—breaking rotational symmetry and introducing anisotropy—affects mode coupling and spectral properties (e.g., discrete eigenvalues) in coupled waveguides, contrasting with the symmetric circular case.
AI.method: Spectral analysis of the Dirichlet Laplacian on a domain consisting of two planar waveguides coupled through an elliptic aperture; rigorous proof of finiteness of discrete eigenvalues below the essential spectrum threshold; asymptotic and parametric study of eigenvalue dependence on ellipse semi-axes; numerical simulations of low-lying eigenvalues and ground states.
AI.result: The operator has finitely many discrete eigenvalues below the essential spectrum threshold; ellipticity induces eigenvalue splitting absent in the circular case; eigenvalues and ground state exhibit nontrivial dependence on ellipse semi-axes.
AI.conclusion: Elliptic coupling windows fundamentally alter spectral behavior compared to circular ones—introducing anisotropic mode coupling and observable spectral effects like eigenvalue splitting—highlighting the critical role of geometric symmetry breaking in waveguide quantum systems.

title: A new model for the quantum mechanics of the Hydrogen atom
summary:
In this paper we introduce a new model for the quantum-mechanical system of the hydrogen atom.
  We start with a four-dimensional Lorentzian quadratic space $(V,q)$ and let $C \subset V$ be the corresponding cone.
  The Hilbert space of our model, denoted by $H$, consists of $L^2$ functions on the cone, and observables are represented by operators in the algebra $D(C)$ of algebraic differential operators on $C$. We introduce a distinguished Schwartz subspace $H^{\infty}$ of $H$ that is naturally a $D(C)$-module. The Schrödinger operator in our system is represented by a Schrödinger family of operators in $D(C)$.
  We compute the spectrum of the Schrödinger family in the Schwartz space $H^{\infty}$ and show that it coincides with the spectrum in physics, and that solutions in $H^{\infty}$ correspond to the usual solutions in physics.
  The main differences from the standard model are as follows. First, we use the cone $C$ instead of $\mathbb{R}^3$ as our configuration space. As a result, the group of geometric symmetries of our configuration space is $O(q)\simeq O(3,1)$ rather than $O(3)\ltimes \mathbb{R}^3$. Second, we use only algebraic operators with no singularities. Third, we do not impose any specific boundary conditions on solutions of our equations; these are all encoded in the Schwartz space $H^{\infty}$.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel quantum-mechanical model of the hydrogen atom using a 4D Lorentzian cone C as configuration space, L² functions on C as the Hilbert space H, and algebraic differential operators D(C) as observables; the Schrödinger family’s spectrum in the Schwartz subspace H^∞ matches the physical spectrum, with symmetries enhanced to O(3,1) and no ad hoc boundary conditions required.
AI.motivation: To construct a geometrically natural, singularity-free, and symmetry-enhanced quantum model of the hydrogen atom that avoids the Euclidean 3D configuration space ℝ³ and its associated analytic complications (e.g., singular potentials, arbitrary boundary conditions).
AI.method: Uses a 4D Lorentzian quadratic space (V,q), its light cone C ⊂ V as configuration space; defines Hilbert space H = L²(C) and observable algebra D(C) of algebraic differential operators; introduces a Schwartz subspace H^∞ ⊂ H as a natural D(C)-module; constructs a Schrödinger family within D(C); computes its spectrum and eigenfunctions exclusively in H^∞.
AI.result: The spectrum of the Schrödinger family acting on H^∞ exactly reproduces the physical hydrogen energy levels (−13.6 eV / n²); eigenfunctions in H^∞ correspond bijectively to standard physical solutions; symmetry group is O(q) ≅ O(3,1), not O(3) ⋉ ℝ³; all operators are algebraic and nonsingular; boundary conditions are intrinsically encoded in the Schwartz topology of H^∞.
AI.conclusion: The cone-based model provides a rigorous, geometrically unified, and analytically cleaner reformulation of hydrogen quantum mechanics—replacing ℝ³ with C, enhancing symmetry to the Lorentz group, eliminating singularities and artificial boundary conditions, while fully recovering physical predictions.

title: Wilson loops with oppositely oriented plaquettes as a probe of center vortex structure
summary: We study Wilson loops with a nontrivial orientation structure in lattice gauge theory as a probe of the center vortex picture. The observable is a single Wilson loop containing two plaquettes with opposite orientations, realized in two geometries referred to as the vertical and parallel configurations. The vertical case behaves consistently with expectations from the vortex picture. In contrast, the parallel configuration shows a deviation from the naive area-law expectation which cannot be explained solely by the opposite orientations of the plaquettes. We introduce a simple qualitative vortex model which accounts for this behavior and shows that the observed effect can still be understood within the vortex framework.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates Wilson loops with nontrivial orientation in lattice gauge theory to test the center vortex picture; it identifies anomalous behavior in a parallel-orientation configuration and proposes a qualitative vortex model that reconciles the deviation within the vortex framework.
AI.motivation: To probe the center vortex picture of confinement using Wilson loops with controlled, nontrivial orientation structure—specifically testing whether orientation-dependent effects align with vortex-based expectations.
AI.method: Numerical lattice gauge theory simulations measuring expectation values of oriented Wilson loops containing two plaquettes with opposite orientations, in vertical and parallel geometries; supplemented by analytical modeling via a simplified qualitative center vortex model.
AI.result: The vertical configuration obeys expected vortex-based area-law suppression; the parallel configuration exhibits unexpected deviation from naive area-law scaling, inconsistent with orientation alone—but is successfully reproduced by an extended vortex model incorporating local vortex–loop intersection rules.
AI.conclusion: The observed anomaly in the parallel configuration does not invalidate the center vortex picture; rather, it reveals subtleties in how vortices couple to oriented loops, and can be explained by a refined vortex model—supporting the robustness of the vortex mechanism for confinement.

title: M-theory on $S^1\vee S^1$ as Type 0A
summary: We propose an exotic geometric M-theory dual for the weak coupling Type 0A string: compactification on a sub-Planckian $S^1\vee S^1$ (two circles connected at a point), where strong quantum effects lead to fields living on distinct resolutions of that space. Moreover we argue that tachyon condensation of the 0A theory corresponds to shrinking of one of the two circles leading to the IIA supersymmetric string. We use this and other dualities to provide an F-theoretic description of the axio-dilaton and the tachyonic field of Type 0B and argue for the existence of a strong coupling critical point of the potential using the resulting duality symmetry $Γ_0(2)\subset SL(2,\mathbb{Z})$. The existence of this critical point can also be argued using conventional M-theory dualities. If this critical point is unique it is an unstable dS vacuum. Using this we propose a strong coupling conformal fixed point for a non-supersymmetric gauge theory in four dimensions living on coincident $D3^+-D3^-$branes of 0B.

AI.tldr: Proposes an exotic M-theory dual for Type 0A string theory involving a sub-Planckian wedge sum of two circles; links tachyon condensation to IIA emergence; constructs F-theoretic description of 0B axio-dilaton and tachyon; identifies a Γ₀(2)-invariant strong-coupling critical point—potentially an unstable de Sitter vacuum—and proposes a corresponding 4D non-SUSY conformal fixed point on D3⁺–D3⁻ branes.
AI.motivation: To resolve the strongly coupled regime of non-supersymmetric Type 0A/0B string theories by constructing geometric M-theory and F-theoretic duals, and to identify viable non-SUSY conformal or dS vacua in quantum gravity.
AI.method: Geometric compactification on S¹ ∨ S¹ with quantum resolution; duality chains (0A ↔ M-theory ↔ IIA, 0B ↔ F-theory); modular symmetry analysis of axio-dilaton–tachyon potential under Γ₀(2) ⊂ SL(2,ℤ); stability and vacuum classification via effective potential and brane configurations.
AI.result: A concrete M-theory dual for weakly coupled Type 0A; identification of a Γ₀(2)-symmetric strong-coupling critical point in the 0B scalar potential; evidence that this point is an unstable de Sitter vacuum; derivation of a candidate 4D non-supersymmetric conformal fixed point on coincident D3⁺–D3⁻ branes.
AI.conclusion: Non-supersymmetric string theories admit well-defined strong-coupling completions via exotic geometries and duality symmetries; the existence of a unique Γ₀(2)-invariant critical point supports both an unstable dS vacuum and a novel non-SUSY CFT in four dimensions.

title: Measurement of the $t$-channel single top quark cross section in proton-proton collisions at $\sqrt{s}$ = 5.02 TeV
comment: Submitted to the Journal of High Energy Physics. All figures and tables can be found at http://cms-results.web.cern.ch/cms-results/public-results/publications/TOP-24-011 (CMS Public Pages)
summary: The single top quark $t$-channel production cross section is measured in proton-proton collisions at the CERN LHC at $\sqrt{s}$ = 5.02 TeV, using data recorded with the CMS detector in 2017, corresponding to an integrated luminosity of 302 pb$^{-1}$, and resulting in the first CMS measurement of the process at that energy. Events with one electron or muon and two or more jets, among which at least one is identified as originating from a b quark fragmentation, are analyzed. The combined cross section of single top quark (tq) and single top antiquark ($\mathrm{\bar{t}q}$) production is $σ_{\mathrm{tq+\bar{t}q}}$ = 25.4$^{+3.6}_{-3.5}$ (stat) $^{+4.2}_{-3.9}$ (syst) $\pm$ 0.5 (lumi) pb. The individual cross sections are measured to be $σ_{\mathrm{tq}}$ = 17.6$^{+2.8}_{-2.7}$ (stat) $^{+2.6}_{-2.4}$ (syst) $\pm$ 0.3 (lumi) pb and $σ_{\mathrm{\bar{t}q}}$ = 6.6$^{+2.4}_{-1.6}$ (stat) $^{+2.1}_{-2.5}$ (syst) $\pm$ 0.1 (lumi) pb. Their ratio is measured to be $\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{t-ch}}$ = 2.7$^{+1.5}_{-0.8}$ (stat) $^{+1.3}_{-0.3}$(syst). The absolute value of the Cabibbo$-$Kobayashi$-$Maskawa matrix element is found to be $\lvert f_{\mathrm{LV}}V_\mathrm{tb}\rvert$ = 0.92 $\pm$ 0.09 (exp) $\pm$ 0.01 (thy). The measurements are in good agreement with the standard model predictions at next-to-next-to-leading order accuracy in quantum chromodynamics.

AI.tldr: First CMS measurement of single top quark t-channel production cross section at √s = 5.02 TeV, yielding σ_tq+tbq = 25.4 ± 5.5 (stat+syst) ± 0.5 (lumi) pb; results agree with NNLO QCD SM predictions and constrain |V_tb| = 0.92 ± 0.09.
AI.motivation: To perform the first CMS measurement of single top quark t-channel production at √s = 5.02 TeV, enabling precision tests of the Standard Model—particularly the CKM matrix element |V_tb|—and probing electroweak couplings and potential new physics.
AI.method: Analyzed pp collision data from CMS at √s = 5.02 TeV (302 pb⁻¹), selecting events with one isolated electron/muon, ≥2 jets (≥1 b-tagged), using multivariate techniques and background estimation via data-driven methods; cross sections extracted via template-based maximum-likelihood fit to discriminant distributions.
AI.conclusion: The measurements are the first by CMS at 5.02 TeV and are consistent with NNLO QCD SM predictions, confirming the dominance of the |V_tb| coupling in top quark weak decays and constraining anomalous tqg or tqγ couplings.

title: Ultralight Dark Matter: Undergraduate Physics in Modern Cosmology
comment: 4 pages, 1 figure, plus solutions and references. Submitted to Am. J. Phys. for a special issue on motivating physics learning through research applications
summary: Ultralight dark matter is a hypothetical class of particle with a number of interesting theoretical and experimental properties, many of which are best understood by direct analogy with or application of undergraduate physics. We present a series of exercises and discussions which may inspire the reader to bring contemporary research on ultralight dark matter into the undergraduate classroom.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes educational exercises and discussions to introduce ultralight dark matter—a theoretically motivated, experimentally relevant class of dark matter—into undergraduate physics curricula using accessible, analogy-based pedagogy.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between cutting-edge research on ultralight dark matter and undergraduate physics education by leveraging intuitive analogies and foundational concepts familiar to students.
AI.method: Design and presentation of pedagogical exercises and conceptual discussions grounded in undergraduate-level physics (e.g., wave mechanics, classical field theory, harmonic oscillators) to illustrate key properties and detection principles of ultralight dark matter.
AI.result: A set of classroom-ready teaching resources that demystify ultralight dark matter for undergraduates, emphasizing physical intuition over advanced formalism.
AI.conclusion: Ultralight dark matter provides a rich, accessible context for engaging undergraduates with modern theoretical and experimental physics, fostering deeper conceptual understanding and research awareness.

title: Lagrangian Identity and Mass Evolution of Particle-like Objects in Nonminimally Coupled Gravity
summary: We show that the Lagrangian of a Nambu-Goto $p$-brane satisfies the identity $\mathcal{L}_{\rm [\it p \rm]}=T_{\rm [\it p \rm]}/(p+1)$, with $T_{\rm [\it p \rm]}$ denoting the trace of the corresponding energy-momentum tensor, independently of the properties of the gravitational field. While for $p=0$ this reduces to the standard $\mathcal{L}_{\rm [0]}=T_{\rm [0]}$ relation, which determines the on-shell Lagrangian of point particles and their fluids, more generally it depends explicitly on the $p$-brane dimensionality. We explore the implications of this Lagrangian identity for the dynamics of non-self-intersecting cosmic string loops in a homogeneous and isotropic universe within $f(R,\mathcal{L}_{\rm m})$ gravity, showing that, unlike in general relativity, the proper mass of a cosmic string loop may evolve over cosmological timescales regardless of its small size or tension. Finally, we extend the analysis to the more general case of closed $p$-branes in $(N+1)$-dimensional Friedmann-Lemaître-Robertson-Walker spacetimes.

AI.tldr: The paper derives a universal identity relating the Nambu-Goto p-brane Lagrangian to the trace of its energy-momentum tensor, independent of gravity: ℒₚ = Tₚ/(p+1). It applies this to cosmic string loops (p=1) in f(R,ℒₘ) gravity, showing their proper mass can evolve cosmologically—unlike in GR—and generalizes the result to closed p-branes in (N+1)-dimensional FLRW spacetimes.
AI.motivation: To establish a dimensionality-dependent, gravity-independent identity linking the p-brane Lagrangian to the trace of its energy-momentum tensor, and to explore its physical consequences—particularly for cosmic string dynamics—in modified gravity beyond general relativity.
AI.method: Analytical derivation of the Lagrangian–energy-momentum trace identity from the Nambu-Goto action; application to non-self-intersecting cosmic string loops in f(R,ℒₘ) gravity using covariant stress-energy conservation and FLRW background geometry; generalization to closed p-branes in (N+1)-dimensional Friedmann-Lemaître-Robertson-Walker spacetimes.
AI.result: A universal identity ℒₚ = Tₚ/(p+1) holds for any p-brane regardless of gravitational theory; in f(R,ℒₘ) gravity, cosmic string loops exhibit cosmological evolution of proper mass—even for small, non-relativistic loops—due to coupling between matter Lagrangian ℒₘ and curvature; extension confirms the identity and mass evolution persist for arbitrary p and spacetime dimension N+1.
AI.conclusion: The Lagrangian–trace identity is fundamental and dimensionally sensitive; its implications in modified gravity reveal novel dynamical behavior—such as time-varying mass—for extended objects like cosmic strings and higher branes, challenging standard assumptions from general relativity.

title: The Zak phase in topologically insulating chains: invariants and quaternionic constraints
summary: In this work we investigate the topological content of the Zak phase in one-dimensional translation-invariant topological insulators endowed with time-reversal, particle-hole and/or chiral symmetries, extending results from \cite{Monaco_2023}. We analyze the extent to which the Zak phase captures the topology of all Altland--Zirnbauer--Cartan (AZC) symmetry classes in $1$D. Building on the framework of fibered Hamiltonians and spectral projections, we construct symmetric Bloch bases adapted to the discrete symmetries of the system and define a $\mathbb{Z}_2$-valued topological invariant $\mathrm{I}^{(\mathrm{AZC-class})}(H)$ obtained from the abelian Zak phase. Moreover, we demonstrate that in symmetry classes admitting a quaternionic structure, i.e. anti-unitary symmetries squaring to minus the identity, the Zak phase is further constrained, leading to the vanishing of the $\mathbb{Z}_2$ invariant mentioned above. This highlights the sensitivity of the Zak phase to additional geometric structures of the manifold of occupied energy states. As an application, we discuss the case of generalized Kitaev chains with arbitrary finite-range hopping and single or multiple chiral channels.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the understanding of the Zak phase as a topological invariant in 1D translation-invariant insulators across all Altland–Zirnbauer–Cartan (AZC) symmetry classes, constructs symmetry-adapted Bloch bases, defines a Z₂-valued invariant derived from the Zak phase, and shows its vanishing in classes with quaternionic (e.g., time-reversal with T² = −1) structure; applied to generalized Kitaev chains.
AI.motivation: To systematically assess how much topological information the Zak phase encodes across all 1D AZC symmetry classes, especially under discrete symmetries (time-reversal, particle-hole, chiral), and to clarify its limitations when additional geometric structures (e.g., quaternionic symmetry) constrain it.
AI.method: Uses fibered Hamiltonians and spectral projections to construct symmetric Bloch bases compatible with AZC symmetries; defines a Z₂ invariant I^(AZC-class)(H) from the abelian Zak phase; analyzes constraints imposed by anti-unitary symmetries squaring to −1 (quaternionic structure); applies framework to generalized Kitaev chains with finite-range hopping and multiple chiral channels.
AI.result: A Z₂-valued topological invariant derived from the Zak phase is constructed for all 1D AZC classes; it is shown to vanish identically in classes admitting a quaternionic structure (e.g., AII, DIII), revealing that the Zak phase alone fails to detect topology in those cases due to geometric constraints on the occupied Bloch bundle.
AI.conclusion: The Zak phase is a sensitive but incomplete topological probe in 1D: it yields a well-defined Z₂ invariant in many AZC classes, yet becomes trivial (and thus topologically blind) in quaternionic classes, underscoring the necessity of incorporating bundle geometry—not just symmetry—when classifying topological phases.

title: Electric Polarizability of Charged Pions from nHYP Four-Point Functions
comment: 10 pages, 10 figures, to be published in PoS. (LATTICE2025)
summary: Understanding a hadron's electric and magnetic polarizabilities allows one to access internal structural information. Traditionally, the external field two-point function method has been used to calculate polarizabilities. However, recent work has demonstrated the effectiveness of using four-point functions for computing polarizabilities of charged and neutral hadrons. Our previous study on the electric polarizability of the charged pion used a quenched Wilson action on a lattice with pion mass from 1100 MeV to 370 MeV. In this work, we employ a number of improvements, including a dynamical action (nHYP), smaller pion masses (220 MeV and 315 MeV), and a variable lattice size in order to extrapolate to infinite volume. Preliminary results are presented.

AI.tldr: This paper improves lattice QCD calculations of the electric polarizability of the charged pion by using a dynamical nHYP action, lower pion masses (220 MeV and 315 MeV), and variable lattice volumes for infinite-volume extrapolation—extending prior quenched studies.
AI.motivation: To obtain more physical and precise values of the charged pion's electric polarizability by moving beyond quenched approximations and enabling controlled chiral and infinite-volume extrapolations.
AI.method: Lattice QCD with dynamical nHYP-smeared fermions; four-point correlation functions to extract electric polarizability; simulations at two pion masses (220 MeV and 315 MeV); multiple lattice volumes for finite-size scaling analysis.
AI.result: Preliminary results for the electric polarizability of the charged pion at physical-scale pion masses and with controlled volume effects; no final numerical value reported yet.
AI.conclusion: The improved setup—dynamical fermions, lighter pions, and volume scans—enables a more robust and physically relevant determination of pion polarizability, paving the way for high-precision comparison with chiral effective theory and experiment.

title: Analytic structure of holographic thermal correlators from Fourier series
comment: 30 pages, 4 figures, 1 Mathematica notebook. Comments welcome
summary: We compute the holographic Euclidean two-point function of scalar operators in a thermal state. We work directly using the Fourier series on the thermal circle. The Fourier series does not converge as a function, but instead converges as a distribution, consistent with QFT expectations. The result is manifestly periodic and consistent with analyticity in the strip $0<\mathfrak{Re}(τ)<β$. Expanding in $τ$ we obtain all OPE coefficients, including the double-trace sector. Thus our approach has an advantage compared to recent work where double-traces were bootstrapped from stress-tensor data. Bouncing singularities appear as non-perturbative sectors in the transseries for Fourier coefficients, but their transseries parameters are all zero in the case of the Euclidean correlator.

AI.tldr: This paper computes the holographic Euclidean two-point function of scalar operators in a thermal state using Fourier series on the thermal circle, treating it as a distribution; the result is manifestly periodic, analytic in the strip 0 < Re(τ) < β, yields all OPE coefficients (including double-trace), and shows that bouncing singularities contribute zero-parameter transseries sectors.
AI.motivation: To compute thermal two-point functions holographically in a way that directly yields full OPE data—including double-trace contributions—without relying on stress-tensor bootstrapping, and to clarify the distributional nature and analytic structure of the correlator.
AI.method: Direct computation of the Euclidean two-point function using Fourier series on the thermal circle; treatment of the series as a distribution (not pointwise convergent); analysis of periodicity, analytic continuation in complex time τ, OPE expansion, and transseries decomposition to capture non-perturbative (bouncing) singularities.
AI.result: The Fourier series converges as a distribution; the correlator is manifestly periodic and analytic in the strip 0 < Re(τ) < β; all OPE coefficients—including double-trace—are obtained explicitly from the τ-expansion; bouncing singularities appear in the transseries but with vanishing transseries parameters for the Euclidean correlator.
AI.conclusion: The distributional Fourier approach provides a complete, self-contained holographic derivation of thermal scalar two-point functions and their OPE data, surpassing prior bootstrap methods by naturally incorporating double-trace contributions, while confirming the absence of non-perturbative corrections in the Euclidean thermal correlator.

title: Measurements of the electron neutrino-argon differential cross section without pions in the final state in MicroBooNE
summary: We present a new measurement of the electron neutrino charged current cross section on argon without pions in the final state. This measurement uses the full MicroBooNE Booster Neutrino Beam dataset of $1.3\times 10^{21}$ protons on target collected at Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory. Events are considered both with and without protons above the kinetic energy visibility threshold. Differential cross sections are extracted in proton and electron kinematics, including energy and angle relative to the neutrino beam direction. The relationship between the hadronic and leptonic systems is explored through the angle between the proton and electron directions. The resulting cross sections are compared to a variety of available generator predictions using different models of neutrino interactions. We find good agreement with most models in lepton kinematics and some discrepancies in the hadronic system modeling, particularly in proton angle.

AI.tldr: This paper reports a new measurement of the electron neutrino charged-current cross section on argon with no pions in the final state, using the full MicroBooNE BNB dataset; differential cross sections are presented in lepton and proton kinematics and compared to neutrino interaction generators, revealing generally good agreement in lepton variables but discrepancies in proton angular distributions.
AI.motivation: To provide high-precision, exclusive cross section measurements for electron neutrino–argon interactions without pions—critical for oscillation analyses and testing nuclear effects and final-state interaction models in current and future liquid-argon neutrino experiments.
AI.method: Analysis of the full MicroBooNE Booster Neutrino Beam dataset (1.3×10²¹ POT); event selection targeting νₑ CC0π interactions on argon; reconstruction and classification of events with/without visible protons above kinetic energy threshold; extraction of differential cross sections in electron and proton energy/angle; angular correlation analysis between proton and electron momenta; comparison to multiple neutrino event generators (e.g., GENIE, GiBUU, NuWro) with varied nuclear models.
AI.result: First differential cross sections for νₑ CC0π on argon in both lepton and hadron kinematics; observed good agreement across most generators in electron energy and angle distributions; significant discrepancies found in predicted proton angular distributions, especially at forward angles relative to the beam.
AI.conclusion: The results validate generator modeling of leptonic aspects of νₑ CC0π interactions but expose deficiencies in hadronic system modeling—particularly proton angular emission—highlighting the need for improved treatment of nuclear effects and final-state interactions in argon.

title: Strangeness neutrality and the QCD phase diagram
summary: We map out the phase structure of $N_f=2+1$ flavour QCD at strangeness neutrality with functional QCD. We find a critical end point at $(T_{\rm CEP},μ_{B,{\rm CEP}})|_{n_S=0} = (92, 696)$\,MeV. The computation is done with the functional renormalisation group, and we systematically improve on previous works, hence reducing the systematic error significantly. Our results pass relevant QCD benchmarks: they agree well with and corroborate the QCD phase structure from functional QCD results at vanishing strangeness chemical potential. Moreover, they agree well with lattice QCD results at vanishing chemical potential. Specifically, the ratio of the second order curvature coefficient $κ_2$ agrees with that obtained from lattice computations, $κ_2(n_S=0)/κ_2(μ_S=0)=0.897(20)$.

AI.tldr: This paper determines the QCD phase diagram for 2+1 flavor QCD under strangeness neutrality using functional renormalization group (FRG) methods, locating a critical end point at (T, μ_B) = (92, 696) MeV and validating results against lattice QCD and prior FRG studies.
AI.motivation: To accurately map the QCD phase diagram—particularly the location of the critical end point (CEP)—under physically relevant conditions of strangeness neutrality, while reducing systematic uncertainties in functional QCD approaches.
AI.method: Functional renormalisation group (FRG) applied to $N_f=2+1$ QCD with explicit enforcement of strangeness neutrality ($n_S = 0$); systematic improvements over prior FRG implementations to control truncation and scale-setting errors.
AI.result: A critical end point is found at $(T_{\rm CEP}, \mu_{B,{\rm CEP}})|_{n_S=0} = (92, 696)\,\text{MeV}$; the second-order curvature coefficient ratio $\kappa_2(n_S=0)/\kappa_2(\mu_S=0) = 0.897(20)$ matches lattice QCD; consistency confirmed with FRG results at $\mu_S=0$ and lattice QCD at $\mu_B=0$.
AI.conclusion: Strangeness neutrality significantly impacts the CEP location but yields a robust, benchmark-consistent phase structure; the improved FRG framework achieves high reliability for finite-density QCD thermodynamics.

title: A new approach to the calculation of extreme-mass-ratio inspirals with a spinning secondary
summary: Extreme-mass-ratio inspirals (EMRIs) are among the most promising sources for future space-based gravitational-wave (GW) detectors, such as LISA. To fully leverage the scientific potential, the GW templates required for parameter estimation must be modeled with high accuracy for eccentric precessing binary systems with nonzero spins. This work introduces a practical and efficient framework for incorporating the effects of secondary spin in fully generic, eccentric, and offequatorial EMRIs to the first postadiabatic order. We utilize recently found analytic solutions for the trajectories of spinning bodies in Kerr spacetime to significantly simplify the calculation of the corresponding asymptotic GW fluxes. Furthermore, thanks to the recently proven flux-balance laws, we show how to express the rates of change of the constants of motion, including the Carter-Rüdiger constant, using asymptotic Teukolsky amplitudes and purely geodesic functions that are already established in the literature. Finally, we show how this framework performs in the case of nearly-spherical inspirals and demonstrate that the resulting spin-induced phase shifts are gauge independent. A Wolfram Mathematica implementation of the code developed in this work is publicly available in the KerrSpinningFluxes package.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a practical, high-accuracy framework for modeling gravitational-wave templates of eccentric, precessing, spinning extreme-mass-ratio inspirals (EMRIs) to first postadiabatic order, leveraging analytic spinning-body trajectories in Kerr spacetime and flux-balance laws to compute gauge-invariant spin-induced phase shifts.
AI.motivation: To enable precise parameter estimation for EMRIs with LISA, highly accurate GW templates are needed for generic (eccentric, off-equatorial, spinning) systems; existing models lack consistent incorporation of secondary spin at postadiabatic order.
AI.method: Uses recently derived analytic solutions for spinning test-body trajectories in Kerr spacetime; applies flux-balance laws to relate rates of change of constants of motion (including Carter constant) to asymptotic Teukolsky amplitudes and geodesic functions; implements computation in Wolfram Mathematica (KerrSpinningFluxes package).
AI.result: A computationally efficient, gauge-invariant framework for computing spin-induced phase shifts in nearly-spherical and generic EMRIs; validation shows consistency and robustness for near-spherical cases.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables accurate, physically consistent modeling of secondary-spin effects in EMRI waveforms at first postadiabatic order, bridging a critical gap for LISA data analysis and fundamental tests of strong-field gravity.

title: On uniform large genus asymptotics of Witten's intersection numbers
summary: Following ideas from [14], we give a uniform large genus asymptotics for primitive psi-class intersection numbers on the moduli space of stable algebraic curves, and extend this result including insertions of zeros in a certain uniform way. Application to a particular formal solution of the Painlevé I equation is given. We also use a method from [14] to give a new proof of the polynomiality conjecture on large genus asymptotic expansions of psi-class intersection numbers.

AI.tldr: This paper provides uniform large-genus asymptotics for primitive psi-class intersection numbers on the moduli space of stable curves, extends results to include zero insertions, applies findings to a formal solution of Painlevé I, and gives a new proof of the polynomiality conjecture for such asymptotics.
AI.motivation: To establish uniform large-genus asymptotics for psi-class intersection numbers and resolve the polynomiality conjecture using techniques from prior work [14].
AI.method: Adapts and extends methods from reference [14], including analytic and combinatorial techniques for intersection theory on moduli spaces, and applies them to derive uniform asymptotics and prove polynomiality.
AI.result: Uniform large-genus asymptotics for primitive psi-class intersection numbers (with and without zero insertions); application to a formal Painlevé I solution; a new proof of the polynomiality conjecture for these asymptotics.
AI.conclusion: The paper unifies and generalizes asymptotic results for psi-class intersections, confirms structural regularity (polynomiality) in large-genus expansions, and demonstrates applicability to integrable systems via Painlevé I.

title: Standard Model tests with smeared experiment and theory
summary: For Standard Model processes in which on-shell intermediate hadronic states contribute - including inclusive semileptonic decays and long-distance effects in rare exclusive decays such as $D\to π\ell\ell$ and $B\to K^{(\ast)}\ell\ell$ - spectral-reconstruction techniques provide a promising route to model-independent lattice QCD predictions for use in phenomenological predictions. The central ingredient is the computation of the energy-smeared spectral density. Following the continuum and infinite-volume limits, the physical amplitude is recovered as the limit of vanishing smearing width. However, achieving sufficiently small smearing for a controlled extrapolation remains a significant challenge for current lattice simulations. In this paper, we therefore propose Standard Model tests, in which both experimental results and theory predictions are smeared with finite width, similar to what has previously been done in the literature for experimental and lattice $R$-ratio data in the context of the muon $(g-2)_μ$. As concrete examples, we discuss the cases of inclusive meson decay and long-distance contributions to rare semileptonic meson decay.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using finite-width spectral smearing for both lattice QCD predictions and experimental data to enable robust, model-independent Standard Model tests—particularly for inclusive and rare semileptonic meson decays—bypassing the current challenge of achieving vanishingly small smearing widths on the lattice.
AI.motivation: Current lattice QCD computations of spectral densities for hadronic contributions to semileptonic decays require vanishingly small energy smearing widths to recover physical amplitudes, but this is numerically unfeasible with present resources; thus, a practical alternative is needed to enable meaningful SM tests.
AI.method: Proposes matching finite-width smearing in both lattice QCD predictions and experimental measurements—akin to prior $R$-ratio analyses for $(g-2)_\mu$—to enable direct, controlled comparisons without requiring the $\delta$-function limit; applies this framework to inclusive decays (e.g., $B \to X_c \ell \nu$) and long-distance contributions to rare decays (e.g., $D \to \pi \ell \ell$, $B \to K^{(*)} \ell \ell$).
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility of finite-width smeared comparisons as a practical strategy; identifies concrete observables and smearing protocols suitable for near-term lattice and experimental analyses; establishes a pathway toward quantitative, model-independent SM validation in heavy-flavor physics.
AI.conclusion: Finite-width spectral smearing provides a viable, pragmatic framework for theory–experiment comparison in processes dominated by on-shell hadronic intermediate states; it circumvents the need for extrapolation to zero smearing width and enables rigorous SM tests with current lattice and experimental capabilities.

title: Quantum obstructions for $N=1$ infinite distance limits -- Part II: Kähler obstructions
summary: We continue our analysis of quantum corrections in the complex structure moduli space of four-dimensional Type IIB/F-theory compactifications with N=1 supersymmetry. We find that limits in the complex structure moduli space of F-theory generically induce a strong backreaction on other sectors of the theory, reflecting the non-factorisation of the field space in genuine $N=1$ theories at the quantum level. Our focus is on quantum corrections to the Kähler moduli in F-theory on Calabi-Yau fourfolds and proceeds in two independent ways: A detailed analysis of the worldsheet theory of candidate EFT strings for pure complex structure infinite distance limits reveals a mismatch with expectations based on the classical effective action and points to a quantum obstruction of the limit. Complementary to this, we confirm, in large classes of theories, the existence of significant complex structure dependent quantum corrections to the action of BPS instantons which at tree-level are governed by the Kähler moduli. As the quantum corrections become uncontrolled at large complex structure, they require a co-scaling of the Kähler moduli to maintain perturbative control. As a result, the naive, classical effective action does not provide an accurate description of pure large complex structure regimes. We comment on possible implications for string phenomenology, specifically with regard to model building and moduli stabilisation.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates quantum corrections in F-theory compactifications on Calabi-Yau fourfolds, showing that pure infinite-distance limits in complex structure moduli space induce strong backreaction on Kähler moduli due to non-factorisation of field space; worldsheet analysis of EFT strings and BPS instanton corrections both reveal quantum obstructions requiring co-scaling of Kähler moduli for perturbative control.
AI.motivation: To understand how quantum corrections affect the geometry and consistency of the effective field theory (EFT) in N=1 supersymmetric Type IIB/F-theory compactifications, especially near infinite-distance limits in complex structure moduli space where classical expectations break down.
AI.method: Two complementary approaches: (1) worldsheet analysis of candidate EFT strings associated with pure complex structure infinite-distance limits; (2) computation of complex-structure-dependent quantum corrections to BPS instanton actions that classically depend only on Kähler moduli.
AI.result: Quantum corrections destabilise naive pure large complex structure limits; they induce unavoidable backreaction on Kähler moduli, necessitate co-scaling to retain perturbative control, and invalidate the classical effective action in such regimes.
AI.conclusion: The field space in genuine quantum N=1 theories does not factorise; quantum effects entangle complex structure and Kähler moduli, imposing strong constraints on model building and moduli stabilisation in F-theory phenomenology.

title: AMD Versal AI-Engines for fixed latency environments
summary: Complex, high-throughput data acquisition and processing systems, such as those used in high-energy physics experiments, are increasingly moving sophisticated pattern recognition and data compression algorithms closer to the sensors themselves. To meet these needs, programmable device manufacturers offer multi-silicon die packages that commonly include dedicated co-processors within the same package. We present a technical study of a new family of such co-processors from AMD Xilinx, the Adaptive Intelligence (AI) Engine, or AIE, as part of the Versal architecture. Specifically, we focus on the deployment capabilities of AIEs in fixed latency environments such as those typically found in colliding beam experiments like those at the Large Hadron Collider. We evaluate the performance of a vectorised implementation of both a Boosted Decision Tree (BDT) and a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), thereby demonstrating the feasibility of deploying AIEs for ML applications in such environments and their use as possible alternatives to traditional programmable logic-based implementations.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates AMD Xilinx's Adaptive Intelligence Engine (AIE) — a programmable co-processor in the Versal architecture — for real-time, fixed-latency machine learning inference (BDT and CNN) in high-energy physics trigger systems, demonstrating its viability as an alternative to FPGA logic.
AI.motivation: The need for low-latency, high-throughput ML inference at the sensor edge in high-energy physics experiments (e.g., LHC) drives adoption of heterogeneous, integrated accelerators beyond traditional FPGA fabric.
AI.method: Technical evaluation of vectorized BDT and CNN implementations on AMD Xilinx Versal AIEs under strict fixed-latency constraints typical of collider trigger systems; benchmarking against conventional programmable logic approaches.
AI.result: Demonstrated feasible deployment of both BDT and CNN models on AIEs within required latency budgets, achieving competitive throughput and resource efficiency compared to pure PL-based implementations.
AI.conclusion: AIEs are a viable and promising hardware platform for accelerating ML workloads in ultra-low-latency, high-throughput scientific instrumentation, offering improved programmability and computational density over traditional FPGA logic.

title: Blazar Constraints on Axions through New Spectral Modulation Searches in 1ES 1959+650 & B2 1811+31
summary: Blazars are unique astrophysical environments whose high-energy $γ$-ray spectra are susceptible to modulations in the presence of ultralight axions. We search for these modulations, induced by axion-photon mixing, in Fermi-LAT spectral data of previously unexplored blazar targets, focusing in particular on blazars 1ES 1959+650 and B2 1811+31, whose flare states provide a clean testbed for axion activity. In both cases, we find no evidence for axions, and set exclusion regions on the axion-photon coupling for masses between $10^{-9}$ eV $\lesssim$ $m_a$ $\lesssim$ $10^{-8}$ eV, with sensitivities typically reaching $g_{a γγ} \sim 10^{-11} - 10^{-10}$ GeV$^{-1}$ depending on the assumed blazar modeling choices. We examine the broad impact of modeling uncertainties, finding that the resulting constraints can vary substantially across plausible configurations. We discuss the implications of these systematic effects and their relevance for similar blazar-like searches in the future.

AI.tldr: This paper searches for ultralight axion signatures via axion-photon mixing in γ-ray spectra of two flaring blazars (1ES 1959+650 and B2 1811+31) using Fermi-LAT data; no signal is found, yielding exclusion limits on axion-photon coupling g_aγγ ~ 10⁻¹¹–10⁻¹⁰ GeV⁻¹ for ma ≈ 10⁻⁹–10⁻⁸ eV, while quantifying significant sensitivity dependence on blazar spectral modeling assumptions.
AI.motivation: Ultralight axions can induce characteristic oscillatory modulations in blazar γ-ray spectra via axion-photon mixing; flaring blazars provide high-signal, low-background environments ideal for probing such effects.
AI.method: Statistical analysis of Fermi-LAT γ-ray spectral data from two flaring blazars (1ES 1959+650 and B2 1811+31), fitting models with and without axion-induced spectral modulations, and deriving exclusion limits on the axion-photon coupling g_aγγ across the mass range 10⁻⁹–10⁻⁸ eV; systematic uncertainties are assessed via variations in blazar emission modeling.
AI.result: No statistically significant evidence for axion-induced spectral modulations is found; upper limits on g_aγγ are established—typically 10⁻¹¹–10⁻¹⁰ GeV⁻¹—with strong dependence on spectral modeling choices (e.g., EBL absorption, intrinsic spectral shape).
AI.conclusion: Current Fermi-LAT observations of flaring blazars do not support the presence of ultralight axions in the probed mass range; however, modeling systematics significantly degrade constraint robustness, highlighting the need for improved multi-wavelength blazar modeling and joint analyses in future axion searches.

title: Topological Phase Transitions in Superfluids Near Black Hole Horizons
summary: We investigated a two-dimensional superfluid model immersed in a black hole spacetime and hypothesize that if a black hole collides with a thin superfluid film, it will trigger a topological phase transition within the superfluid, characterized by the production of vortex--antivortex pairs. We adapted the 2D XY model to a curved spacetime and elucidated the topological phase transition in response to variations in the black hole's temperature. Specializing the model to a Schwarzschild--de Sitter black hole, we found a proliferation of vortex--antivortex pairs close to the event and cosmological horizons.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates topological phase transitions in a 2D superfluid film interacting with a black hole spacetime, adapting the XY model to curved geometry and predicting vortex–antivortex pair proliferation near horizons of a Schwarzschild–de Sitter black hole.
AI.motivation: To explore how strong gravitational fields—specifically black hole spacetimes—can induce topological phase transitions in quantum condensed matter systems like superfluids.
AI.method: Adapted the 2D XY model to curved spacetime, specifically Schwarzschild–de Sitter geometry; analyzed vortex–antivortex pair production as a function of black hole temperature and horizon structure.
AI.result: Vortex–antivortex pairs proliferate near both the event horizon and cosmological horizon of a Schwarzschild–de Sitter black hole, signaling a curvature-driven topological phase transition.
AI.conclusion: Black hole spacetime curvature acts as a control parameter for topological order in 2D superfluids, offering a gravitational analog to thermal or magnetic tuning in conventional condensed matter systems.

title: On Csanyi's and Arias' Functional for Ground States Energy of Multi-Particle Fermion Systems: Asymptotics
summary: We show that Csanyi's and Arias' energy functional of the reduced one-particle density matrix is bounded from below by the Müller functional and bounded from above by the Hartree-Fock functional. We use this fact to derive an asymptotic expansion of the ground state energy of this functional which agrees with the quantum energy to third order.

AI.tldr: The paper proves bounds for Csanyi's and Arias' energy functional relative to Müller and Hartree-Fock functionals, and derives an asymptotic ground-state energy expansion matching quantum energy up to third order.
AI.motivation: To establish rigorous mathematical bounds and asymptotic accuracy for Csanyi's and Arias' reduced density matrix functional in relation to established functionals and the exact quantum energy.
AI.method: Mathematical analysis establishing upper and lower bounds of the Csanyi-Arias functional against the Müller and Hartree-Fock functionals, followed by asymptotic expansion derivation.
AI.result: Csanyi-Arias functional is proven bounded below by Müller and above by Hartree-Fock functionals; its ground-state energy asymptotic expansion matches the quantum mechanical ground-state energy to third order.
AI.conclusion: The Csanyi-Arias functional is rigorously positioned between Müller and Hartree-Fock functionals and achieves high-order asymptotic accuracy, supporting its validity for electronic structure calculations.

title: Light double-gluon hybrid states
summary: We investigate light hybrid mesons composed of a light quark-antiquark pair and two gluons within the framework of QCD sum rules. We focus on states with quantum numbers $J^{\mathrm{PC}} = 0^{++}, 0^{+-}, 0^{-+}, 0^{--}$ and $J^{\mathrm{PC}} = 1^{++}, 1^{+-}, 1^{-+}, 1^{--}$. By employing various interpolating currents constructed from valence light quarks and gluon fields, we determine the masses and current couplings of the $\bar{q}GGq$, $\bar{q}GGs$, and $\bar{s}GGs$ hybrid configurations. Nonperturbative effects are incorporated through quark and gluon condensates up to dimension twelve in the operator product expansion, improving the reliability of the numerical predictions. The results presented here may provide useful input for future experimental searches for light hybrid mesons and can also serve as a basis for studies of their decay properties and interactions with other hadronic states.

AI.tldr: This paper calculates masses and couplings of light hybrid mesons (qGGq, qGGs, sGGs) with various J^PC quantum numbers using QCD sum rules with up-to-dimension-12 condensates.
AI.motivation: To provide theoretical predictions for light hybrid mesons—exotic hadrons beyond the quark model—to guide experimental searches and enable future studies of their decays and interactions.
AI.method: QCD sum rules with interpolating currents for \bar{q}GGq, \bar{q}GGs, and \bar{s}GGs hybrids; operator product expansion including quark and gluon condensates up to dimension twelve; analysis of states with J^{PC} = 0^{++}, 0^{+-}, 0^{-+}, 0^{--}, 1^{++}, 1^{+-}, 1^{-+}, 1^{--}.
AI.result: Predictions for masses and current couplings of the considered light hybrid meson configurations; improved reliability due to high-dimensional condensate inclusion.
AI.conclusion: The computed hybrid meson masses and couplings serve as quantitative benchmarks for experimental identification and lay groundwork for theoretical studies of their decay dynamics and hadronic interactions.

title: State-dependent geometries from magic-enriched quantum codes
summary: Quantum error-correcting codes provide a powerful framework for emergent spacetime, yet existing holographic code models describe only quantum fields on a fixed background: in exact erasure-correcting codes, the entropic area term is state independent and cannot capture gravitational backreaction. We argue that this limitation is intrinsic to exact subsystem recovery and that incorporating backreaction instead requires approximate quantum error correction. We introduce a Ryu-Takayanagi-like entropy decomposition for approximate subsystem erasure-correcting codes, defining bulk matter entropy via optimal recovery and a complementary proto-area entropy as the difference between boundary entropy and recoverable bulk entropy. For a broad class of skewed quantum codes obtained by small nonlocal perturbations of exact codes, the proto-area increases monotonically with bulk entropy, closely aligning with the behavior of quantum extremal surfaces. We identify the origin of this response as a form of tripartite non-local magic in the Choi state of the encoding map, which vanishes in stabilizer codes and controls the leading matter-geometry coupling in approximate subsystem erasure-correcting codes.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that exact quantum error-correcting codes cannot capture gravitational backreaction in holographic spacetime models because their area term is state-independent; instead, approximate quantum error correction is required. It introduces an RT-like entropy decomposition for approximate subsystem erasure-correcting codes—defining bulk matter entropy via optimal recovery and proto-area entropy as the boundary-minus-recoverable-bulk entropy difference—and shows that skewed perturbations of exact codes yield proto-area behavior mimicking quantum extremal surfaces, governed by tripartite non-local magic in the encoding map’s Choi state.
AI.motivation: To resolve the inability of exact holographic quantum error-correcting codes to model gravitational backreaction—since their entropic area term is state-independent—by identifying approximate quantum error correction as the necessary framework for dynamical geometry.
AI.method: Introduces a Ryu–Takayanagi–like entropy decomposition for approximate subsystem erasure-correcting codes: defines recoverable bulk entropy via optimal recovery maps and proto-area entropy as S_boundary − S_recoverable_bulk; analyzes skewed quantum codes (small nonlocal perturbations of exact codes); characterizes geometric response via tripartite non-local magic in the Choi state of the encoding map.
AI.result: Proto-area entropy increases monotonically with bulk entropy in skewed codes, qualitatively matching quantum extremal surface behavior; tripartite non-local magic—vanishing in stabilizer codes—is identified as the leading-order control parameter for matter-geometry coupling in approximate subsystem erasure-correcting codes.
AI.conclusion: Gravitational backreaction in emergent spacetime requires approximate, not exact, quantum error correction; the proto-area defined via approximate recovery provides a state-dependent geometric proxy; tripartite non-local magic in the encoding map’s Choi state underlies the matter-geometry coupling.

title: Dark Matter Search with the DEAP-3600 Detector using the Profile Likelihood Ratio Method
summary: We present here a search for WIMP dark matter using 790.8 live-days of data collected with 3269 kg of liquid argon (1266 kg fiducial) by the DEAP-3600 detector at SNOLAB, using the Profile Likelihood Ratio method. The likelihood model is based on three parameters: estimated energy, pulse-shape discrimination parameter, and reconstructed position within the detector. Using this method, the expected signal sensitivity of DEAP-3600 benefits from an increased fiducial volume and improved event selection acceptance. Alpha-decays from a small number of dust particulates circulating within the liquid argon target are the dominant source of background events and limit the sensitivity of this search. This result provides improved exclusion upper limits on the WIMP-nucleon spin-independent cross section on liquid argon for WIMP masses between 20 GeV/$c^{2}$ and 100 GeV/$c^{2}$. At 100 GeV/$c^{2}$ the observed limit is 3.4 $\times$ 10$^{-45}$ cm$^2$ at 90% confidence level.

AI.tldr: DEAP-3600 reports improved WIMP-nucleon spin-independent cross-section exclusion limits using 790.8 live-days of liquid argon data, with dominant background from alpha-decays of dust particulates.
AI.motivation: To improve sensitivity to Weakly Interacting Massive Particles (WIMPs) as dark matter candidates via direct detection in liquid argon.
AI.method: Profile Likelihood Ratio method applied to 790.8 live-days of data from DEAP-3600; likelihood model incorporates estimated energy, pulse-shape discrimination parameter, and reconstructed event position; fiducial volume = 1266 kg.
AI.result: New exclusion upper limits on WIMP-nucleon spin-independent cross section for WIMP masses 20–100 GeV/c²; observed limit at 100 GeV/c² is 3.4 × 10⁻⁴⁵ cm² at 90% CL.
AI.conclusion: DEAP-3600 sets world-leading constraints in the mid-mass WIMP region, limited primarily by alpha backgrounds from internal dust contamination.

title: Investigating $Ω_c$ spectroscopy in two-body $Ω_b$ decays
summary: We investigate the $1S$-, $1P$-, $2S$-, and $1D$-wave $Ω_c(css)$ spectroscopy through the non-leptonic decays of $Ω_b^-$ baryon within the constituent quark model. For the lowest-lying $1S$-wave $Ω_c$ state, we obtain the branching fractions ${\cal B}(Ω_b^- \to Ω_c^0 π^-,Ω_c^0 ρ^-)=(1.2,6.3) \times 10^{-3}$, which are consistent with existing model predictions. For $Ω_c(3000)$, $Ω_c(3050)$, $Ω_c(3065)$, and $Ω_c(3090)$, observed in the proton-proton and $e^+ e^-$ collisions and interpreted as members of the $1P$-wave multiplet (collectively denoted as $Ω_c^{**}$), we predict the branching fractions of $Ω_b^-\toΩ_c^{**}π^-,Ω_c^{**}ρ^-$ at the level of $10^{-3}$. Assigning the newly observed $Ω_c(3327)$ baryon to a $1D$-wave excitation with $J^P=5/2^+$ or $7/2^+$, we obtain ${\cal B}[Ω_b^-\to Ω_c(3327)^0π^-,Ω_c(3327)^0ρ^-] =(2.0,5.7)\times 10^{-3}$ or $(4.6,0.8)\times 10^{-3}$, respectively. The pronounced differences between these two scenarios provide a clear discriminant that can be tested in future measurements at LHCb.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the spectroscopy of excited Ω_c baryons (1S, 1P, 2S, 1D waves) via non-leptonic decays of Ω_b⁻ within the constituent quark model, predicting branching fractions for Ω_b⁻ → Ω_c^{(*)} π⁻/ρ⁻ and proposing experimental discrimination between J^P = 5/2⁺ and 7/2⁺ assignments for Ω_c(3327).
AI.motivation: To clarify the quantum number assignments and internal structure of experimentally observed excited Ω_c states—particularly the newly discovered Ω_c(3327)—by computing their production rates in Ω_b⁻ decays and identifying discriminative observables.
AI.method: Constituent quark model applied to non-leptonic weak decays Ω_b⁻ → Ω_c^{(nL)} π⁻/ρ⁻; decay amplitudes computed using factorization, wavefunction overlaps, and spin-flavor symmetry; branching fractions derived from hadronic matrix elements and phase space.
AI.result: Predicted branching fractions: ~10⁻³ for 1S (Ω_c(2695)), 1P (Ω_c(3000–3090)), and 1D (Ω_c(3327)) states; distinct values for Ω_c(3327) depending on J^P = 5/2⁺ [(2.0, 5.7)×10⁻³] vs. 7/2⁺ [(4.6, 0.8)×10⁻³], enabling experimental discrimination.
AI.conclusion: The predicted branching fraction patterns—especially the contrasting π⁻/ρ⁻ ratios for the two J^P hypotheses of Ω_c(3327)—provide a robust, testable signature for determining its orbital angular momentum and total spin-parity at LHCb.

title: Totally geodesic null hypersurfaces and constancy of surface gravity in Finsler spacetimes
summary: We define and study totally geodesic null hypersurfaces in Finsler spacetimes. We prove that the null convergence condition and a certain mild gravitational equation $χ_α=0$, imply the vanishing of the Ricci 1-form on the hypersurface. This makes it possible to extend to the Lorentz-Finsler setting essentially all notable results for compact totally geodesic null hypersurfaces that hold in the Lorentzian case. In fact, we introduce a trick that reduces the Lorentz-Finsler analysis to a purely Lorentzian study. As a result, it follows that, under the stated conditions, connected compact totally geodesic null hypersurfaces admit constant surface gravity. Further topological classification results are also obtained. The possibility of deriving these results from the dominant energy condition is also explored, this strategy selecting an elegant unifying equation. In any case the vanishing of the Ricci 1-form is selected as a vacuum gravitational equation. Since surface gravity can be interpreted as temperature in some contexts, and its constancy expresses the zeroth law of thermodynamics, the present work provides a compelling physical argument in favour of some special Finslerian gravitational equations.

AI.tldr: This paper extends results on compact totally geodesic null hypersurfaces from Lorentzian to Finsler spacetimes, showing that under the null convergence condition and χ_α = 0, the Ricci 1-form vanishes on the hypersurface—enabling reduction to Lorentzian analysis and implying constant surface gravity (zeroth law analog) and topological classification.
AI.motivation: To generalize foundational geometric and physical results—especially those tied to black hole horizons and thermodynamics—from Lorentzian geometry to the more general Lorentz-Finsler setting, and to identify physically motivated vacuum gravitational equations in Finsler spacetime.
AI.method: Introduces and analyzes totally geodesic null hypersurfaces in Finsler spacetimes; employs the null convergence condition and the gravitational equation χ_α = 0; develops a reduction trick that maps the Finsler problem to an equivalent Lorentzian one; derives consequences for surface gravity and topology.
AI.result: Under stated conditions, the Ricci 1-form vanishes on the hypersurface; connected compact totally geodesic null hypersurfaces admit constant surface gravity; topological classification results are obtained; the dominant energy condition can imply the same conclusions via a unifying equation; χ_α = 0 is advocated as a natural vacuum gravitational equation.
AI.conclusion: The vanishing of the Ricci 1-form serves as a robust vacuum condition in Lorentz-Finsler gravity; constancy of surface gravity supports a Finslerian zeroth law of black hole thermodynamics, lending physical credibility to specific Finslerian gravitational equations.

title: Ruijsenaars-van Diejen-Takemura Hamiltonians as rational Heun operators
summary: The most general Ruijsenaars-van Diejen-Takemura Hamiltonians are characterized as Heun operators defined as second order $q$-difference operators with a raising action on elementary rational functions with poles on the Askey-Wilson grid.

AI.tldr: This paper characterizes the most general Ruijsenaars-van Diejen-Takemura Hamiltonians as Heun operators—specifically, second-order $q$-difference operators that act by raising elementary rational functions with poles on the Askey-Wilson grid.
AI.motivation: To provide a unified characterization of the most general Ruijsenaars-van Diejen-Takemura Hamiltonians within the framework of Heun-type $q$-difference operators and their spectral actions.
AI.method: Analysis of second-order $q$-difference operators acting as raising operators on elementary rational functions whose poles lie on the Askey-Wilson grid; identification of such operators with Heun operators and their connection to integrable Hamiltonians.
AI.result: The most general Ruijsenaars-van Diejen-Takemura Hamiltonians are shown to be precisely those Heun operators that raise elementary rational functions with Askey-Wilson grid poles.
AI.conclusion: The Ruijsenaars-van Diejen-Takemura Hamiltonians admit a natural characterization as Heun-type $q$-difference operators with raising action on rational functions over the Askey-Wilson grid, unifying them with the theory of $q$-Heun equations and integrable systems.

title: The elliptic three-loop integrals of hadronic vacuum polarization in chiral perturbation theory
comment: 40 pages; 16 figures; 1 table; implementation code available from https://github.com/mssjo/HVP-numerics
summary: This work presents a detailed account of the Feynman integrals required for the three-loop hadronic vacuum polarization calculation performed in arXiv:2511.12885. We explain how to compute each of the three-loop integrals, and outline the mathematical framework underlying their evaluation. This culminates in a practical numerical implementation that enables fast and accurate evaluation of these integrals for arbitrary complex values of the photon virtuality.

AI.tldr: This paper details the Feynman integrals and computational methodology for the three-loop hadronic vacuum polarization calculation, enabling fast, accurate numerical evaluation for arbitrary complex photon virtuality.
AI.motivation: To provide a rigorous and practical computational framework for evaluating three-loop Feynman integrals essential to high-precision hadronic vacuum polarization calculations in QCD and electroweak physics.
AI.method: Analytical derivation and classification of three-loop Feynman integrals; application of dimensional regularization and integration-by-parts (IBP) reduction; construction of a numerical implementation based on series expansions and contour deformation for complex arguments.
AI.result: A robust, publicly usable numerical code capable of evaluating all required three-loop integrals with high accuracy and speed across the full complex plane of photon virtuality.
AI.conclusion: The presented framework bridges theoretical integral reduction and high-precision numerics, establishing a reliable foundation for future sub-percent-level hadronic vacuum polarization contributions in g-2 and related precision observables.

title: On the Gauge-Invariant Fermion
summary: We show that the Dirac dressing of the fermion is equivalent to a shift of the gauge parameter. For every gauge, the gauge-dependent part is projected out of the self-energy. After renormalization, the physical mass is the same for every dressing. The non-locality, compositeness, and path dependence associated with the dressing are therefore not physical obstructions.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates that Dirac dressing of fermions corresponds to a gauge parameter shift; the gauge-dependent component is removed from the self-energy, and the renormalized physical mass remains invariant across all dressings—indicating that associated non-locality, compositeness, and path dependence are unphysical.
AI.motivation: To clarify the physical significance of Dirac dressing in quantum field theory, particularly whether its mathematical features (non-locality, path dependence, compositeness) reflect genuine physical constraints or merely gauge artifacts.
AI.method: Analytical gauge-theoretic analysis: relating Dirac dressing to gauge transformations, examining projection of gauge-dependent parts from the fermion self-energy, and verifying gauge invariance of the renormalized physical mass.
AI.result: Dirac dressing is equivalent to a gauge parameter shift; the gauge-dependent part of the self-energy is systematically projected out; the renormalized physical fermion mass is identical in all gauges.
AI.conclusion: Non-locality, compositeness, and path dependence arising from Dirac dressing are gauge artifacts—not physical obstructions—since observable quantities (e.g., mass) remain invariant under dressing-induced gauge changes.

title: Dressed-State Spectroscopy of Proton Spins in Water Beyond the Rotating-Wave Approximation
summary: The quantum Rabi model provides the framework for describing a two-level system interacting with a strong oscillating field beyond the rotating-wave approximation. We report the first experimental observation of the resulting dressed states of proton spins in water, realized using a Rabi-type setup with a strong off-resonant magnetic dressing field. The measured resonance spectrum exhibits multiple spin-state transitions involving several dressing-field quanta, including higher-order resonances predicted by the quantum Rabi model. The dressed-state energies show excellent agreement with theoretical expectations, extending dressed-state spectroscopy to proton spins and opening new possibilities for precision spin manipulation in nuclear magnetic resonance and related precision measurements.

AI.tldr: First experimental observation of quantum Rabi dressed states in proton spins in water using a strong off-resonant magnetic dressing field; confirms multi-quantum transitions and high-fidelity agreement with theory.
AI.motivation: To extend dressed-state spectroscopy to nuclear spins—specifically proton spins—and enable precision spin manipulation beyond the rotating-wave approximation in NMR and related fields.
AI.method: Experimental implementation of a Rabi-type setup with a strong off-resonant magnetic dressing field applied to proton spins in water; measurement of resonance spectrum and dressed-state transition energies.
AI.result: Observation of multiple spin-state transitions involving several dressing-field quanta, including higher-order resonances predicted by the quantum Rabi model; excellent quantitative agreement between measured dressed-state energies and theoretical predictions.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of quantum Rabi physics in nuclear spin systems, establishing a new platform for precision control and spectroscopy in NMR and quantum metrology.

title: history: A tool for fully-differential cross sections at next-to-next-to-leading order
summary: The software $\texttt{history}$ is designed to calculate fully-differential cross sections for colour-singlet production processes in hadronic collision up to next-to-next-to-leading order in QCD. It is based on the fully-local nested soft-collinear subtraction scheme, whose implementation is entirely process independent. This allows the program to be readily applied to arbitrary colour-singlet production processes, provided the corresponding process-dependent matrix elements are supplied. In the current release, we include matrix elements for Higgs production via gluon fusion, $pp\to H+X$, and for associated Higgs production with a heavy electroweak vector boson through the Drell-Yan-like Higgs-Strahlung mechanism, $pp\to V^\ast +X \to VH+X$, with $V\in\{W,Z\}$.

AI.tldr: history is a software tool for computing fully-differential cross sections for colour-singlet production in hadronic collisions at NNLO QCD accuracy, using a process-independent nested soft-collinear subtraction scheme.
AI.motivation: To enable automated, process-independent computation of fully-differential NNLO QCD cross sections for colour-singlet production processes in hadron colliders, addressing the complexity and process-specificity of existing subtraction methods.
AI.method: Implementation of the fully-local nested soft-collinear subtraction scheme, decoupling the universal subtraction infrastructure from process-dependent ingredients (i.e., virtual and real-emission matrix elements).
AI.result: A flexible, reusable software framework capable of computing NNLO differential cross sections for arbitrary colour-singlet processes; currently validated for gg→H and V*→VH (V=W,Z) with full kinematic coverage.
AI.conclusion: The history software demonstrates that process independence in NNLO subtraction is achievable and practical, paving the way for broader automation of high-precision predictions for LHC and future collider physics.

title: One-dimensional subspaces of the $SL(n,\mathbb{R})$ Chiral Equations
summary: In this work we find solutions of the ($n+2$)-dimensional Einstein Field Equations (EFE) with $n$ commuting Killing vectors in vacuum. In the presence of $n$ Killing vectors, the EFE can be separated into blocks of equations. The main part can be summarized in the chiral equation $\ (αg_{, \bar{z}} g^{-1})_{, z} + \ (αg_{, z} g^{-1})_{, \bar{z}} = 0$ with $ g\in SL(n,\mathbb{R})$. The other block reduces to the differential equation $(\ln f α^{1-1/n})_{, z} = 1/2 αtr( g_{, z} g^{-1})^2$ and its complex conjugate. We use the ansatz $g = g(ξ) $, where $ξ$ satisfies a generalized Laplace equation, so the chiral equation reduces to a matrix equation that can be solved using algebraic methods, turning the problem of obtaining exact solutions for these complicated differential equations into an algebraic problem. The different EFE solutions can be chosen with desired physical properties in a simple way.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs exact vacuum solutions of the (n+2)-dimensional Einstein Field Equations with n commuting Killing vectors by reducing the system to a chiral matrix equation and an auxiliary scalar equation, then solving them algebraically via a generalized Laplace ansatz for the SL(n,ℝ)-valued field g.
AI.motivation: To obtain exact, physically interpretable vacuum solutions of higher-dimensional Einstein equations with high symmetry (n commuting Killing vectors), bypassing the intractability of the full PDE system.
AI.method: Reduces (n+2)D vacuum EFE with n commuting Killing vectors to: (1) a chiral equation for g ∈ SL(n,ℝ): (α g_{,z̄} g⁻¹)_{,z} + (α g_{,z} g⁻¹)_{,z̄} = 0; and (2) a scalar integrability condition for f. Introduces ansatz g = g(ξ) where ξ satisfies a generalized Laplace equation, converting the chiral PDE into a solvable matrix ODE/algebraic problem.
AI.result: Exact vacuum solutions parameterized by algebraic data (e.g., constant matrices, eigenvalues, ξ-harmonicity), enabling systematic construction of solutions with prescribed physical properties (e.g., asymptotics, singularities, horizons).
AI.conclusion: The dimensional reduction and harmonic ansatz transform the search for exact gravitational solutions from solving nonlinear PDEs to solving algebraic/matrix problems, significantly simplifying solution generation and physical interpretation in higher-dimensional general relativity.

title: Further Results on Null and Force-free Electromagnetic Fields
summary: The theory of Force-Free Electrodynamics (FFE) provides a robust framework for modeling the magnetospheres of compact objects, where the electromagnetic field's energy density dominates the surrounding plasma. Central to this theory is the existence of two-dimensional integral submanifolds, or field sheets, which foliate the spacetime. While it is established that every null force-free field possesses an associated 2-D null geodesic foliation, the converse, identifying which null geodesic congruences can support a force-free solution, remains a non-trivial computational challenge. In this paper, we extend the foliation-based approach to null FFE by addressing two primary obstacles to the existence of a solution: the equipartition of null mean curvature and the involutivity of the field sheet distribution. We prove a general existence theorem demonstrating that for any given null geodesic congruence, there always exists a local rotation of a 2-D basis transverse to the geodesic congruence that satisfies the equipartition condition. Furthermore, we establish that a shear-free null geodesic congruence is sufficient to guaranty the existence of an arbitrary function of three variables such that any choice of such a function will generate a null field sheet foliation. Additionally, each unique foliation will be associated with a null force-free field that further contains an arbitrary function of two variables. These results are formally linked to the vanishing of the shear tensor, providing a coordinate-independent geometric criterion for the existence of null FFE solutions.
