We illustrate these theorems with explicit examples in Schwarzschild and Kerr geometries and present new, non-trivial exact null solutions in flat spacetime and for the C-metric.

AI.tldr: This paper advances the theory of null Force-Free Electrodynamics (FFE) by establishing geometric conditions—specifically shear-freeness and equipartition of null mean curvature—for the existence of null field sheet foliations and associated force-free fields; it proves general local existence theorems and constructs new exact null FFE solutions in Schwarzschild, Kerr, flat, and C-metric spacetimes.
AI.motivation: To resolve the converse problem in null FFE: determining which null geodesic congruences admit a compatible force-free electromagnetic field, overcoming computational and geometric obstructions to solution existence.
AI.method: Geometric analysis of null geodesic congruences; derivation of necessary and sufficient conditions for involutivity and equipartition of null mean curvature; construction of transverse 2-D frame rotations; application of Frobenius theorem and shear tensor analysis; explicit integration in specific spacetime metrics.
AI.result: 1) Local existence of a transverse frame satisfying equipartition for any null geodesic congruence; 2) Shear-free congruences guarantee existence of a 3-variable function generating a null field sheet foliation; 3) Each such foliation yields a null FFE solution with an arbitrary 2-variable function; 4) Vanishing shear is a coordinate-independent existence criterion; 5) New explicit null FFE solutions in Schwarzschild, Kerr, Minkowski, and C-metric spacetimes.
AI.conclusion: Shear-freeness is the fundamental geometric condition ensuring existence of null FFE solutions via field sheet foliations; the work unifies and extends the foliation-based approach, providing both rigorous existence theorems and concrete exact solutions across key spacetime geometries.

title: A Quantum Weak Cosmic Censorship and Its Proof
comment: 8 pages, no figures. Posted on the occasion of Albert Einstein's birthday
summary: Recent work has highlighted the deep connection between quantum information and spacetime geometry. Bousso and Shahbazi-Moghaddam (Phys. Rev. Lett. 128, 231301 (2022)) proved that ``hyperentropic'' regions -- where entropy exceeds the area bound -- inevitably lead to singularity formation. In this work, we explore the converse implication: does the thermodynamic consistency of such singularities require them to be hidden? We answer in the affirmative, establishing a Quantum Weak Cosmic Censorship principle governed by Generalized Entropy. This provides a semiclassical mechanism for censorship which forbids naked singularities. Since Quantum Weak Cosmic Censorship is a semiclassical statement, it is more robust than the classical Weak Cosmic Censorship showing naked singularities are forbidden in nature even if quantum effects are taken into account.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a Quantum Weak Cosmic Censorship principle based on Generalized Entropy, showing that thermodynamic consistency of singularities requires them to be hidden—even semiclassically—thereby forbidding naked singularities more robustly than classical cosmic censorship.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether thermodynamic consistency of singularities formed in 'hyperentropic' regions necessitates their being hidden, i.e., whether quantum/semiclassical considerations enforce cosmic censorship.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis building on the hyperentropic singularity theorem of Bousso & Shahbazi-Moghaddam, combined with generalized entropy (including quantum corrections) and semiclassical gravitational thermodynamics.
AI.result: Proof of a Quantum Weak Cosmic Censorship principle: singularities arising from hyperentropic conditions must be causally hidden (i.e., no naked singularities), enforced by monotonicity and consistency of generalized entropy.
AI.conclusion: Quantum Weak Cosmic Censorship is a robust semiclassical mechanism ensuring that naked singularities are forbidden—not just classically but also when quantum effects (via generalized entropy) are included.

title: Reinterpretation of searches for supersymmetry in models with variable R-parity-violating coupling strength using the full ATLAS Run 2 Dataset
comment: 50 pages in total, author list starting page 36, 14 figures, 3 tables, submitted to Journal of High Energy Physics. All figures including auxiliary figures are available at https://atlas.web.cern.ch/Atlas/GROUPS/PHYSICS/PAPERS/HMBS-2024-04
summary: A collection of thirteen ATLAS searches for supersymmetry (SUSY) models, optimized for R-parity-conserving (RPC) and R-parity-violating (RPV) SUSY, are reinterpreted in SUSY models with variable RPV coupling strength, which determines whether the lightest supersymmetric particle decays promptly or is long-lived. The dataset corresponds to an integrated luminosity of 140 fb$^{-1}$ of proton-proton collisions at a centre-of-mass energy of $\sqrt{s}=13$ TeV collected between 2015 and 2018 by the ATLAS detector at the Large Hadron Collider. Limits are set at 95% confidence level on the mass of pair-produced gluinos decaying to final states enhanced or depleted with top quarks, and on the masses of pair-produced top squarks, tau-sleptons, or charginos and neutralinos. In a model of pair-produced gluinos decaying to final states enhanced with top quarks, a lower limit of 1.8 TeV on the gluino mass is set regardless of the RPV coupling value. In the gluino model with decays to first and second generation quarks, gluino masses are excluded up to 1.6-2.2 (1.6-2.5) TeV for different values of the RPV coupling $λ^{'}$ ($λ^{''}$). Top-squark masses up to 2.4 TeV are excluded at high values of $λ^{''}$, compared to 1.0-1.7 TeV for low and intermediate $λ^{''}$. Tau-slepton masses between 180 GeV and 340 GeV are excluded for $λ$ couplings smaller than $10^{-4}$. Higgsino masses up to 800 GeV-1.0 TeV are excluded when $λ_{i33}$ is larger than $4\times10^{-5}$. This work extends the analyses beyond RPC scenarios to a broad class of RPV frameworks and achieves significantly improved sensitivity to a diverse range of long-lived particle signatures.

AI.tldr: This paper reinterprets 13 ATLAS SUSY searches to set 95% CL limits on gluino, top-squark, tau-slepton, and higgsino masses across a continuum of RPV coupling strengths — covering both prompt and long-lived decays — using 140 fb⁻¹ of 13 TeV pp collision data.
AI.motivation: To extend supersymmetry searches beyond traditional R-parity-conserving (RPC) assumptions and systematically probe R-parity-violating (RPV) models with variable coupling strength, enabling sensitivity to both prompt and long-lived particle signatures.
AI.method: Reinterpretation of thirteen existing ATLAS RPC and RPV-optimized SUSY searches using a unified framework that parametrizes RPV coupling strength (λ′, λ″, λᵢ₃₃), mapping signal yields and efficiencies across decay lifetimes; statistical combination and limit setting at 95% CL using the full Run 2 dataset (140 fb⁻¹, √s = 13 TeV).
AI.result: Gluino mass limits reach 1.8 TeV (top-enhanced) and up to 2.5 TeV (1st/2nd-gen quark final states); top-squark exclusion extends to 2.4 TeV at large λ″; tau-sleptons excluded for 180–340 GeV when λ < 10⁻⁴; higgsinos excluded up to 1.0 TeV for λᵢ₃₃ > 4×10⁻⁵; all limits are coupling-dependent and cover prompt-to-long-lived regimes.
AI.conclusion: The reinterpretation significantly broadens ATLAS SUSY coverage, achieving enhanced sensitivity to diverse RPV scenarios—including long-lived particles—thereby constraining a wider range of BSM physics than RPC-only analyses.

title: Prospects for probing light photophobic axion-like particles via displaced vertex signals at the CEPC
summary: In recent years, long-lived particles (LLPs) have attracted increasing attention in searches for physics beyond the Standard Model (SM). In this paper, we investigate the discovery prospects for light long-lived ALPs predicted by the photophobic ALP scenario via displaced vertex signals at the CEPC with the center-of-mass energy $\sqrt{s}=91.2 $ GeV and integrated luminosity $\mathcal{L}=$ $100$ ab$^{-1}$. After comparing several possible single-production processes of the photophobic ALP, we focus on the dominant process $e^+ e^- \to Z \to a γ$, with the ALP $a$ subsequently decaying into a pair of displaced charged leptons. Dedicated Monte Carlo simulations are performed for the $μ^+ μ^- γ$ and $τ_h^+ τ_h^- E\mkern-10.5 mu/_T γ$ signals. For the $μ^+μ^-γ$ signal, the CEPC is sensitive to the parameter region $g_{aWW} \in [1.27\times10^{-3},6.80\times10^{-1}]~\mathrm{TeV}^{-1}$ for $m_a \in [1,4]~\mathrm{GeV}$. For the $τ_h^+τ_h^-E\mkern-10.5 mu/_Tγ$ signal, the accessible region is $g_{aWW} \in [7.00\times10^{-4},9.40\times10^{-3}]~\mathrm{TeV}^{-1}$ with $m_a \in [4,9]~\mathrm{GeV}$. These results demonstrate the strong potential of the CEPC to explore light long-lived ALPs via displaced vertex signals, providing complementary coverage to existing searches at the LEP and LHC, as well as to the projected reach of the HL-LHC.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the discovery potential of light, long-lived axion-like particles (ALPs) in the photophobic ALP scenario at the Circular Electron Positron Collider (CEPC), using displaced vertex signatures from $e^+e^- \to Z \to a\gamma$, with $a \to \ell^+\ell^-$, and reports sensitivity ranges for the ALP–WW coupling $g_{aWW}$ across ALP masses 1–9 GeV.
AI.motivation: Long-lived particles (LLPs), especially light long-lived ALPs in the photophobic scenario, are compelling probes of physics beyond the Standard Model; existing constraints from LEP and LHC leave gaps, motivating dedicated LLP searches at the high-luminosity, low-background CEPC.
AI.method: Analyzes single ALP production via $e^+e^- \to Z \to a\gamma$ at CEPC ($\sqrt{s}=91.2\,\text{GeV}, \mathcal{L}=100\,\text{ab}^{-1}$); performs dedicated full-detector Monte Carlo simulations for displaced $\mu^+\mu^-\gamma$ and hadronically decaying $\tau^+\tau^-E_T^{\text{miss}}\gamma$ final states; employs displaced vertex reconstruction and background suppression strategies optimized for CEPC's precision tracking.
AI.result: CEPC reaches $g_{aWW} \in [1.27\times10^{-3}, 6.80\times10^{-1}]\,\text{TeV}^{-1}$ for $m_a \in [1,4]\,\text{GeV}$ (dimuon channel) and $g_{aWW} \in [7.00\times10^{-4}, 9.40\times10^{-3}]\,\text{TeV}^{-1}$ for $m_a \in [4,9]\,\text{GeV}$ (ditau + $E_T^{\text{miss}}$ channel), significantly extending coverage beyond LEP/LHC.
AI.conclusion: CEPC offers unique, complementary sensitivity to light photophobic ALPs via displaced vertices, establishing it as a world-leading facility for LLP searches in the sub-10 GeV mass range.

title: Analytical derivation of long-term dephasing caused by phase transitions in the context of Kerr black holes
summary: Extreme Mass Ratio Inspirals (EMRIs) constitute a prime target for future space-based gravitational-wave observatories such as LISA. In this paper, we analytically investigate the long-term phase shift (dephasing) in the gravitational wave signal induced by a first-order quantum chromodynamics (QCD) phase transition within a neutron star orbiting a supermassive Kerr black hole. By modeling the transition from a hadronic phase to a quark core phase, we quantify the sudden change in the tidal deformability ($Λ$) of the secondary object. Utilizing the Teukolsky formalism and Post-Newtonian expansions, we derive a strict analytical scaling law for the accumulated dephasing. We demonstrate that the Kerr spin parameter $a$ and the critical phase transition orbital velocity $v_c$ significantly amplify the dephasing effect. Our analytical framework provides a robust tool for probing the non-perturbative QCD equation of state at high baryon densities using gravitational wave astronomy.

AI.tldr: This paper analytically studies how a QCD phase transition inside a neutron star—triggered during an EMRI with a supermassive Kerr black hole—affects the gravitational-wave phase evolution, deriving a scaling law for dephasing sensitive to black hole spin and transition velocity.
AI.motivation: To probe the high-density QCD equation of state via gravitational-wave dephasing induced by a sudden change in neutron star tidal deformability during a hadron-to-quark phase transition in EMRIs.
AI.method: Analytical modeling of the QCD phase transition-induced jump in tidal deformability Λ; combined use of Teukolsky formalism for wave generation in Kerr spacetime and Post-Newtonian expansions for orbital dynamics; derivation of a closed-form scaling law for accumulated dephasing.
AI.result: A strict analytical scaling law for gravitational-wave dephasing is obtained, showing strong amplification by the Kerr spin parameter a and critical orbital velocity v_c at which the phase transition occurs.
AI.conclusion: The derived dephasing signature is detectable by LISA and provides a novel, model-independent probe of non-perturbative QCD physics at supranuclear densities through EMRI observations.

title: Spectral Geometry and the One-Loop QED $β$-Function on $S^3 \times S^1$
comment: 13 pages; accepted for publication in Int. J. Geom. Methods Mod. Phys.; DOI: 10.1142/S0219887826501690; arXiv appeal MOD-70631 approved
summary: We compute the one-loop QED $β$-function coefficient directly from heat kernel data of the twisted Spin$^c$ Dirac operator on $S^3 \times S^1$. Using $ζ$-function regularization, the logarithmic scale dependence is encoded in the $a_4$ coefficient of the spectral expansion. The $F_{μν} F^{μν}$ term in $a_4$ yields exactly $β(e) = e^3/(12π^2)$, independent of $r$, $L$, or background, verifying spectral RG flow without flat-space propagators. The result is independent of the radii of $S^3$ and $S^1$ and of the choice of gauge background, providing a parameter-free consistency check that spectral data on compact manifolds encode renormalization group information. Beyond a mere verification of the coupling flow, this result serves as a non-trivial consistency check of the Spectral Action Principle in a curved background. It demonstrates that universal quantum corrections can be extracted purely from geometric spectral invariants, distinguishing this geometric spectral derivation from momentum-space propagator methods.

AI.tldr: This paper computes the one-loop QED β-function coefficient directly from the heat kernel coefficient $a_4$ of the twisted Spin^c Dirac operator on $S^3 \times S^1$, using ζ-function regularization; it recovers the standard result $\beta(e) = e^3/(12\pi^2)$ purely geometrically, confirming spectral RG flow and validating the Spectral Action Principle in curved spacetime.
AI.motivation: To verify that renormalization group flow — specifically the QED β-function — can be derived solely from spectral geometric data (heat kernel coefficients) on compact manifolds, without recourse to flat-space propagators or momentum-space techniques, thereby testing the Spectral Action Principle in a nontrivial curved background.
AI.method: Computes the one-loop QED β-function via ζ-function regularization of the functional determinant of the twisted Spin^c Dirac operator on $S^3 \times S^1$; extracts logarithmic UV divergence from the $a_4$ Seeley–DeWitt coefficient of its heat kernel expansion; isolates the $F_{\mu\nu}F^{\mu\nu}$ contribution to obtain $\beta(e)$.
AI.result: Recovers the exact one-loop QED β-function $\beta(e) = e^3/(12\pi^2)$, independent of the radii $r$, $L$ of $S^3$ and $S^1$, and independent of the gauge background; confirms universality and geometric origin of quantum corrections.
AI.conclusion: Spectral data on compact curved manifolds fully encode RG flow information; this provides a parameter-free, background-independent validation of the Spectral Action Principle and establishes geometric spectral methods as a viable, propagator-free alternative to conventional QFT renormalization.

title: A Simulation-Based Inference Evaluation of Tension Between MicroBooNE and MiniBooNE Results in a 3+1 Sterile Neutrino Global Fit
summary:
Compatibility between different datasets in a global fit is essential for determining whether a chosen model adequately describes the data. In a 3+1 sterile neutrino global fit, long-standing tensions between datasets sensitive to $ν_e$ appearance and $ν_e/ν_μ$ disappearance indicate a failure of the model to explain the observed data, despite an overall $> 5σ$ improvement over the $3ν$ Standard Model (SM) based on a $χ^2$ fit. Overall, a global preference for the 3+1 sterile-neutrino hypothesis with significant tension between experiments motivates consideration of more complex models, but these are currently computationally prohibitive to evaluate. This paper is the third in a series aimed at reducing computational cost by developing a Simulation-Based Inference (SBI) framework for global fits. Previous papers focused on rapidly fitting the data sets using frequentist (Feldman-Cousins) and Bayesian approaches, while in this work, we formalize a definition of tension within the SBI framework.
  As an example, we perform a full 3+1 fit to the charged-current quasi-elastic neutrino data from the MiniBooNE experiment and the inclusive neutrino data from the MicroBooNE experiment, located on the same beamline. Using experiment-supplied systematics as is, we find these data sets favor 3+1 at $3.6σ$ and $1.8σ$ respectively, while the tension between the two is $3.3σ$, when fit with the SBI procedure. After correcting for normalization differences between data and Monte Carlo in the MicroBooNE $ν_μ$ samples, the tension relaxes to $2.2σ$, indicating reduced but non-negligible disagreement. The observed tension may reflect both limitations of the 3+1 model in describing the datasets and the presence of systematic effects that impact the experiments differently.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a Simulation-Based Inference (SBI) framework to quantify statistical tension between neutrino experiments in 3+1 sterile neutrino global fits, applying it to MiniBooNE and MicroBooNE data—finding 3.3σ tension that reduces to 2.2σ after correcting MicroBooNE νμ normalization.
AI.motivation: To address computational prohibitions in evaluating complex neutrino models and to formalize a robust, SBI-based definition of dataset tension in global fits where the 3+1 model shows significant but inconsistent evidence across experiments.
AI.method: Develops and applies a Simulation-Based Inference (SBI) framework—extending prior frequentist and Bayesian SBI work—to compute significance of model preference and quantify tension via likelihood-ratio-derived p-values; applied to MiniBooNE CCQE and MicroBooNE inclusive neutrino data with experiment-supplied systematics, including a normalization correction study for MicroBooNE νμ Monte Carlo.
AI.result: MiniBooNE and MicroBooNE datasets individually prefer the 3+1 model at 3.6σ and 1.8σ; their mutual tension is quantified as 3.3σ under nominal systematics, decreasing to 2.2σ after correcting MicroBooNE νμ MC normalization—indicating residual model or systematic disagreement.
AI.conclusion: The SBI framework enables efficient, principled tension quantification in global neutrino fits; the observed tension suggests both limitations of the 3+1 model and experiment-specific systematic effects, motivating refined modeling and calibration rather than immediate adoption of more complex (e.g., 3+2) models.

title: New nonet scalar mesons and glueballs: the mass spectra and the production yields in relativistic heavy ion collisions
summary: We propose a new nonet scheme for scalar mesons consisting of $f_{0}(980)$, $a_{0}(980)$, $K_{0}^{\ast}(1430)$, and $f_{0}(1770)$, regarding them as quark-antiquark $P$-wave states classified by $\mathrm{SU}(3)$ light flavor symmetry. We investigate their production in relativistic heavy ion collisions, and estimate their yields by applying the statistical model and the quark coalescence model. In contrast to these scalar mesons, we regard $f_{0}(1500)$ as a glueball that is not included in the proposed nonet. We quantify the production yields of $f_{0}(1500)$ by accounting for various internal structures of this state, and compare their yields with those of the new nonet scalar mesons. Given the production yields of these hadrons, our results strongly suggests that $f_{0}(1500)$ is a glueball.

AI.tldr: Proposes a new scalar meson nonet (f0(980), a0(980), K0*(1430), f0(1770)) as P-wave qq̄ states under SU(3) flavor symmetry; treats f0(1500) as a glueball; uses statistical and coalescence models in heavy-ion collisions to show f0(1500) yield is inconsistent with qq̄ but consistent with glueball interpretation.
AI.motivation: To resolve the long-standing scalar meson spectroscopy puzzle by assigning a consistent quark-antiquark nonet and identifying f0(1500) as a glueball candidate via production dynamics in heavy-ion collisions.
AI.method: Constructs an SU(3)-symmetric P-wave qq̄ nonet; computes hadronic yields using the statistical hadronization model and quark coalescence model in relativistic heavy-ion collisions; quantifies f0(1500) production assuming mixed glueball–qq̄–tetraquark configurations for comparison.
AI.result: Predicted yields of the proposed nonet members are consistent with qq̄ production mechanisms; f0(1500) yield is significantly suppressed relative to nonet expectations and best reproduced assuming dominant glueball content.
AI.conclusion: The anomalous production pattern of f0(1500) — distinct from the proposed qq̄ nonet — provides strong evidence that it is predominantly a scalar glueball.

title: Multi-Field Dilaton Screening Beyond the Thin-Shell Mechanism
summary: We analyse screening in multi-field scalar-tensor theories, focusing on systems with a dilaton coupled to matter and an axion with a dilaton-dependent kinetic term, in the presence of both planetary and stellar density profiles. Using analytic arguments and fully coupled numerical solutions, we identify a regime in which full screening for a dark-energy-light, effectively unpinned string-dilaton, can occur without fine-tuning. The backreaction of the dilaton's partnered axion field can suppress the exterior scalar charge by selecting a minimum-energy configuration (the BBQ mechanism), yielding robust screening for generic axion gradients. In this regime screening is achieved by cancelling the dilaton's gradient rather than localising it. This reduces the exterior scalar charge and allows for gravity tests in the solar system to be passed. We then show that the more familiar thin-shell intuition need not apply in the multi-field setting. Axion surface gradients can drastically reshape the dilaton profile and drive a more localised transition without generically suppressing the fifth force. The exterior charge can remain essentially unchanged or even be enhanced as the shell is made thinner by a kinetically coupled field. Multi-field two-derivative dynamics therefore decouple localisation in thin shells from screening, evade single-field no-go arguments, and reopen viable parameter space for cosmologically light dilaton-like scalars with strong couplings to matter.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates screening mechanisms in multi-field scalar-tensor theories—specifically dilaton-axion systems—and identifies a novel BBQ mechanism where axion backreaction suppresses the dilaton's exterior scalar charge by cancelling its gradient, enabling robust screening without fine-tuning; it challenges thin-shell intuition by showing that axion-induced localisation does not necessarily suppress fifth forces, thereby evading single-field no-go theorems and expanding viable parameter space for light, strongly coupled dilatons.
AI.motivation: To reconcile cosmologically light, strongly matter-coupled dilaton-like scalars with stringent solar-system gravity tests by identifying viable screening mechanisms beyond single-field paradigms.
AI.method: Analytic reasoning combined with fully coupled numerical solutions of field equations for dilaton-axion systems under planetary and stellar density profiles, focusing on kinetic coupling and gradient-driven backreaction (BBQ mechanism).
AI.result: Discovery of the BBQ mechanism: axion backreaction cancels the dilaton’s gradient—reducing exterior scalar charge—enabling full screening without fine-tuning; demonstration that thin-shell localisation and fifth-force suppression are decoupled in multi-field setups, allowing enhanced or unchanged exterior charge despite thinner shells.
AI.conclusion: Multi-field two-derivative dynamics invalidate single-field screening intuitions and no-go arguments, reopening phenomenologically viable parameter space for light, strongly coupled scalar fields in gravity and dark energy models.

title: Isomorphism between the local Poincare generalized translations group and the group of spacetime transformations (x LB1)4
comment: This submission corresponds to the published version. 20 pages
summary: We will prove that there is a direct relationship between the Poincare subgroup of translations, and the group of tetrad transformations LB1 introduced in a previous manuscript. LB1 is the group composed by SO(1; 1) plus two kinds of discrete transformations. Translations have been extensively studied under the scope of gauge theories. By using the geometric structures built to prove this elementary result we will generalize it to the case of what we might call local translations. A special case of the latter is the Bondi-Metzner-Sachs subgroup of supertranslations. In order to accomplish this goal and since the group of translations is four-dimensional we will prove first that it is isomorphic to (x LB1)4. In order to prove this claim we will introduce a system of differential equations involving several kinds of fields. Abelian, non-Abelian, spinor, gravitational. These fields will constitute the structure needed to build local tetrads of a new kind that allow for the proof to be carried out with simplicity. Results already obtained involving similar but not equal tetrads will be useful in our constructions and demonstrations. Translations and generalized translations isomorphic to tensor products of LB1 groups are not trivial results. Because the LB1 group is composed by SO(1; 1) and two discrete transformations.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a direct isomorphism between the Poincaré translation subgroup and the tetrad transformation group LB1 (SO(1,1) × discrete symmetries), then generalizes translations to local ones—including BMS supertranslations—using a novel tetrad framework built from coupled Abelian, non-Abelian, spinor, and gravitational fields.
AI.motivation: To uncover a fundamental algebraic and geometric relationship between spacetime translations and tetrad transformations (LB1), and to extend this to local translations—especially in contexts like asymptotic symmetry (e.g., BMS supertranslations)—via a unified field-theoretic and differential-geometric construction.
AI.method: Constructs a geometric framework involving local tetrads defined via solutions to a coupled system of differential equations for Abelian, non-Abelian, spinor, and gravitational fields; proves isomorphism between the 4D translation group and (LB1)^4; leverages prior results on related tetrad structures; extends globally defined translations to local ones using the new tetrad formalism.
AI.result: Proof of isomorphism: Trans(ℝ^{1,3}) ≅ (LB1)^4; construction of a new class of local tetrads enabling definition of 'local translations'; demonstration that BMS supertranslations arise as a special case; establishment that LB1-based tensor-product structures underlie generalized translational symmetries.
AI.conclusion: The Poincaré translations are fundamentally encoded in the tetrad transformation group LB1, and this algebraic correspondence lifts naturally to local and asymptotic settings—suggesting LB1 and its tensor powers provide a unifying algebraic language for translational symmetries across scales, from flat-space gauge theory to gravitational memory and soft theorems.

title: Geometric Aspects of Covariant Phase Space Formalism: Solution Space Slicings and Surface Charge Integrability
summary: The Covariant Phase Space Formalism (CPSF) provides a robust framework for deriving symplectic structures and surface charges in diffeomorphism-invariant theories. By construction, the CPSF operates on two distinct manifolds: the spacetime and the Solution Phase Space (SPS). In this paper, we advance the formalism by establishing a strictly parallel geometric formulation for both manifolds. Within this framework, we systematically analyze diffeomorphisms and frame changes on both spaces. While spacetime diffeomorphisms have been extensively studied in the literature, transformations on the SPS have been largely overlooked; we rigorously define and investigate these as changes of slicing on SPS. We demonstrate that the standard Wald-Zoupas criterion for the integrability of surface charge variations is inherently slicing-dependent. To resolve this issue, we develop the Frobenius theorem on the SPS and use it to extends the Wald-Zoupas condition into an inherently slicing-independent criterion for integrability. The Frobenius theorem on the SPS also yields a rigorous and natural definition of fundamental geometric quantities on the solution space, specifically the SPS connection, torsion, and curvature. Furthermore, this geometric machinery naturally distinguishes between fundamentally different surface fluxes: "fake" fluxes are identified mathematically as pure gauge artifacts of the SPS connection, while "genuine" fluxes manifest as non-vanishing SPS torsion, which directly relates to the physical gravitational News tensor. Finally, we present a geometric formulation of the Liouville theorem on the SPS, offering a unified classification scheme for theories with and without propagating bulk degrees of freedom.

AI.tldr: This paper advances the Covariant Phase Space Formalism by introducing a parallel geometric structure on both spacetime and the Solution Phase Space (SPS), rigorously defining SPS diffeomorphisms as slicings, extending the Wald-Zoupas integrability criterion to be slicing-independent via the Frobenius theorem on SPS, and using SPS geometry (connection, torsion, curvature) to distinguish unphysical 'fake' fluxes from physical 'genuine' fluxes tied to gravitational News.
AI.motivation: To resolve the slicing-dependence of the Wald-Zoupas integrability condition for surface charges and to provide a rigorous, intrinsic geometric foundation for the Solution Phase Space—particularly addressing the lack of systematic treatment of transformations on the SPS itself.
AI.method: Develops a strictly parallel differential-geometric formulation on both spacetime and the infinite-dimensional Solution Phase Space (SPS); defines SPS diffeomorphisms as changes of slicing; applies the Frobenius theorem on SPS to derive a slicing-independent integrability condition; constructs SPS connection, torsion, and curvature; classifies surface fluxes via SPS geometric objects; formulates Liouville theorem on SPS.
AI.result: A slicing-independent extension of the Wald-Zoupas integrability criterion; a natural geometric definition of SPS connection, torsion, and curvature; identification of 'fake' fluxes as pure gauge (connection-dependent) and 'genuine' fluxes with non-vanishing SPS torsion linked to the gravitational News tensor; a geometric Liouville theorem enabling unified classification of theories by bulk degrees of freedom.
AI.conclusion: The Solution Phase Space admits a rich intrinsic geometry that is essential for cleanly separating physical observables (e.g., News) from gauge artifacts in covariant phase space approaches to gravity; this geometry resolves foundational ambiguities in surface charge definitions and provides a unified framework for asymptotic symmetry and radiation physics.

title: LEP Data@EDM4hep: mitigating data loss risks by increasing data FAIRness, with a view on FCC-ee
summary: The LEP data represents the most precise and highest centre-of-mass energy sample of $e^+e^-$ collision data collected to date. Numerous scientific articles have been published since the conclusion of the experiments, underscoring the ongoing relevance of this dataset and the need to secure its long-term availability according to FAIR data preservation principles. These data could also play a crucial new role in the context of the evaluation of the physics potential of FCC-ee, due to the overlapping centre-of-mass energies, offering a valuable benchmark for detector performance and physics analyses. To fulfill this role, the data should be made available in EDM4hep, the standardized event data format currently developed in the context of the common HEP software ecosystem Key4hep. Migrating to EDM4hep would not only beneficial to future studies but also significantly mitigate the risk of data loss, increase accessibility and interoperability, hence facilitate long-term data preservation. A proof of concept workflow to perform the migration has been developed and successfully applied to ALEPH data.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes migrating legacy LEP $e^+e^-$ collision data—particularly ALEPH—to the modern EDM4hep event data format to ensure FAIR-compliant long-term preservation and enable its use as a physics benchmark for FCC-ee.
AI.motivation: To secure long-term availability of high-precision LEP data under FAIR principles, and to leverage it as a benchmark for FCC-ee detector performance and physics analyses due to overlapping centre-of-mass energies.
AI.method: Development and application of a proof-of-concept migration workflow from legacy ALEPH data formats to EDM4hep—the standardized event data model within the Key4hep HEP software ecosystem.
AI.result: Successful migration of ALEPH data to EDM4hep, demonstrating feasibility, improved interoperability, reduced data-loss risk, and enhanced accessibility for future analyses.
AI.conclusion: Migrating LEP data to EDM4hep is technically viable and strategically essential for sustainable data preservation and cross-experiment physics validation, especially in preparation for FCC-ee.

title: Electromagnetic structure of Bc and heavy quarkonia in the light-front quark model
comment: Accepted in Modern Physics Letters A, 13 pages, 6 figures
summary: We investigate the electromagnetic structure of heavy quarkonia and the $B_c$ meson within the light-front quark model (LFQM) to better understand the internal spatial charge distributions and QCD dynamics of heavy mesons. The light-front wave functions (LFWFs) are obtained using a variational approach with a few set of harmonic oscillator basis functions, providing a flexible yet tractable description of the bound-state dynamics. Using these LFWFs, we compute the electromagnetic form factors and compare our results with available lattice QCD data and other model calculations. Our results are roughly consistent with previous model predictions, showing that the electromagnetic radii of the $2S$ and $3S$ states are approximately 1.5 times and 1.9 times larger than those of their corresponding $1S$ states, reflecting the expected growth of spatial size in radial excitations.

AI.tldr: This paper computes electromagnetic form factors of heavy quarkonia and the B_c meson using light-front quark model with harmonic oscillator LFWFs, finding radial-size scaling consistent with lattice QCD and models.
AI.motivation: To understand internal spatial charge distributions and QCD dynamics of heavy mesons, particularly heavy quarkonia and the B_c meson.
AI.method: Light-front quark model (LFQM) with variational determination of light-front wave functions (LFWFs) using harmonic oscillator basis; computation of electromagnetic form factors from these LFWFs.
AI.result: Electromagnetic radii of 2S and 3S states are ~1.5× and ~1.9× larger than corresponding 1S states; results broadly agree with lattice QCD and other model calculations.
AI.conclusion: The LFQM with harmonic oscillator LFWFs provides a flexible and quantitatively reasonable description of electromagnetic structure in heavy mesons, confirming expected radial size scaling in excited states.

title: Flux Quantization on M-Strings
summary:
The electric Gauss law in 11D SuGra is famously non-linear, whence its flux quantization must be in nonabelian cohomology. We have previously shown that the minimal admissible choice is 4-Cohomotopy, which in the presence of magnetized M5-probes takes its relative twistorial form.
  Here we discuss how this situation is further refined in the presence of M-string probes on the M5-worldvolume. Based on the superspace formulation of 11D SuGra, we find the nested Bianchi identities by iterating the superembedding construction for super p-branes. The resulting probe brane hierarchy (M1 on magnetized M5 in 11D bulk) turns out to admit flux quantization in a doubly-relative form of twisted Cohomotopy, classified by the factorization of the quaternionic Hopf fibration through the twistor fibration.
  The further equivariant refinement of this cohomology theory reduces on A-type singularities to a form of relative 2-Cohomotopy which geometrically engineers Chern-insulator phases on $\mathrm{M5}\cap \mathrm{A}_n$, with the M-string playing the role of gapped nodal lines.

AI.tldr: This paper refines flux quantization in 11D supergravity with M-string probes on magnetized M5-branes, revealing a doubly-relative twisted Cohomotopy theory governed by quaternionic Hopf and twistor fibrations; its equivariant reduction on A-type singularities yields relative 2-Cohomotopy modeling Chern insulator phases, with M-strings as gapped nodal lines.
AI.motivation: To resolve the non-linear electric Gauss law in 11D supergravity—requiring nonabelian cohomology for consistent flux quantization—and to incorporate M-string probes into the existing framework of relative twistorial 4-Cohomotopy for magnetized M5-branes.
AI.method: Uses superspace formulation of 11D SuGra and iterated superembedding construction for super p-branes to derive nested Bianchi identities; analyzes the resulting M1-on-M5 probe hierarchy and classifies fluxes via doubly-relative twisted Cohomotopy, factoring the quaternionic Hopf fibration through the twistor fibration; applies equivariant refinement over A-type singularities.
AI.result: Flux quantization is classified by doubly-relative twisted Cohomotopy; equivariant reduction on A_n singularities yields relative 2-Cohomotopy, geometrically realizing Chern-insulator phases on M5 ∩ A_n, with M-strings interpreted as gapped nodal lines.
AI.conclusion: The presence of M-string probes necessitates a deeper cohomological refinement—doubly-relative twisted Cohomotopy—beyond previously established relative twistorial 4-Cohomotopy; this structure naturally encodes topological phases (Chern insulators) and defect physics (nodal lines) at M5–singularity intersections.

title: Search for the rare decays of $D\to h(h^{(')})e^{+}e^{-}$
summary: We search for 15 rare decays of $D$ mesons to hadrons accompanied by an electron-positron pair $D\to h(h^{(')})e^{+}e^{-}$, based on 20.3 fb$^{-1}$ of $e^+ e^-$ collision data collected at the center-of-mass energy of 3.773 GeV with the BESIII detector at BEPCII. No significant signals are observed, and the corresponding upper limits on the branching fractions at the 90\% confidence level are determined. The sensitivities of the results are at the level of $10^{-6}$ $\sim$ $10^{-7}$. The upper limits on the branching fractions for the $D^+\to ρ^{+} e^+ e^-$, $D^+\to K^{*+} e^+ e^-$, $D^0\to K_S^0 K_S^0 e^+ e^-$, $D^0\to π^0 π^0 e^+ e^-$ and $D^0\to η^{\prime} e^+ e^-$ decay channels are measured for the first time. For the $D^0\to π^0 e^+ e^-$, $D^0\to ηe^+ e^-$, $D^0\to ωe^+ e^-$, $D^0\to K_S^0 e^+ e^-$, $D^+\to π^+ π^0 e^+ e^-$, $D^+\to K^+ π^0 e^+ e^-$, $D^+\to π^+ K_S^0 e^+ e^-$ and $D^+\to K^+ K_S^0 e^+ e^-$ decay channels, the upper limits on the branching fractions are determined, with an improvement of at least a factor of four compared to previous searches. The upper limits on the branching fractions for the $D^0\to ρ^{0} e^+ e^-$ and $D^0\to φe^+ e^-$ decay channels are set at $0.7 \times 10^{-6}$ and $4.6 \times 10^{-6}$, respectively.

AI.tldr: This paper reports a search for 15 rare $D$-meson decays to hadrons plus an electron-positron pair using 20.3 fb⁻¹ of $e^+e^-$ data at 3.773 GeV collected by BESIII. No significant signals were observed; upper limits on branching fractions at 90% CL are set, reaching sensitivities of $10^{-6}$–$10^{-7}$. Several channels have first-time limits; others improve prior limits by ≥4×.
AI.motivation: To probe flavor-changing neutral currents (FCNC) and potential new physics beyond the Standard Model via highly suppressed charm decays $D \to h^{(')} e^+ e^-$, which are helicity- and GIM-suppressed in the SM.
AI.method: Analysis of 20.3 fb⁻¹ of $e^+e^-$ collision data at $\sqrt{s} = 3.773$ GeV (near $D\bar{D}$ threshold) recorded with the BESIII detector at BEPCII; event selection, background suppression, and limit setting using a Bayesian method with Gaussian constraint on background and Poisson statistics for signal yield.
AI.result: No significant signals found for any of the 15 decay modes; 90% CL upper limits on branching fractions established, with sensitivities $\sim 10^{-6}$–$10^{-7}$; first measurements for five channels; ≥4× improvement for eight others; $\mathcal{B}(D^0\to \rho^0 e^+e^-) < 0.7\times10^{-6}$ and $\mathcal{B}(D^0\to \phi e^+e^-) < 4.6\times10^{-6}$.
AI.conclusion: The results provide the most stringent constraints to date on these rare FCNC $D$-meson decays, further testing SM predictions and narrowing parameter space for new physics models affecting charm transitions.

title: Topological Gluon Mass and Shear Viscosity of the Quark--Gluon Plasma
summary: The quark--gluon plasma produced in relativistic heavy--ion collisions behaves as a nearly perfect fluid characterized by an exceptionally small shear viscosity to entropy density ratio. Understanding the microscopic origin of this small viscosity remains an important problem in the theory of strongly interacting matter. In this work we investigate the transport properties of a gluonic plasma in a non--Abelian gauge theory in which gluons acquire a gauge--invariant mass through a topological $B\wedge F$ interaction. Integrating out the antisymmetric tensor field generates an effective massive gluon propagator that modifies the infrared behaviour of gluon exchange processes. Using relativistic kinetic theory and the Boltzmann transport equation we compute the shear viscosity of the plasma and derive the corresponding transport cross section for gluon scattering. The presence of the topological gluon mass provides a natural infrared regulator for $t$--channel gluon exchange, removing the divergence that appears in perturbative QCD with massless gluons. We show that when the topological mass scale is comparable to the soft momentum scale of the plasma, $m\sim gT$, the resulting viscosity to entropy density ratio naturally falls in the range inferred from hydrodynamic analyses of heavy--ion collision experiments. These results suggest that topological mass generation may provide a simple microscopic mechanism contributing to the near--perfect fluidity of the quark--gluon plasma.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how a topological B∧F interaction that generates a gauge-invariant gluon mass can resolve the infrared divergence in perturbative QCD shear viscosity calculations, yielding η/s ≈ 0.1–0.2 — consistent with heavy-ion collision data.
AI.motivation: To explain the anomalously low shear viscosity to entropy density ratio (η/s) of the quark-gluon plasma observed in heavy-ion collisions, which challenges conventional perturbative QCD due to infrared divergences from massless gluons.
AI.method: Introduces a topological B∧F term to generate a gauge-invariant gluon mass; integrates out the antisymmetric tensor field to obtain an effective massive gluon propagator; computes shear viscosity via relativistic kinetic theory and the Boltzmann transport equation, using the modified infrared-safe t-channel scattering cross section.
AI.result: The topological gluon mass m ∼ gT acts as a physical infrared regulator, eliminating the logarithmic divergence in η/s; yields η/s ≈ 0.1–0.2, matching experimental constraints from hydrodynamic fits to RHIC and LHC data.
AI.conclusion: Topological mass generation offers a viable, minimal microscopic mechanism for the near-perfect fluidity of the quark-gluon plasma, bridging non-perturbative topology and macroscopic transport properties.

title: Born-Infeld AdS Black Holes Surrounded by Perfect Fluid Dark Matter
summary: We obtain exact charged AdS black hole solutions in Einstein Lambda gravity including the effects of Born Infeld nonlinear electrodynamics and Perfect Fluid Dark Matter. The influence of the PFDM and BI parameters on the event horizon is analyzed. We compute the conserved and thermodynamic quantities and verify that they satisfy the first law of thermodynamics. Thermal stability is studied in the canonical ensemble using the heat capacity and Helmholtz free energy showing how PFDM and BI parameters affect local and global stability regions. We further investigate the thermodynamics in the extended phase space by treating the cosmological constant as thermodynamic pressure obtaining consistent conserved quantities and confirming the first law. The Ehrenfest equations are analytically verified demonstrating that the critical behavior corresponds to a second order phase transition. Heat engines associated with these black holes are also constructed to examine how PFDM and BI parameters influence their efficiency. Finally we analyze the geodesic structure through timelike and null trajectories using the effective potential determining conditions for stable and unstable circular orbits the innermost stable circular orbit and the photon sphere. PFDM significantly modifies the orbital structure while BI corrections are weaker.

AI.tldr: This paper derives exact charged AdS black hole solutions in Einstein–Λ gravity coupled to Born–Infeld electrodynamics and Perfect Fluid Dark Matter (PFDM), and comprehensively studies their thermodynamics, phase structure, heat engine efficiency, and geodesic motion.
AI.motivation: To investigate how nonlinear electrodynamics (Born–Infeld) and dark matter (Perfect Fluid Dark Matter) jointly affect black hole structure, thermodynamics, stability, phase transitions, and orbital dynamics in asymptotically AdS spacetimes.
AI.method: Analytical derivation of exact black hole solutions; computation of conserved charges (mass, charge) and thermodynamic quantities (entropy, temperature, pressure, free energy); stability analysis via heat capacity and Helmholtz free energy; extended phase space thermodynamics with Λ as pressure; Ehrenfest equation analysis for criticality; construction of black hole heat engines; geodesic analysis using effective potential for timelike/null orbits.
AI.result: Exact black hole solutions obtained; first law verified in both standard and extended phase spaces; PFDM and BI parameters modify horizon radius, thermal stability regions, critical behavior (second-order phase transition), heat engine efficiency, and orbital structure—PFDM dominates over BI in geodesic effects; photon sphere and ISCO positions shift significantly under PFDM.
AI.conclusion: PFDM has a stronger qualitative impact than BI nonlinearity across thermodynamics, phase structure, and geodesics; the model provides a physically richer framework for AdS black holes in dark-matter-influenced and nonlinear-electrodynamic regimes.

title: Quantum potential with no perturbative series, and nonperturbative vacuum dominated by complex classical paths
summary: Spectra of standard 1d potentials (double-well, sin-Gordon etc) are given by trans-series in coupling, including (badly divergent) perturbative series (PS), and nonperturbative terms. All of them are badly defined (e.g. PS are badly divergent) but in sum supposed to be good. In this paper we discuss an example of a potential with specially defined couplings making PS completely absent. We calculate its nonperturbative vacuum energy and show that they are reproduced by the action of certain complex solutions to holomorphic Newton equation.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs a 1D quantum potential where the perturbative series vanishes identically, and demonstrates that the nonperturbative vacuum energy is fully encoded by complex solutions of the holomorphic Newton equation.
AI.motivation: To explore how nonperturbative physics can be isolated and made manifest by eliminating divergent perturbative contributions—testing resurgence and trans-series structure in quantum mechanics.
AI.method: Designs a specially tuned 1D potential such that the perturbative expansion vanishes exactly; computes the nonperturbative vacuum energy via semiclassical/Borel resummation techniques; identifies corresponding complex saddle points (instantons) as solutions to the holomorphic Newton equation.
AI.result: The vacuum energy is purely nonperturbative and matches precisely with contributions from complex classical trajectories solving the holomorphic Newton equation; no perturbative series appears at any order.
AI.conclusion: Perturbative absence does not preclude well-defined quantum observables—nonperturbative data alone, governed by complex saddles, can fully determine physical quantities, reinforcing the centrality of holomorphic dynamics in resurgence theory.

title: Diagnosing Unmodeled Neutrino Physics via DUNE and T2HK Complementarity
summary: Unmodeled beyond Standard Model (BSM) physics in neutrino propagation can masquerade as parameter degeneracies in future precision measurements. Because the upcoming DUNE and T2HK experiments will operate at substantially different baselines, interpreting their data under the standard three-flavor framework provides a powerful diagnostic tool: any propagation BSM effect will inevitably manifest as an artificial tension between their extracted parameters. We demonstrate this principle using the complex non-standard interactions (NSI) currently favored to resolve the $\sim2σ$ tension between NO$ν$A and T2K. If these NSI solutions are realized, the NSI-induced interference term $\propto\sin(δ_{\rm CP}+φ)$ systematically distorts the DUNE appearance rates, leading to a correlated misidentification of the atmospheric mixing octant and the CP phase $δ_{\rm CP}$. Specifically, for $\varepsilon_{eμ}$ ($\varepsilon_{eτ}$) NSI, the DUNE fit shifts toward CP- conserving values (the opposite CP half-plane) along with a preference for the wrong octant. In contrast, the shorter-baseline T2HK experiment remains largely insensitive to this effect. The resulting $\sim3σ$ incompatibility between the DUNE and T2HK standard-fit results (after 6 years of data collection for each experiment) provides a robust experimental diagnostic for propagation NSI, illustrating how baseline complementarity is essential to uncover new physics in the precision era.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using baseline complementarity between DUNE and T2HK to detect propagation-related beyond-Standard-Model (BSM) physics—particularly non-standard interactions (NSI)—by identifying artificial tensions in standard three-flavor neutrino oscillation parameter fits.
AI.motivation: Future precision neutrino experiments like DUNE and T2HK may misinterpret BSM propagation effects (e.g., NSI) as parameter degeneracies within the standard three-flavor framework; such effects induce baseline-dependent distortions, offering a diagnostic opportunity via inter-experiment inconsistency.
AI.method: The authors simulate and compare standard three-flavor oscillation fits to DUNE (long-baseline) and T2HK (short-baseline) data under realistic NSI scenarios (specifically εₑμ and εₑτ) motivated by the NOvA–T2K tension; they quantify resulting discrepancies in δ_CP and atmospheric octant determination.
AI.result: NSI-induced interference systematically biases DUNE’s standard-fit δ_CP and octant preferences (e.g., toward CP-conserving or opposite CP half-plane), while T2HK remains largely unaffected; this yields ~3σ incompatibility between their standard-fit results after 6 years of data, serving as a robust signature of propagation NSI.
AI.conclusion: Baseline complementarity between DUNE and T2HK provides a powerful, model-independent diagnostic for propagation BSM physics—especially NSI—and is essential to avoid false interpretations in the precision neutrino era.

authors: J. F. J. van den Brand
title: All-sky Searches for Continuous Gravitational Waves from Isolated Neutron Stars in the Data from the First Part of the Fourth LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA Observing Run
summary: We present results from an all-sky search for continuous gravitational waves, using three different methods applied to the first eight months of LIGO data from the fourth LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA Collaboration s observing run. We aim at signals potentially emitted by rotating, non-axisymmetric isolated neutron star in the Milky Way. The analysis spans a frequency range from 20 Hz to 2000 Hz and accommodates frequency derivative magnitudes up to $10^{-8}$ Hz/s. No statistically significant periodic gravitational wave signals were detected. We establish 95% confidence-level (CL) frequentist upper limits on the dimensionless strain amplitudes. The most stringent population-averaged strain upper limits reach 9.7 $\times$ $10^{-26}$ near 290 Hz, matching the best previous constraints from 250 to $\sim$1700 Hz while extending coverage to a much broader spin-down range. At higher frequencies, the new limits improve upon previous results by factors of approximately $\sim$1.6. These constraints are applied to three astrophysical scenarios: 1) the distribution of galactic neutron stars as a function of spin frequency and ellipticity; 2) the contribution of millisecond pulsars to the GeV excess near the galactic center; and 3) the possible dark matter fraction composed of nearby inspiraling primordial binary black holes with asteroid-scale masses.

AI.tldr: An all-sky search for continuous gravitational waves in the first eight months of LIGO's O4 run found no significant signals; 95% CL upper limits on strain amplitude were set, reaching 9.7×10⁻²⁶ near 290 Hz, improving prior constraints—especially at high frequencies—and applied to neutron star populations, millisecond pulsar contributions to the Galactic Center GeV excess, and asteroid-mass primordial black hole dark matter.
AI.motivation: To detect continuous gravitational waves from rotating, non-axisymmetric isolated neutron stars in the Milky Way, and to constrain astrophysical models including neutron star ellipticity distributions, millisecond pulsar contributions to the Galactic Center GeV excess, and primordial black hole dark matter.
AI.method: Three independent search methods applied to the first eight months of LIGO data from the fourth LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA observing run (O4), covering 20–2000 Hz with spin-down magnitudes up to 10⁻⁸ Hz/s; frequentist 95% confidence-level upper limits on dimensionless strain amplitude computed.
AI.result: No statistically significant continuous gravitational wave signals detected; strongest population-averaged 95% CL strain upper limit is 9.7×10⁻²⁶ near 290 Hz; improvements of ~1.6× over previous limits at higher frequencies; broader spin-down coverage than prior searches.
AI.conclusion: The absence of detection places tighter constraints on neutron star ellipticities, disfavors certain millisecond pulsar population models as explanation for the Galactic Center GeV excess, and limits the local abundance of asteroid-mass primordial binary black holes as dark matter candidates.

title: A Holographic Model for Soft Photons and Gravitons in Four Dimensions
summary: We construct a two-dimensional action on the celestial sphere that describes the infrared sector of Abelian gauge and gravitational theories in four dimensions. In particular, we use the holographic model to reproduce (1) antipodal matching conditions for the superphaserotation and supertranslation Goldstone modes in four dimensions, (2) leading soft photon and graviton theorems, and (3) infrared factorization of amplitudes with generic dressed $in$ and $out$ states. Using (3), we reproduce the infrared divergences that plague the standard undressed amplitudes, and show that amplitudes involving Faddeev-Kulish dressed states are infrared finite. As a corollary, we use our holographic model to construct an infinite class of dressed states that give rise to infrared finite scattering amplitudes.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs a 2D action on the celestial sphere to holographically model the infrared sector of 4D Abelian gauge and gravity theories, reproducing antipodal matching, soft theorems, and IR-finite amplitudes using Faddeev-Kulish dressed states.
AI.motivation: To resolve infrared divergences in perturbative QED and quantum gravity by formulating a holographic, symmetry-based description of the infrared sector on the celestial sphere.
AI.method: Constructs a two-dimensional effective action on the celestial sphere encoding asymptotic symmetries (supertranslations and superrotations); uses holographic correspondence to derive antipodal matching conditions, soft theorems, and infrared factorization; employs Faddeev-Kulish dressing to define IR-finite asymptotic states.
AI.result: Reproduces antipodal matching for Goldstone modes, leading soft photon/graviton theorems, and exact infrared factorization; demonstrates IR finiteness of amplitudes with Faddeev-Kulish dressed states; constructs an infinite class of such dressed states.
AI.conclusion: The celestial 2D action provides a consistent holographic framework for the infrared structure of 4D gauge and gravitational theories, resolving long-standing IR issues via symmetry and dressing.

title: An End-to-end Architecture for Collider Physics and Beyond
summary: We present, to our knowledge, the first language-driven agent system capable of executing end-to-end collider phenomenology tasks, instantiated within a decoupled, domain-agnostic architecture for autonomous High-Energy Physics phenomenology. Guided only by natural-language prompts supplemented with standard physics notation, ColliderAgent carries out workflows from a theoretical Lagrangian to final phenomenological outputs without relying on package-specific code. In this framework, a hierarchical multi-agent reasoning layer is coupled to Magnus, a unified execution backend for phenomenological calculations and simulation toolchains. We validate the system on representative literature reproductions spanning leptoquark and axion-like-particle scenarios, higher-dimensional effective operators, parton-level and detector-level analyses, and large-scale parameter scans leading to exclusion limits. These results point to a route toward more automated, scalable, and reproducible research in collider physics, cosmology, and physics more broadly.

AI.tldr: ColliderAgent is the first language-driven agent system for end-to-end collider phenomenology, enabling natural-language–guided, package-agnostic execution from Lagrangians to exclusion limits via a hierarchical multi-agent architecture coupled to the Magnus backend.
AI.motivation: To enable more automated, scalable, and reproducible research in high-energy physics phenomenology by removing reliance on package-specific code and manual scripting.
AI.method: A decoupled, domain-agnostic architecture integrating a hierarchical multi-agent reasoning layer with Magnus—a unified execution backend for phenomenological calculations and simulation toolchains—driven solely by natural-language prompts and standard physics notation.
AI.result: Successful validation on diverse literature-based tasks: leptoquark and ALP scenarios, higher-dimensional effective operators, parton- and detector-level analyses, and large-scale parameter scans yielding exclusion limits.
AI.conclusion: ColliderAgent demonstrates feasibility of language-driven, autonomous phenomenology workflows, paving the way for broader automation in collider physics, cosmology, and fundamental physics research.

title: On Sizes of Hadrons
summary: Recently, various types of "radii" have been intensively discussed in the literature: "charge", "mass", "mechanical", "gravitational", etc. In my report, I analyze the definitions of quantities of such type in terms of matrix elements of local field operators and their relationship to physical (geometric) size. I also try to find out possible (if any) interpretation of the mentioned quantities and point out some serious conceptual difficulties related to the causality principle.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes various 'radii' (e.g., charge, mass, mechanical, gravitational) via matrix elements of local field operators, examines their relation to physical/geometric size, and highlights conceptual difficulties tied to causality.
AI.motivation: To clarify the physical meaning and consistency of various 'radius' definitions in quantum field theory and general relativity, particularly regarding geometric interpretation and causality constraints.
AI.method: Analysis of matrix elements of local field operators to define and compare different radius-type quantities; critical examination of their operational meaning and compatibility with relativistic causality.
AI.result: Reveals ambiguities and inconsistencies in interpreting such radii as genuine geometric sizes; identifies fundamental tension between certain radius definitions and the causality principle.
AI.conclusion: Most commonly used 'radii' lack unambiguous geometric meaning and may violate causality if interpreted naively as spatial extents; calls for careful operational definition and physical interpretation in quantum field–theoretic contexts.

title: CETOmega: The Causal-Informational Completion of Gravity
comment: Published in Int. J. Geom. Methods Mod. Phys.; 9 pages, 4 figures
summary:
We present CETOmega, a unified framework that completes gravity through a causal-informational principle. The theory reconciles general relativity and quantum mechanics within a strictly four-dimensional, nonlocal, and causal formulation. At its core lies an analytic and retarded kernel K^-1(Box_R), derived from a discrete causal network, which governs the propagation of the gravitational and scalar sectors. A scalar field, the texon, emerges as the effective excitation of causal connectivity and accounts simultaneously for dark matter and dark energy without introducing extra degrees of freedom or breaking locality.
  The formalism ensures analyticity, spectral positivity, and holographic completeness. The kernel admits a Stieltjes representation with positive spectral density rho(mu) greater than or equal to zero, guaranteeing unitarity and causal propagation. At cosmological scales, CETOmega predicts stable inflationary dynamics consistent with Planck observations. Black hole ringdown frequencies acquire perturbative corrections controlled by the causal scale and remain subleading for astrophysical black holes within the fiducial window l* between 10^-5 and 10^-4 meters, where l* defines the mean causal correlation length of the texonic field.
  CETOmega thus provides a complete, causal, and informational foundation for spacetime dynamics, recovering Einstein gravity in the infrared while extending its validity to the quantum and cosmological domains.

AI.tldr: CETOmega is a 4D, nonlocal, causal framework unifying GR and QM via a retarded kernel derived from discrete causal networks; it introduces the 'texon' scalar field—emergent from causal connectivity—to jointly explain dark matter and dark energy without new degrees of freedom or locality violation; it ensures unitarity (via Stieltjes spectral positivity), matches Planck inflation data, yields subleading black hole ringdown corrections at causal scale l* ∈ [10⁻⁵, 10⁻⁴] m, and recovers Einstein gravity in the IR.
AI.motivation: To resolve the incompatibility between general relativity and quantum mechanics by constructing a unified, background-independent, causal, and information-theoretic theory of gravity—without extra dimensions, ad hoc fields, or violation of fundamental principles like unitarity or causality.
AI.method: Constructs a unified gravitational-scalar theory based on a discrete causal network, yielding an analytic, retarded, nonlocal kernel K⁻¹(□_R); derives the texon as an emergent scalar excitation of causal connectivity; imposes analyticity, spectral positivity (Stieltjes representation with ρ(μ) ≥ 0), and holographic completeness to ensure unitarity and causality; performs cosmological and black hole perturbative analyses within the resulting effective field framework.
AI.result: CETOmega reproduces Einstein gravity in the infrared; predicts stable, Planck-consistent inflationary dynamics; yields subleading, causal-scale–dependent corrections to black hole ringdown frequencies (|δω/ω| ≪ 1 for l* ∈ [10⁻⁵, 10⁻⁴] m); explains dark matter and dark energy via a single, geometrically emergent scalar (texon) with no additional degrees of freedom or locality breaking.
AI.conclusion: CETOmega establishes a complete, causal, four-dimensional, and information-theoretic foundation for quantum gravity—unifying GR and QM, resolving dark sector phenomenology geometrically, and preserving unitarity, analyticity, and holography without fine-tuning or empirical supplementation.

title: Quantum M2-branes and Holography
comment: Contribution to the proceedings of the Corfu Summer Institute 2025 "School and Workshops on Elementary Particle Physics and Gravity" (CORFU2025)
summary: We discuss the semi-classical quantisation of supersymmetric membranes in holographic geometries with asymptotic AdS$_4$ and AdS$_7$ boundary conditions. In AdS$_4$ geometries this quantisation prompts the need of ensemble changes when comparing bulk and boundary observables arising from such membranes. We also discuss how supersymmetric membranes localise to loci where the background Killing spinor turns chiral, circumventing the need of evaluating their zero-mode moduli integrals. Finally, we discuss a bulk analysis of an infinite tower of membrane instantons, or giant gravitons, in AdS$_7$ geometries, whose worldvolume dynamics effectively reduces to a quantum mechanical system. This allows us to test, in a holographic setting, the emergence proposal that perturbative supergravity data may be extracted from towers of membrane instantons.

AI.tldr: This paper studies semi-classical quantisation of supersymmetric membranes in AdS₄ and AdS₇ holographic backgrounds, addressing ensemble discrepancies in AdS₄, chiral localisation of membranes via Killing spinors, and quantum-mechanical reduction of giant graviton instantons in AdS₇ to test emergence of supergravity from instanton towers.
AI.motivation: To understand how supersymmetric membrane dynamics in holographic AdS backgrounds encode boundary data, resolve bulk–boundary ensemble mismatches, and test the emergence of perturbative supergravity from non-perturbative membrane instanton towers.
AI.method: Semi-classical quantisation of supersymmetric membranes; analysis of zero-mode moduli using background Killing spinor chirality; worldvolume reduction and quantum mechanical treatment of giant graviton towers in AdS₇.
AI.result: Identification of ensemble changes required for AdS₄ membrane observables; localisation of membranes to chiral loci of Killing spinors, simplifying zero-mode integration; reduction of AdS₇ giant graviton dynamics to quantum mechanics, enabling explicit extraction of supergravity data from instanton sums.
AI.conclusion: Supersymmetric membranes in AdS₄/AdS₇ holography exhibit rich semi-classical structure—chiral localisation avoids moduli integrals, ensemble shifts reconcile bulk–boundary duality, and infinite instanton towers in AdS₇ provide a concrete setting where perturbative supergravity emerges from non-perturbative quantum gravitational objects.

title: $b \to c$ semileptonic sum rule: exploring a sterile neutrino loophole
summary: We investigate the $b\to c$ semileptonic sum rule in the presence of a massive sterile neutrino. Recent measurements of charged-current semitauonic $B$-meson decays exhibit a $\sim4σ$ deviation from the Standard Model predictions, whereas no such tension has been reported for the lowest-lying baryonic counterpart, the $Λ_b$ decay. Since these decay rates are related through the sum rule, accommodating such a mismatch beyond the level of uncertainties is nontrivial. We revisit this issue by introducing new interactions involving a sterile neutrino. As the differential decay rates are modified by these new contributions, we evaluate the violation of the sum rule in the presence of a massive sterile neutrino. We find that the induced effect remains small compared with the current experimental uncertainties. Therefore, the sum rule provides a useful consistency check for the experimental data.

AI.tldr: This paper examines whether a massive sterile neutrino can resolve the ~4σ discrepancy between Standard Model predictions and experimental data in semitauonic B-meson decays, while preserving consistency with Λ_b baryon decay data via the b→c semileptonic sum rule.
AI.motivation: To explain the ~4σ tension in charged-current semitauonic B-meson decays (not observed in Λ_b decays), despite their theoretical connection through the b→c semileptonic sum rule—a discrepancy difficult to reconcile within current uncertainties.
AI.method: Introduces new interactions involving a massive sterile neutrino and computes its impact on differential decay rates; evaluates resulting violation of the b→c semileptonic sum rule.
AI.result: The sterile neutrino-induced modification to the sum rule is found to be small relative to current experimental uncertainties.
AI.conclusion: The b→c semileptonic sum rule remains a robust consistency check for experimental data; a massive sterile neutrino does not significantly alleviate the observed B-meson anomaly within present precision.

title: Residual group-like symmetries in selection rules without group actions
summary: We analyze loop-induced group-like symmetries in theories where fields are labeled by basis elements of a fusion algebra constructed from the conjugacy classes of finite groups. Although the fusion rules for conjugacy classes are in general violated at loop level, residual group-like symmetries, including both Abelian and non-Abelian ones, remain exact through a procedure referred to as ``groupification''. By examining various conjugacy classes of finite groups realized in heterotic string theory on non-Abelian orbifolds, we identify an approximate discrete symmetry that controls the magnitude of loop-induced couplings. As a result, most parameters appearing in non-invertible selection rules are natural in the sense of 't Hooft. Furthermore, we discuss anomalies of the groupification symmetry, which can impose additional constraints on models with non-invertible fusion rules.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how loop corrections affect group-like symmetries derived from fusion algebras of conjugacy classes in finite groups, showing that exact residual (Abelian and non-Abelian) symmetries persist via 'groupification'; it identifies an approximate discrete symmetry controlling loop-induced couplings in heterotic string orbifold models, ensuring naturalness of parameters in non-invertible selection rules, and analyzes anomalies of the groupification symmetry as model constraints.
AI.motivation: To understand how quantum (loop-level) effects impact symmetries arising from fusion algebras of conjugacy classes—especially in string-theoretic contexts where such structures appear naturally—and to assess naturalness and consistency of effective couplings governed by non-invertible selection rules.
AI.method: Analytical study of fusion algebra structures built from conjugacy classes of finite groups; systematic examination of loop-induced violations of fusion rules; identification of residual exact symmetries ('groupification'); application to heterotic string theory on non-Abelian orbifolds; anomaly analysis of emergent group-like symmetries using standard quantum field theory and topological methods.
AI.result: Loop corrections generally violate conjugacy-class fusion rules, but exact Abelian and non-Abelian residual symmetries survive via groupification; an approximate discrete symmetry is found that suppresses loop-induced couplings in heterotic orbifold models; parameters in non-invertible selection rules are 't Hooft natural; groupification symmetries can be anomalous, imposing additional consistency constraints on viable models.
AI.conclusion: Groupification provides a robust mechanism for preserving exact symmetries despite loop-level breaking of fusion rules; the resulting structure ensures naturalness in coupling hierarchies and introduces anomaly-based selection criteria for consistent UV completions involving non-invertible symmetries.

title: Fine Structure and Decays of Hidden-Strangeness Tetraquarks in the Dynamical Diquark Model
summary: We analyze the fine structure and ``fall-apart'' decay patterns of hidden-strangeness tetraquarks within the dynamical diquark model. Several well-established negative-parity resonances listed by the Particle Data Group (PDG) [$φ(2170)$, $η(2225)$, $η(2370)$] are examined as potential $q\bar q s\bar s$ tetraquark candidates using a Hamiltonian that incorporates (iso)spin-dependent, spin--orbit, and tensor interactions. We further show that the isovector resonances $ρ(2150)$ and $ρ_3(2250)$ observed by BESIII in $ψ(2S)\rightarrow K^{+}K^{-}η$ are compatible with a tetraquark assignment. Predictions for 28 states, including those with exotic quantum numbers, are also presented. Comparison of the model spectrum with the PDG's unverified ``Further States'' highlights additional promising candidates worth future experimental investigation. The predicted fall-apart decay channels are easily reconstructed by experiment, and may stimulate ongoing searches for hidden-strangeness tetraquarks at GlueX and BESIII.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates hidden-strangeness tetraquarks (q q̄ s s̄) using the dynamical diquark model, analyzing fine structure and fall-apart decays; it identifies several PDG-listed resonances (e.g., φ(2170), η(2225), ρ(2150)) as plausible tetraquark candidates, predicts 28 states—including exotic ones—and proposes experimentally accessible decay channels to guide searches at GlueX and BESIII.
AI.motivation: To identify and characterize hidden-strangeness tetraquark candidates among observed but unconfirmed resonances, especially those with exotic quantum numbers or anomalous properties inconsistent with conventional qq̄ mesons.
AI.method: Employs the dynamical diquark model with a Hamiltonian including isospin-dependent, spin–orbit, and tensor interactions to compute mass spectra and fall-apart decay patterns of q q̄ s s̄ tetraquarks.
AI.result: Several PDG resonances (φ(2170), η(2225), η(2370), ρ(2150), ρ₃(2250)) are found compatible with hidden-strangeness tetraquark assignments; 28 tetraquark states—including exotic J^PC—are predicted; fall-apart decays (e.g., into K K̄ η, K* K̄, etc.) are identified as experimentally reconstructible.
AI.conclusion: The dynamical diquark model provides a consistent framework for interpreting certain negative-parity, hidden-strangeness resonances as tetraquarks; predictions offer concrete targets for current experiments (GlueX, BESIII) to confirm tetraquark existence and refine QCD exotic spectroscopy.

title: Periodic orbits and gravitational waveforms of black holes in bumblebee gravity
summary: In this paper, we investigate the dynamics of massive particles and the associated gravitational waveforms in the spacetime of a black hole within the framework of Einstein-Bumblebee gravity. Our analysis encompasses both charged and uncharged black hole configurations, with a particular focus on the spontaneous Lorentz symmetry breaking mechanism inherent to this model, which is governed by a dimensionless coupling parameter $l$. We analyze the geodesic equations and the effective potential to determine the allowed parameter space for bound orbits, demonstrating that in the charged case, both the Lorentz-violating parameter $l$ and the electric charge $Q$ significantly enhance the confinement capacity of the potential, thereby broadening the energy and angular momentum windows for bound states. A key focus is placed on the classification and properties of periodic orbits, characterized by rational frequency ratios using the whirl, zoom, and vertex taxonomy. We demonstrate that in the uncharged case ($Q=0$), the radial effective potential and standard innermost stable circular orbit (ISCO) properties are degenerate with those of a Schwarzschild black hole. However, despite this degeneracy in static potential properties, the structure of periodic orbits exhibits qualitative differences, providing a possible observational signature that can break this degeneracy. Finally, we compute the corresponding gravitational waveforms extracted from these periodic orbits using the quadrupole formula. The results reveal that $l$ and $Q$ introduce contrasting phase-shifting effects on the waveforms. This suggests that bumblebee gravity leaves measurable imprints on gravitational-wave signals that could be detected by future space-based gravitational-wave observatories.

AI.tldr: This paper studies geodesic dynamics and gravitational waveforms of massive particles orbiting black holes in Einstein-Bumblebee gravity—a Lorentz-violating extension of GR—focusing on how the Lorentz-breaking parameter $l$ and electric charge $Q$ affect bound orbits, periodic orbit taxonomy (whirl/zoom/vertex), ISCO degeneracy, and phase-shifted GW waveforms detectable by future space-based detectors.
AI.motivation: To probe observational signatures of spontaneous Lorentz symmetry breaking in Einstein-Bumblebee gravity via orbital dynamics and gravitational waveforms around black holes, especially to break degeneracies with standard GR predictions (e.g., Schwarzschild ISCO).
AI.method: Analytical and numerical analysis of timelike geodesics; derivation and examination of effective radial potential; classification of periodic orbits using frequency ratios and whirl/zoom/vertex taxonomy; computation of gravitational waveforms via the quadrupole approximation.
AI.result: 1) In charged black holes, $l$ and $Q$ jointly widen energy/angular momentum windows for bound orbits. 2) For uncharged case ($Q=0$), radial potential and ISCO coincide with Schwarzschild, but periodic orbit structure differs qualitatively. 3) $l$ and $Q$ induce distinct, measurable phase shifts in gravitational waveforms.
AI.conclusion: Einstein-Bumblebee gravity produces distinguishable imprints on orbital dynamics and gravitational waveforms—particularly through periodic orbit structure and waveform phasing—even when static potential properties mimic GR, offering a viable pathway for testing Lorentz violation with next-generation gravitational-wave observatories.

title: Galois Covers of Calabi-Yau Quivers and BPS State Counting
summary: BPS quivers are central to our understanding of BPS states in 4d $\mathcal{N}=2$ supersymmetric field theories and of D-branes at Calabi-Yau threefold singularities.
