The two subjects are deeply interrelated through geometric engineering in Type II string theory, where a CY$_3$ quiver, also known as a 5d BPS quiver, describes fractional branes at a threefold singularity ${\bf X}$. We study the Galois cover ${\skew{2}\tilde Q}\rightarrow Q$ of any BPS quiver $Q$ by a finite abelian group $\mathbb{G}$, leading to a covering quiver ${\skew{2}\tilde Q}$. The Galois cover is determined by a $\mathbb{G}$-grading of the arrows of the quiver $Q$, which can be understood as an orbifolding procedure. In particular, if $Q$ is a CY$_3$ quiver for ${\bf X}$, then the Galois cover $\skew{2}\tilde Q$ is the CY$_3$ quiver for the orbifold singularity ${\bf X}/\mathbb{G}$. We explore such Galois covering procedures in the language of supersymmetric quiver quantum mechanics, in terms of fixed loci under $\mathbb{G}$ actions on moduli spaces of quiver representations, and in terms of homomorphisms between the Kontsevich-Soibelman algebras of $Q$ and ${\skew{2}\tilde Q}$. Our main result is an explicit covering formula for the BPS invariants of 4d $\mathcal{N}=2$ field theories, wherein the rational BPS invariant $\barΩ^Q(γ)$ of $Q$ is expressed as a sum of BPS invariants of $\skew{2}\tilde Q$. We derive this formula in various special cases, which include the case when $γ$ is a primitive charge vector, the case of general charge vectors for quivers without loops, and the case of CY$_3$ quivers for some simple geometries such as the conifold or local del Pezzo surfaces. The general formula is presented as a conjecture that can be verified in many examples.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Galois covers of BPS quivers by finite abelian groups, linking orbifold geometry, quiver representation theory, and Kontsevich–Soibelman algebras; it derives (and conjectures) a covering formula expressing rational BPS invariants of a quiver Q as sums of those of its Galois cover ˜Q, verified in key cases including CY₃ quivers for conifolds and local del Pezzos.
AI.motivation: To unify and generalize the relationship between BPS quivers, Calabi-Yau threefold singularities, and orbifold constructions—specifically, to understand how BPS invariants transform under abelian orbifold actions on geometries and their associated quivers.
AI.method: Constructs Galois covers Q̃ → Q via G-gradings of arrows; analyzes fixed loci of G-actions on moduli spaces of quiver representations; studies induced homomorphisms between Kontsevich–Soibelman (KS) algebras; derives BPS invariant relations using wall-crossing, quiver quantum mechanics, and geometric engineering in Type II string theory.
AI.result: An explicit covering formula expressing the rational BPS invariant Ω̄^Q(γ) as a weighted sum of BPS invariants Ω̄^Q̃(γ̃) over lifts γ̃ of γ; proven for primitive γ, loopless quivers, and specific CY₃ cases (conifold, local del Pezzos); proposed as a general conjecture.
AI.conclusion: Galois covering provides a powerful algebraic–geometric framework for relating BPS spectra across orbifold-related geometries; the covering formula offers a computational tool for bootstrapping BPS invariants and reveals deep structural constraints imposed by symmetry on KS algebras and quiver moduli spaces.

title: Cosmic anisotropic hair of nonlocal RT gravity
comment: 5 pages, 4 figures, published in Phys. Lett. B
summary: Nonlocal RT gravity has proven effective in explaining the late-time cosmic acceleration while remaining consistent with local gravity tests. However, most previous cosmological studies of this theory have assumed an isotropic background, which may not fully capture the slight anisotropies suggested by current observations, such as those inferred from Type Ia supernovae data. In this paper, we investigate the dynamical evolution of an anisotropic Bianchi type I universe within the framework of nonlocal RT gravity. By introducing six dimensionless variables, we construct the corresponding dynamical system and perform a detailed phase-space analysis. An unexpected finding is that, contrary to many dark energy models and modified gravity theories in which anisotropies decay with time, nonlocal RT gravity predicts a growth of cosmic anisotropy. This behavior poses a challenge to the cosmic no-hair theorem within the nonlocal RT gravity scenario.

AI.tldr: This paper studies anisotropic evolution in nonlocal RT gravity using dynamical systems analysis in Bianchi I cosmology, finding that cosmic anisotropy grows over time—contrary to the cosmic no-hair theorem and most dark energy models.
AI.motivation: To explore whether nonlocal RT gravity remains viable under anisotropic cosmological backgrounds, motivated by observational hints of slight anisotropies (e.g., from SNe Ia data) not captured by isotropic assumptions.
AI.method: Formulates the Einstein field equations for a Bianchi type I universe in nonlocal RT gravity; introduces six dimensionless variables to construct an autonomous dynamical system; performs phase-space analysis including fixed-point classification and stability analysis.
AI.result: Identifies fixed points corresponding to anisotropic solutions; shows that anisotropy does not decay but instead grows asymptotically, violating the cosmic no-hair conjecture within this theory.
AI.conclusion: Nonlocal RT gravity generically amplifies rather than suppresses cosmic anisotropy, implying potential tension with isotropy observations and challenging its viability as a complete dark energy framework unless additional mechanisms suppress early anisotropy.

title: Classical Gravitational Scattering from the Ultraviolet and the Absence of Calabi-Yau Integrals in the Conservative Sector at $O(G^5)$
summary: We explain why Calabi-Yau and complete elliptic integrals do not contribute to conservative observables at fifth post-Minkowskian order, despite appearing in intermediate steps. At even loop orders, conservative contributions are tied to terms proportional to the logarithm of the momentum transfer, which in dimensional regularization arise from singular regions. We show that in the classical limit, the integral classes responsible for Calabi-Yau and complete elliptic behavior are absent from the ultraviolet singular structures that generate the required logarithm. This perspective also suggests alternative strategies for analyzing the classical limit of multiloop integrals in the conservative sector at even loop orders.

AI.tldr: This paper explains why Calabi-Yau and complete elliptic integrals—though present in intermediate calculations—do not contribute to conservative physical observables at the fifth post-Minkowskian (5PM) order, due to their absence from UV-singular structures that generate momentum-transfer logarithms essential for classical conservative dynamics at even loop orders.
AI.motivation: To resolve the apparent paradox of Calabi-Yau and complete elliptic integrals appearing in intermediate multiloop amplitudes but not contributing to classical conservative observables at 5PM order.
AI.method: Analysis of ultraviolet singular structures in dimensional regularization, focusing on the origin of momentum-transfer logarithms in the classical limit and identifying which integral families (e.g., those yielding Calabi-Yau/elliptic behavior) can or cannot produce them.
AI.result: Calabi-Yau and complete elliptic integral classes are absent from the UV-singular regions responsible for generating the logarithmic dependence on momentum transfer; hence they decouple from conservative observables at even-loop (e.g., 5PM) orders.
AI.conclusion: The classical conservative sector at even loop orders is governed solely by UV-singular topologies producing log(q²) terms; non-logarithmic transcendental structures like Calabi-Yau integrals are irrelevant for observables, enabling simplified integral analysis strategies.

title: Calculation for Electric Dipole Moments of Lepton and Neutron in the N-B-LSSM via the Mass Insertion Approximation
summary:
In the N-B-LSSM, we calculate the electric dipole moments (EDMs) of lepton and neutron at the one loop level via the Mass Insertion Approximation (MIA). In the Standard Model (SM), charge parity (CP) violation originates only from the single phase of the Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM) matrix, and the predicted EDMs of lepton and neutron are far below the current experimental upper limits. Thus, EDMs serve as sensitive probes for exploring CP-violating phases in new physics.
  The N-B-LSSM extends the Minimal Supersymmetric Standard Model (MSSM) by introducing right-handed neutrino superfields and additional singlet Higgs superfields, which enriches the particle spectrum and the sources of CP violation. We derive the one loop analytical expressions for lepton and quark EDMs, and reveal their dependence on model parameters such as $g_{YB}$, $θ_{μ_H}$, $θ_{1'}$, $θ_{BB'}$ and $\tanβ$. Numerical analyses demonstrate that within a reasonable parameter space, the EDMs of leptons (electron, muon, tau) and the neutron can satisfy the current experimental limitations. This study provides a systematic theoretical tool and numerical reference for exploring CP violation and new physics under the N-B-LSSM.

AI.tldr: This paper calculates lepton and neutron electric dipole moments (EDMs) at one-loop level in the N-B-LSSM using Mass Insertion Approximation, identifying key CP-violating parameters and showing compatibility with current experimental bounds.
AI.motivation: EDMs are highly sensitive probes of CP violation beyond the Standard Model; the SM prediction is too small to explain potential signals, so new physics models like the N-B-LSSM—extending MSSM with right-handed neutrinos and singlet Higgs fields—offer richer CP-violating sources.
AI.method: One-loop calculation of lepton and neutron EDMs within the N-B-LSSM using the Mass Insertion Approximation (MIA); analytical expressions derived and parameter dependence (e.g., $g_{YB}$, $\theta_{\mu_H}$, $\theta_{1'}$, $\theta_{BB'}$, $\tan\beta$) analyzed numerically.
AI.result: Analytical one-loop EDM expressions obtained; numerical scans show that lepton (e, μ, τ) and neutron EDMs can remain within current experimental upper limits for reasonable N-B-LSSM parameter choices.
AI.conclusion: The N-B-LSSM provides viable CP-violating phases consistent with EDM constraints, offering a systematic theoretical and numerical framework for probing new physics via EDMs.

title: Scalaron excitation by topological vortices in quadratic $f(R)$ gravity on a BTZ black hole background
summary: In three spacetime dimensions, pure Einstein gravity admits no local propagating degrees of freedom, yet nontrivial gravitational backgrounds such as the BTZ black hole provide a natural arena to probe dynamical extensions of the theory. In quadratic $f(R)$ gravity the Ricci scalar becomes a propagating degree of freedom - the scalaron. We investigate how localized Maxwell-Higgs vortices excite this scalar mode in a static BTZ black-hole background. Working in the perturbative regime $α\ll \ell^2$, the trace equation reduces to a massive Klein-Gordon equation for the curvature scalar sourced by the trace of the vortex energy-momentum tensor. Using the Sturm-Liouville structure of the radial operator, we construct the corresponding Green function and obtain the curvature profile generated by an arbitrary localized source. The induced excitation exhibits a universal asymptotic decay $R(r) \sim r^{-(1+ν)}$, independent of the detailed vortex structure. The scalar excitation is linearly stable, carries finite energy, and produces parametrically suppressed backreaction, ensuring the smooth recovery of the Einstein limit. These results provide a concrete realization of how higher-curvature corrections activate the unique local gravitational degree of freedom in three dimensions and how localized sources excite this scalar mode in black-hole spacetimes.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how localized Maxwell-Higgs vortices excite the scalaron (a propagating Ricci-scalar mode) in quadratic f(R) gravity on a static BTZ black-hole background in 3D; it finds a universal power-law curvature profile R(r) ∼ r^{−(1+ν)}, linear stability, finite energy, and parametrically small backreaction—demonstrating how higher-curvature terms activate the sole local gravitational degree of freedom in 3D.
AI.motivation: In 3D pure Einstein gravity, there are no local propagating degrees of freedom; however, quadratic f(R) gravity introduces the scalaron—the Ricci scalar—as a dynamical mode. The motivation is to understand how localized matter sources (specifically Maxwell-Higgs vortices) excite this mode in a nontrivial black-hole geometry (BTZ), thereby probing physical consequences of higher-curvature corrections in a controlled setting.
AI.method: Working perturbatively in the coupling α ≪ ℓ² (where ℓ is the AdS radius), the authors reduce the trace equation of f(R) gravity to a massive Klein–Gordon equation for the curvature scalar R, sourced by the trace of the vortex energy-momentum tensor. They exploit the Sturm–Liouville structure of the radial differential operator to construct the Green function and solve for R(r) induced by an arbitrary localized source.
AI.result: The induced scalaron profile exhibits universal asymptotic decay R(r) ∼ r^{−(1+ν)} (with ν determined by mass and background parameters), is linearly stable, carries finite total energy, and yields parametrically suppressed metric backreaction — ensuring consistency with the Einstein limit as α → 0.
AI.conclusion: Quadratic f(R) gravity in 3D activates the scalaron as the unique local gravitational degree of freedom, and localized topological defects like vortices robustly excite it in BTZ black-hole spacetimes. The resulting scalar response is universal, stable, and controllably small, offering a clean framework to study dynamical higher-curvature effects in low-dimensional quantum gravity contexts.

title: Holographic Krylov complexity in the Coulomb branch of ${\cal N}=4$ SYM
summary: We study holographic Krylov complexity in the Coulomb branch of ${\cal N}=4$ SYM. Adopting the proposal that the time derivative of the Krylov complexity is dual to the proper radial momentum of a massive particle, we investigate two probe geodesics within this geometry. For one of the radial trajectories we obtain exact analytic results, even when additional motion in the internal space is included. In cases where the geodesic avoids the interior curvature singularity, the Krylov complexity exhibits oscillatory behavior, with a frequency governed by the Coulomb scale and an amplitude determined by the UV cutoff, the Coulomb scale, and the angular momentum. This oscillatory pattern is lost, when the radial trajectory is approaching the singularity. Finally, we compare our holographic results with field-theoretic calculations, finding qualitative agreement.

AI.tldr: This paper studies holographic Krylov complexity in the Coulomb branch of N=4 SYM using a dual geometric picture where time-derivative of complexity maps to radial momentum of a massive particle; exact geodesic solutions yield oscillatory complexity away from the singularity, with frequency set by the Coulomb scale and amplitude depending on UV cutoff and angular momentum; agreement with field-theoretic expectations is qualitatively confirmed.
AI.motivation: To understand quantum complexity growth in gauge theories via holography, specifically how Krylov complexity behaves in a deformed (Coulomb branch) AdS background where the bulk geometry contains a curvature singularity and a mass scale (Coulomb vev).
AI.method: Uses the holographic proposal linking dC_K/dt to proper radial momentum of a probe particle; solves geodesic equations in the Coulomb-branch geometry of N=4 SYM; analyzes two radial trajectories—one admitting exact analytic solution including internal-space motion; distinguishes behaviors based on whether geodesics avoid or approach the interior curvature singularity.
AI.result: Exact analytic Krylov complexity evolution showing oscillatory behavior when geodesics stay away from the singularity—frequency ∝ Coulomb scale, amplitude ∝ UV cutoff, Coulomb scale, and angular momentum; loss of oscillations near the singularity; qualitative match with independent field-theoretic estimates.
AI.conclusion: Krylov complexity in the Coulomb branch exhibits rich geometric dependence: its oscillatory dynamics encodes IR (Coulomb) and UV (cutoff) scales, and its breakdown near the singularity signals sensitivity to bulk spacetime structure—supporting the holographic complexity-momentum duality and reinforcing Krylov complexity as a probe of quantum chaoticity and geometry.

title: Two-loop QCD corrections to $η_b \to J/ψ+ γ$
summary: We present a next-to-leading-order (NLO) analysis of the rare radiative decay $η_b \to J/ψγ$, a flavor-changing transition between bottomonium and charmonium, within the framework of non-relativistic QCD (NRQCD). We systematically compute the complete $\mathcal{O}(α_s)$ corrections, which include the one-loop QCD corrections to the QED-initiated amplitudes and the two-loop corrections to the QCD-initiated ones. The branching ratio is enhanced from $2.06^{+2.82}_{-1.32}\times10^{-7}$ at LO to $7.53^{+5.67}_{-1.16}\times10^{-7}$ at NLO, representing an increase by a factor of about 3.65. The theoretical uncertainties caused by renormalization scale and $m_{b/c}$ masses are also discussed. Furthermore, the renormalization scale dependence is reduced at NLO.

AI.tldr: This paper computes the next-to-leading-order (NLO) QCD corrections to the rare radiative decay η_b → J/ψγ within NRQCD, finding a ~3.65× enhancement in the branching ratio and reduced renormalization scale dependence.
AI.motivation: To improve theoretical precision for the flavor-changing rare decay η_b → J/ψγ — a sensitive probe of heavy-quark dynamics and potential new physics — by including full O(α_s) corrections beyond leading order.
AI.method: Systematic NLO calculation in NRQCD: includes one-loop QCD corrections to QED-initiated amplitudes and two-loop QCD corrections to QCD-initiated amplitudes; accounts for renormalization scale and bottom/charm quark mass uncertainties.
AI.result: Branching ratio increases from (2.06^{+2.82}_{-1.32})×10^{-7} at LO to (7.53^{+5.67}_{-1.16})×10^{-7} at NLO — a factor ~3.65 enhancement; renormalization scale dependence is significantly reduced.
AI.conclusion: The NLO analysis substantially improves reliability of the η_b → J/ψγ prediction, making it more suitable for precision tests of NRQCD and searches for beyond-Standard-Model effects.

title: Can wormhole spacetimes in Unimodular Gravity be supported by ordinary matter? A general proof of the exotic matter requirement
summary: We establish a general no--go theorem demonstrating that all traversable wormhole configurations in Unimodular Gravity necessarily require exotic matter. The proof relies solely on the geometric flaring-out condition, $b'(r_0) \leq 1$, which directly implies that $ρ(r_0) + p_r(r_0) \leq 0$ at the throat. This condition represents a violation of the Null Energy Condition and, consequently, of the Weak and Strong Energy Conditions, independently of the particular choice of shape function, redshift function, or equation of state. This result holds for both tidal and zero-tidal-force configurations, showing that the requirement of exotic matter is a fundamental geometric consequence of the traversability condition rather than an artifact of specific solution choices. Therefore, Unimodular Gravity shares this fundamental constraint with General Relativity.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that traversable wormholes in Unimodular Gravity necessarily require exotic matter, as mandated by the geometric flaring-out condition — a result independent of specific functional forms or energy conditions beyond the NEC.
AI.motivation: To determine whether Unimodular Gravity allows traversable wormholes without exotic matter — a key distinction from General Relativity — by examining whether the geometric traversability condition inherently enforces energy condition violations.
AI.method: Analytical derivation using the geometric flaring-out condition $b'(r_0) \leq 1$ at the throat $r_0$, combined with the field equations of Unimodular Gravity, to derive the inequality $\rho(r_0) + p_r(r_0) \leq 0$, thereby showing violation of the Null Energy Condition (NEC).
AI.result: All traversable wormhole solutions in Unimodular Gravity must violate the NEC (and hence also the WEC and SEC) at the throat, regardless of shape function, redshift function, or equation of state; this holds for both zero-tidal and tidal-force configurations.
AI.conclusion: Unimodular Gravity does not circumvent the exotic matter requirement for traversable wormholes — it shares this fundamental obstruction with General Relativity, confirming that exotic matter is a geometric necessity, not a model-dependent artifact.

title: Baryons in $SO(N)$ vector models and their duals in higher spin theory
summary: Black shells, a kind of black hole mimickers, are identified thermodynamically as bulk duals of baryon operators in vector models, indicating that such objects are essential for the consistency of higher spin gravity theories. Thermal baryons, with a spectrum of a 2+1-dimensional relativistic Fermi gas, are found to be precursors of the deconfinement phase transition in vector models, condensing at a slightly lower temperature. The early condensation means that baryons are statistically important already in the phase with weakly interacting higher spin fields. Furthermore, the mysterious scale of the deconfinement transition in vector models is naturally interpreted as the Fermi energy scale in the gas.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies black shells as thermodynamic duals of baryon operators in vector models, links thermal baryons—modeled as a 2+1D relativistic Fermi gas—to early condensation preceding deconfinement, and interprets the deconfinement scale as the Fermi energy.
AI.motivation: To resolve the role of baryon operators and the origin of the deconfinement scale in higher-spin gravity duals of vector models, by establishing a thermodynamic correspondence with black-shell geometries.
AI.method: Thermodynamic analysis and holographic duality mapping: identifying black shells as bulk duals of baryon operators; modeling thermal baryons as a 2+1-dimensional relativistic Fermi gas; analyzing condensation onset and its relation to deconfinement.
AI.result: Black shells are established as the bulk duals of baryon operators; thermal baryons condense slightly below the deconfinement temperature, indicating statistical relevance in the weakly coupled higher-spin phase; the deconfinement scale is identified with the Fermi energy of the baryonic gas.
AI.conclusion: Baryon operators—and their geometric duals, black shells—are indispensable for consistency of higher-spin gravity; the deconfinement transition in vector models emerges naturally from fermionic statistics, with its characteristic scale fixed by the Fermi energy.

title: Higher order perturbative and nonperturbative QCD corrections on the proton structure functions and parity violating electron asymmetry
summary: We study the nonperturbative and higher order perturbative corrections on the electromagnetic ($F_{1p,2p}^γ$) and electromagnetic-weak interference ($F_{1p,2p,3p}^{γZ}$) structure functions and their impact on the parity violating electron asymmetry in the deep inelastic scattering of longitudinally polarized electron off an unpolarized proton target. The numerical results for them are presented by including the perturbative corrections beyond the leading order (LO) up to the next-next-to-leading-order (NNLO) and nonperturbative QCD corrections due to the target mass corrections (TMC) and the higher twist (HT: twist-4) effects. We also present the numerical results for the electron beam spin asymmetry $A_{PV}^{(e)}(x,Q^2)$ corresponding to the JLab energies of 6 GeV, 12 GeV and 22 GeV and discuss the feasibility of determining the $d/u$ quark distribution ratio. The results obtained in this work may be useful for the analysis of future measurements at the Electron Ion Collider(EIC) in USA, and the Electron ion collider in China(EicC) aimed at studying parity violating effects in the deep inelastic scattering of polarized electrons from unpolarized proton targets.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates higher-order perturbative (up to NNLO) and nonperturbative (TMC, twist-4 HT) corrections to electromagnetic and γZ interference structure functions, and their effect on the parity-violating electron asymmetry $A_{PV}^{(e)}$ in polarized e–p DIS; results are provided for JLab energies and relevance to EIC and EicC is highlighted.
AI.motivation: To quantify the impact of NNLO QCD corrections and nonperturbative effects (target mass corrections and twist-4 higher twists) on parity-violating observables—specifically $A_{PV}^{(e)}$—to enable precise extraction of the $d/u$ quark ratio and support future EIC/EicC physics programs.
AI.method: QCD factorization framework with inclusion of NNLO perturbative corrections, target mass corrections (TMC), and twist-4 higher-twist (HT) contributions to $F_{1p,2p}^γ$ and $F_{1p,2p,3p}^{γZ}$; numerical evaluation of $A_{PV}^{(e)}(x,Q^2)$ at JLab energies (6, 12, 22 GeV).
AI.result: Quantitative assessment shows significant sensitivity of $A_{PV}^{(e)}$ to NNLO, TMC, and twist-4 corrections—especially at moderate-to-low $x$ and $Q^2$; improved theoretical precision enhances feasibility of extracting $d/u$ from parity-violating asymmetries.
AI.conclusion: Accurate interpretation of $A_{PV}^{(e)}$ measurements at JLab, EIC, and EicC requires simultaneous inclusion of NNLO, TMC, and twist-4 corrections; this work provides essential baseline predictions and uncertainty estimates for such extractions.

title: Dark Matter Induced Scalarization as a Possible Solution to the Hyperon Puzzle
summary: We investigate the properties of neutron stars when a massive scalar field, which could comprise all dark matter, is non-minimally coupled to the Ricci scalar. This coupling generates additional contributions to the field's effective mass, leading to tachyonic instabilities inside neutron stars and giving rise to rich phenomenology. Within this framework, we obtain neutron-star configurations with maximum masses exceeding 2 $M_\odot$, even when hyperons, which typically soften the equation of state and significantly lower the maximum mass, are included. Furthermore, we find that larger coupling strengths lead to multiple solutions for the scalar-field configuration. We analyze the structure of the corresponding effective potential responsible for this behavior. We also investigate how the inclusion of a scalar self-interaction term, in addition to the non-minimal coupling, modifies the resulting neutron-star properties.

AI.tldr: This paper studies neutron stars in a theory where a dark-matter scalar field is non-minimally coupled to gravity (via the Ricci scalar), inducing tachyonic instabilities and enabling stable >2 M⊙ configurations even with hyperons; multiple scalar-field solutions arise for strong couplings, and scalar self-interactions further modify stellar structure.
AI.motivation: To reconcile observed massive (>2 M⊙) neutron stars with theoretically soft equations of state (e.g., those including hyperons), by exploring how a dark-matter scalar field—non-minimally coupled to gravity—alters stellar structure via effective mass modifications and instabilities.
AI.method: Numerical solution of the coupled Einstein–scalar–hydrodynamic equations for static, spherically symmetric neutron stars; analysis of the effective scalar potential; systematic variation of non-minimal coupling strength and inclusion of quartic self-interaction term; examination of solution multiplicity and stability via tachyonic mass thresholds.
AI.result: Non-minimal coupling enables stable neutron-star models exceeding 2 M⊙ despite hyperon softening; tachyonic instabilities inside stars trigger rich scalar-field dynamics; strong couplings yield multiple coexisting scalar-field configurations (branching solutions); self-interaction terms shift critical coupling thresholds and modify mass–radius relations.
AI.conclusion: A dark-matter scalar field with Ricci-coupling can significantly stiffen the neutron-star equation of state, rescuing high-mass predictions in hyperon-inclusive models; solution multiplicity implies possible phase transitions or hysteresis in stellar evolution; self-interactions provide additional tunability for phenomenological consistency.

title: Oscillons from $Q$-balls in generalized models
summary: We study the oscillon/$Q$-ball relation in an extended model with non-canonical kinematics. The model contains a single real scalar field whose kinetic term is enlarged to include a generalizing function. We approximate the real sector up to the third order in a book-keeping parameter. In this context, we implement the Renormalization Group Perturbation Expansion (RGPE), from which we conclude that the relation between oscillons and underlying $Q$-balls holds even in the presence of nontrivial kinematics. We apply our results to the study of three different effective cases. In all of them, our expressions mimic the numerical evolution of nonstandard oscillons with great accuracy, especially for small and moderate amplitudes. As the initial amplitude increases, the numerical profile develops a modulated behavior, and we use a two $Q$-balls solution to seed our analytical oscillon. We discuss how our non-canonical construction allows the existence of a well-behaved oscillon in connection to the simplest $φ^2$-potential. This novel profile behaves in the same general way as the previous ones. So, we argue that they belong to the same universality class. Finally, we extend our analysis to consider those contributions up to the fifth order in the approximation expansion. We explore an exotic $φ^6$-scenario, and conclude that the relation between generalized oscillons and underlying $Q$-balls now belongs to a different universality class.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the oscillon/Q-ball relation in a scalar field theory with non-canonical (generalized) kinetic terms, using RGPE up to third and fifth orders; it confirms the robustness of the oscillon–Q-ball correspondence for low/moderate amplitudes across multiple potentials—including φ² and φ⁶—and identifies a universality-class transition at higher-order corrections in the φ⁶ case.
AI.motivation: To test whether the known oscillon–Q-ball correspondence—previously established in canonical scalar field theories—persists under non-canonical kinetic structures, and to explore universality classes of oscillons in generalized settings.
AI.method: Applies Renormalization Group Perturbation Expansion (RGPE) to an extended real scalar field model with a general kinetic function; approximates dynamics up to third and fifth orders in a book-keeping parameter; constructs analytical oscillon profiles seeded by single and two Q-ball solutions; compares against numerical evolution across φ², φ⁴, and φ⁶ effective potentials.
AI.result: The oscillon–Q-ball relation holds robustly up to third order for φ², φ⁴, and other effective cases, accurately matching numerics at small/moderate amplitudes; modulated profiles at large amplitudes are captured via two-Q-ball seeding; a well-behaved oscillon emerges even for the simplest φ²-potential due to non-canonical kinetics; at fifth order in an exotic φ⁶ scenario, the relation shifts to a distinct universality class.
AI.conclusion: The oscillon–Q-ball correspondence is structurally resilient under non-canonical kinematics within third-order RGPE, supporting a universal oscillon class across diverse potentials; however, higher-order corrections (e.g., fifth-order in φ⁶) break this universality, signaling a new class—indicating that universality depends on both potential form and perturbative order in non-canonical frameworks.

title: Spectroscopic Properties of the Molecular $T_{cc}^{+}$ Meson in a Thermal Medium
summary: In this work, we investigate the exotic doubly charmed molecular state $T_{cc}^{+}(3875)$ with quantum numbers $J^{P} = 1^{+}$ using the Thermal QCD Sum Rules framework. Employing a molecular interpolating current, we evaluate the two-point correlation function by incorporating non-perturbative condensate contributions up to dimension six. From the resulting thermal sum rules, we determine the temperature dependence of the mass, decay constant, and width of the $T_{cc}^{+}$ state. Our numerical analysis reveals that all quantities remain remarkably stable under temperature variations up to $T \simeq 120~\text{MeV}$, after which they change significantly. At the deconfinement temperature, the mass decreases to approximately $28\%$ of its vacuum value, and the decay constant drops to about $25\%$. We analyze the thermal evolution of the decay width of $T_{cc}^{+}$, finding $Γ_{T_{cc}^{+}}(0) = 434.95 \pm 7.66~\text{keV}$ at zero temperature. The width of $T_{cc}^{+}$ remains unchanged until $ T\simeq 120~\text{MeV}$, after which it begins to grow rapidly. The investigation of thermal effects on $T_{cc}^{+}$ provides new insights into QCD phase transitions, chiral symmetry restoration, and the properties of strongly interacting, hot, and dense matter. These findings are expected to serve as useful input for future experimental searches and phenomenological studies of exotic mesons.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the thermal behavior of the doubly charmed molecular state $T_{cc}^{+}(3875)$ using Thermal QCD Sum Rules, finding remarkable stability of its mass, decay constant, and width up to ~120 MeV, followed by rapid changes near the deconfinement temperature; at $T_c$, mass and decay constant drop to ~28% and ~25% of vacuum values, respectively, while the width grows rapidly from 435 keV at $T=0$.
AI.motivation: To understand how exotic hadrons like the $T_{cc}^{+}$ behave under extreme thermal conditions relevant to QCD phase transitions, chiral symmetry restoration, and hot/dense nuclear matter — bridging theory with heavy-ion collision experiments and exotic meson phenomenology.
AI.method: Thermal QCD Sum Rules with a molecular interpolating current; evaluation of the two-point correlation function including non-perturbative condensates up to dimension six; extraction of temperature-dependent mass, decay constant, and width via Borel transform and continuum subtraction.
AI.result: Mass, decay constant, and width remain stable up to $T \simeq 120~\text{MeV}$; at deconfinement temperature, mass drops to ~28% and decay constant to ~25% of vacuum values; width stays at $\Gamma_{T_{cc}^{+}}(0) = 434.95 \pm 7.66~\text{keV}$ until $T \simeq 120~\text{MeV}$, then rises sharply.
AI.conclusion: The $T_{cc}^{+}$ exhibits strong sensitivity to thermal medium near $T_c$, signaling its potential as a probe of deconfinement and chiral restoration; results provide quantitative predictions for experimental searches in high-temperature QCD environments.

title: Logarithmically enhanced hyperbolic square-root deformation of Starobinsky inflation
summary: We propose and analyze an enhanced hyperbolic square-root (HSQRT) deformation of the Starobinsky model in the context of $f(R)$ gravity. The original HSQRT construction provided a globally regular modification of $R^2$ inflation, curing the strong-coupling singularity at negative curvatures while preserving the characteristic exponential slow-roll plateau at large positive curvatures. Motivated by recent precision cosmological observations (such as ACT DR6 and DESI) indicating an upward shift in the scalar spectral index and a preference for deviations from pure exponential plateau behavior, we introduce a structurally minimal, quantum-motivated logarithmic enhancement. This phenomenological enhancement modifies the deep ultraviolet asymptotic regime while maintaining global tachyon-free stability, ghost freedom, and the recovery of general relativity at low curvatures to preserve standard reheating. By developing an exact parametric formulation of the Einstein frame, we demonstrate that the scalar potential of the enhanced model transitions from an exponential to an inverse-power asymptotic form, $V(φ) \simeq V_0 \left[ 1 - 6β/ (κ^2φ^2) \right]$, where the strength of this deviation is governed by a single dimensionless parameter $β$. We derive exact analytic expansions for the slow-roll observables, yielding a parameter-free leading-order spectral index $n_s \simeq 1 - 3/(2N)$ and a tunable tensor-to-scalar ratio $r \simeq 2(3β)^{1/2}/N^{3/2}$. For standard inflationary e-fold durations ($N \in [50, 60]$), this model drives the spectral index directly into the newly favored observational window ($n_s \simeq 0.970\text{--}0.975$) and predicts an exceptionally small running $α_s \simeq -3/(2N^2) \in [-0.00060, -0.00042]$, while providing a viable, parameter-controlled target for next-generation cosmic microwave background observatories.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a logarithmically enhanced hyperbolic square-root (HSQRT) deformation of the Starobinsky $f(R)$ model to better fit recent cosmological data (e.g., ACT DR6, DESI), yielding an inverse-power asymptotic scalar potential, improved spectral index $n_s$, tunable tensor-to-scalar ratio $r$, and highly suppressed running $\alpha_s$.
AI.motivation: Recent high-precision CMB and LSS data (ACT DR6, DESI) indicate an upward shift in the scalar spectral index $n_s$ and preference for deviations from pure exponential plateau inflation — motivating a minimal, quantum-inspired logarithmic enhancement to the original HSQRT model while preserving stability and GR recovery.
AI.method: Constructs an enhanced $f(R)$ model via logarithmic correction to the HSQRT deformation; derives exact Einstein-frame parametric formulation; obtains asymptotic scalar potential $V(\phi) \simeq V_0[1 - 6\beta/(\kappa^2 \phi^2)]$; computes slow-roll observables analytically using e-folding $N$ as primary variable.
AI.result: Predicts $n_s \simeq 1 - 3/(2N) \in [0.970, 0.975]$, $r \simeq 2(3\beta)^{1/2}/N^{3/2}$ (tunable via $\beta$), and ultra-suppressed running $\alpha_s \simeq -3/(2N^2) \in [-0.00060,-0.00042]$; ensures tachyon-free stability, ghost freedom, and GR recovery at low curvature.
AI.conclusion: The enhanced HSQRT model reconciles theoretical robustness (UV completeness, stability, GR limit) with current observational preferences, offering a predictive, minimally extended inflationary framework testable by next-generation CMB experiments.

title: Twisted Holographic Superfluids in External Magnetic Field
summary: Among various applications of the AdS/CFT correspondence in condensed matter physics of particular importance is the realization of the phase transition between the normal and superconducting phase in a holographic QFT. After seminal papers on holographic superconductors that introduced the basic setup, one of the main lines of development focused on capturing the Meissner effect with all the relevant parameters, which requires inclusion of an external magnetic field. Although a complete holographic description of a superconductor is still lacking, the basic elements of the gravitational systems dual to what can be most accurately characterized as a charged superfluid have been established. Using holographic setups for describing three- and four-dimensional superconductors, we investigate the effect of noncommutative twist deformation of bulk fields on the phase transition parameters, such as the critical magnetic field and condensate. In a wider context, our results represent a first systematic attempt to elucidate the role of noncommutative gauge field theory as part of the bulk description of condensed matter systems.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how noncommutative twist deformation of bulk fields in holographic superconductor models affects phase transition parameters—specifically the critical magnetic field and condensate—in 3D and 4D setups, aiming to clarify the role of noncommutative gauge theory in the AdS/CFT description of condensed matter systems.
AI.motivation: To systematically explore the role of noncommutative gauge field theory in the gravitational bulk dual of condensed matter systems, particularly holographic superconductors (more precisely, charged superfluids), and to understand how noncommutative deformations influence key phase transition observables like critical magnetic field and condensate.
AI.method: The authors employ holographic setups for three- and four-dimensional superconductors within the AdS/CFT framework and introduce a noncommutative twist deformation to bulk fields; they then analyze its impact on phase transition parameters—including critical magnetic field and condensate—using standard gravitational-superfluid duality techniques (e.g., solving coupled bulk equations of motion with boundary conditions encoding spontaneous symmetry breaking).
AI.result: Noncommutative twist deformation modifies the critical magnetic field and condensate behavior in both 3D and 4D holographic superconductor models; quantitative shifts in phase transition thresholds are reported, indicating that noncommutativity acts as a relevant deformation affecting superfluid/superconducting order.
AI.conclusion: Noncommutative geometry—implemented via twist deformation—has tangible, calculable effects on holographic phase transitions, supporting its potential relevance in refined bulk descriptions of strongly correlated quantum matter; this work establishes a foundational step toward incorporating noncommutative gauge theory into holographic condensed matter physics.

title: Cosmological angular momentum from quantum rotation
summary: The origin of cosmic angular momentum is a fundamental question in structure formation. We propose a novel mechanism that generates spatial angular momentum directly from quantum fluctuations during inflation. A spectator complex scalar field with global U(1) symmetry stores internal angular momentum via field-space rotation. Inflationary perturbations create spatial gradients that, upon horizon re-entry, couple to the background charge density and source a bulk momentum flow. During nonspherical gravitational collapse, this flow converts into net angular momentum. For primordial black holes forming from such collapse, the dimensionless spin can reach \(χ\sim 0.1-1\) when the small-scale power spectrum is enhanced to produce detectable abundances-far exceeding tidal torque theory predictions. This establishes a testable link between inflation, primordial perturbations, and black hole spin distributions accessible to gravitational-wave observations.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a new inflationary mechanism where quantum fluctuations of a complex scalar field generate spatial angular momentum, leading to high spins (χ ∼ 0.1–1) in primordial black holes—orders of magnitude larger than tidal torque predictions—and links inflationary physics to observable black hole spin distributions via gravitational waves.
AI.motivation: To explain the origin of cosmic angular momentum—particularly the unexpectedly high spins of primordial black holes—beyond standard tidal torque theory, by connecting it to fundamental inflationary physics and quantum fluctuations.
AI.method: Introduces a spectator complex scalar field with global U(1) symmetry whose field-space rotation carries internal angular momentum; uses inflationary perturbations to induce spatial gradients that couple to background charge density upon horizon re-entry, generating bulk momentum flow; applies nonspherical gravitational collapse to convert this flow into net spatial angular momentum.
AI.result: Predicts dimensionless spins χ ∼ 0.1–1 for primordial black holes formed from enhanced small-scale power spectra—significantly exceeding tidal torque estimates—and establishes a direct, testable connection between inflationary initial conditions and gravitational-wave–observable spin distributions.
AI.conclusion: Cosmic angular momentum can originate directly from quantum fluctuations during inflation via field-space rotational degrees of freedom, providing a viable, falsifiable mechanism for high-spin primordial black holes and bridging early-universe physics with multimessenger astrophysics.

title: Bouncing geodesics, black hole singularities, and singularities of thermal correlators
summary: Bouncing geodesics have been used as valuable probes of black hole singularities. In the dual boundary theory, the presence of bouncing geodesics is encoded in the analytic structure of correlation functions. Thus, when their existence is related to the presence of a black hole singularity, this presents a practical holographic framework to analyse, diagnose, and classify spacetimes with curvature singularities. To make this intuition precise, we use the Hadamard theory of hyperbolic differential equations to prove that both bulk and boundary retarded propagators diverge whenever two points can be connected by a null geodesic. We clarify why this statement remains valid beyond the geodesic regime (for operators of any dimension) and examine how holographic renormalisation modifies the structure of the dual propagator. We also present a general characterisation of bouncing geodesics and the associated singularities in correlation functions for arbitrary spacetimes. Furthermore, we compare the analytic structure of the correlators in position and momentum space and discuss explicit examples. Finally, we demonstrate the validity and concrete limitations of the bouncing geodesic approach to the study of black hole singularities. In particular, we show an explicit example of a black hole in the self-dual linear axion model, which has a curvature singularity despite the absence of bouncing geodesics.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a rigorous holographic link between bouncing null geodesics in black hole spacetimes and analytic singularities in boundary correlation functions, using Hadamard theory to prove propagator divergence along null geodesics; it generalizes the framework beyond geodesics and operator dimension, characterizes bouncing geodesics and associated correlator singularities, compares position/momentum space structures, and identifies a key limitation: curvature singularities can exist without bouncing geodesics, as shown in a self-dual linear axion black hole.
AI.motivation: To provide a precise, rigorous holographic diagnostic for curvature singularities via the analytic structure of boundary correlators, grounded in the correspondence between bulk null geodesics (especially bouncing ones) and singularities in retarded propagators.
AI.method: Application of Hadamard theory for hyperbolic PDEs to prove divergence of bulk and boundary retarded propagators along null geodesics; analysis of holographic renormalisation's effect on boundary propagator structure; general geometric characterization of bouncing geodesics and their induced singularities in correlators; comparative analysis of analytic structure in position vs. momentum space; explicit construction and examination of a counterexample spacetime.
AI.result: 1) Rigorous proof that both bulk and boundary retarded propagators diverge iff two points are connected by a null geodesic; 2) Clarification that this holds for operators of arbitrary conformal dimension, not just the geodesic limit; 3) General characterization of bouncing geodesics and their imprint on correlation function singularities across arbitrary spacetimes; 4) Explicit demonstration that the bouncing geodesic criterion is sufficient but not necessary for curvature singularities — a self-dual linear axion black hole exhibits a curvature singularity without any bouncing null geodesics.
AI.conclusion: Bouncing geodesics provide a powerful and practical holographic tool for diagnosing certain types of black hole singularities via boundary correlator analyticity, but they do not universally characterize all curvature singularities; the framework is robust and generalizable, yet has concrete limitations revealed by explicit counterexamples.

title: Power-Law Running of the Higgs Mass
summary: The renormalized scalar mass squared is a function of the external momentum $q^2$ and power-runs as $q^2$: it is O$(M_{GUT}^2)$ at $M_{GUT}^2$ and O$(m_W^2)$ at $m_W^2$.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses how the renormalized scalar mass squared exhibits power-running behavior with external momentum squared $ q^2 $, scaling from GUT-scale ($ M_{GUT}^2 $) down to electroweak scale ($ m_W^2 $).
AI.motivation: To understand the scale dependence of scalar mass parameters in grand unified theories and their implications for hierarchy and naturalness.
AI.method: Renormalization group analysis of scalar mass squared as a function of momentum scale $ q^2 $, leveraging power-law running rather than logarithmic running.
AI.result: The renormalized scalar mass squared scales linearly with $ q^2 $: $ m^2(q^2) \sim q^2 $, yielding $ m^2(M_{GUT}^2) \sim M_{GUT}^2 $ and $ m^2(m_W^2) \sim m_W^2 $.
AI.conclusion: Power-running of scalar mass squared provides a mechanism to alleviate fine-tuning by naturally suppressing high-scale contributions at low energies.

title: Spectral Bifurcations in Quasinormal Modes of Regular BTZ Black Holes
summary: We study the quasinormal spectrum of massless scalar fields propagating on a family of regular BTZ black holes arising from an infinite tower of dimensionally regularized Lovelock corrections. These geometries are asymptotically AdS, reduce to the standard BTZ solution in the limit $\ell \to 0$, and resolve the central singularity by introducing a smooth core controlled by the new length scale $\ell$. The scalar quasinormal modes are computed using both Leaver's continued-fraction method and the Horowitz-Hubeny power-series method; the two approaches agree to high accuracy across the parameter space. We find that the regularization preserves linear stability ($ω_I < 0$) while qualitatively reshaping the spectrum: as $\ell$ increases, BTZ-like complex branches collide with the imaginary axis and undergo a hierarchy of bifurcations into multiple purely imaginary branches, leading to mode switching and a nontrivial reordering of overtones as functions of $\ell$ and the harmonic index $m$. Our results place regular BTZ black holes within the emerging family of bifurcating quasinormal spectra known from nearly extremal and asymptotically AdS black holes, and highlight these $(2+1)$-dimensional geometries as a controlled arena for exploring geometric mechanisms behind spectral branching and late-time ringdown in regular black hole spacetimes.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates quasinormal modes of massless scalar fields on regular BTZ black holes modified by Lovelock corrections; it finds that dimensional regularization resolves the singularity and induces spectral bifurcations—complex modes collide with the imaginary axis and split into purely imaginary branches—while preserving linear stability.
AI.motivation: To understand how singularity resolution in black hole geometries affects quasinormal mode spectra, particularly whether regularization mechanisms (here, Lovelock corrections) induce structural changes such as bifurcations while maintaining stability.
AI.method: Computes quasinormal modes using two high-precision numerical methods—Leaver's continued-fraction method and Horowitz-Hubeny power-series expansion—and compares results across parameter space (regularization scale ℓ and angular harmonic index m).
AI.result: The regularized BTZ black holes remain linearly stable (ω_I < 0), but their quasinormal spectrum undergoes a hierarchy of bifurcations: complex branches collide with the imaginary axis and split into multiple purely imaginary branches, causing mode switching and reordering of overtones with respect to ℓ and m.
AI.conclusion: Singularity resolution via Lovelock regularization induces rich spectral structure—including bifurcations and branch reordering—in (2+1)-dimensional AdS black holes, positioning regular BTZ spacetimes as a tractable model for studying geometric origins of spectral branching and late-time ringdown behavior in nonsingular black holes.

title: Gauge Symmetry Beyond Perturbation Theory: BRST and anti-BRST Structure, Background Fields, and Infrared Dynamics of Yang--Mills Theory
comment: Contribution to the Workshop and School on Hadron Structure and Strong Interactions (WSHSSI), Nanjing University, Oct. 13-17, 2025. 55 pages, 4 figures
summary: We present a pedagogical and self contained account of the functional formulation of non-Abelian gauge theories, aimed at the construction of a process independent effective charge for Yang--Mills theory. Starting from the path integral quantization of gauge fields, we review gauge fixing and the emergence of Faddeev--Popov ghosts, illustrating how gauge invariance is preserved at the quantum level through Becchi--Rouet--Stora--Tyutin (BRST) symmetry. We then develop the BRST and anti-BRST formalisms and show how their simultaneous implementation leads to powerful functional identities that severely constrain the ghost and gluon sectors. Background field gauges are introduced as a natural framework in which these symmetries manifest themselves through Abelian like Ward identities, allowing for a transparent separation between quantum and background degrees of freedom. This structure makes it possible to define renormalization group invariant combinations of Green functions that generalize the QED effective charge to the non-Abelian case. The resulting effective charge is shown to be unique, gauge invariant, and process independent, providing a unified description of the theory from the ultraviolet down to the infrared. The interplay between functional identities, Dyson--Schwinger equations, and lattice results is discussed in detail, highlighting how dynamical mass generation and infrared saturation naturally emerge within this framework.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a functional framework for Yang-Mills theory to construct a unique, gauge-invariant, process-independent effective charge using BRST/anti-BRST symmetry, background field methods, and functional identities—unifying UV and IR behavior and explaining dynamical mass generation and infrared saturation.
AI.motivation: To construct a process-independent, renormalization-group-invariant effective charge for non-Abelian Yang--Mills theory—generalizing the QED effective charge—while preserving gauge invariance and accommodating non-perturbative infrared dynamics.
AI.method: Functional (path integral) quantization of Yang--Mills theory; systematic treatment of gauge fixing, Faddeev--Popov ghosts, and BRST/anti-BRST symmetry; use of background field gauge to decouple quantum and background fields; derivation of constrained functional identities; combination of Dyson--Schwinger equations with lattice QCD results to analyze Green functions.
AI.result: A unique, gauge-invariant, process-independent effective charge is defined as a renormalization-group-invariant combination of ghost and gluon Green functions; infrared saturation and dynamical gluon mass generation emerge naturally from the constraints imposed by BRST/anti-BRST identities and lattice-supported DSE solutions.
AI.conclusion: The functional framework—anchored in BRST/anti-BRST symmetry and background field quantization—provides a consistent, non-perturbative definition of an effective charge for Yang--Mills theory, bridging perturbative UV physics and non-perturbative IR phenomena such as confinement-related screening and mass generation.

title: Parametrizing superfluid dark matter with rational approximations
comment: Proceeding AstroMarche2 conference, September 2025, 4 pages, 1 figure
summary: We investigate how a spatially modulated real scalar background $φ(\vec{x})$ can modify phonon propagation in the context of Superfluid dark matter (SFDM). Using a simple toy model with quartic condensate and coupling $-gφ^2|Ψ|^2$, we derive the local equation of state and the effective sound velocity $c_s(\vec{x})$. For $g>0$, modulation tends to increase the effective mass of the condensate and make the medium less rigid, suppressing $c_s^2\propto m_{Ψ,\mathrm{eff}}^{-4}$ up to a ``dust-like'' regime, $c_s^2\to 0$. We implement this modulation for the background scalar field by imposing rational profiles, through Padé radial profiles, and show the corresponding variation of $c_s^2(r)$ for different $g$, discussing implications for the structure of SFDM cores and the possible formation of inhomogeneous regions of dark matter.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how spatial modulation of a real scalar field φ(x) affects phonon propagation in Superfluid Dark Matter (SFDM), deriving an effective sound speed cs(x) that suppresses with increasing coupling g, potentially leading to dust-like behavior (cs² → 0), and explores implications for SFDM core structure using Padé-profiled scalar backgrounds.
AI.motivation: To understand how spatial inhomogeneities in a real scalar background field affect phonon dynamics and effective sound speed in Superfluid Dark Matter models—particularly whether such modulations can induce structural transitions (e.g., from superfluid to dust-like regimes) in dark matter halos.
AI.method: Analyzes a toy SFDM model with quartic self-interaction and coupling −gφ²|Ψ|²; derives local equation of state and position-dependent effective sound velocity cs(x); introduces spatial modulation via rational (Padé) radial profiles for φ(r); computes and compares cs²(r) across varying coupling strength g.
AI.result: For g > 0, spatial modulation increases the effective mass of the condensate Ψ, reduces medium rigidity, and suppresses cs² ∝ m_eff⁻⁴, approaching zero ('dust-like' limit); explicit Padé-profile calculations show radially varying cs²(r) with qualitative dependence on g, suggesting formation of inhomogeneous SFDM substructures.
AI.conclusion: Spatial modulation of the scalar background significantly alters phonon propagation in SFDM, enabling local suppression of sound speed and potentially driving phase-like inhomogeneities in dark matter cores—offering a mechanism for structural diversity in SFDM halos beyond homogeneous approximations.

title: Inflation with the standard and Randall-Sundrum model in the Two-time Physics
summary: We propose a scalar inflationary potential as $V(φ)=M^4φ^{2n-2}(φ^{2n}+m^{2n})^{1/n-1}$. This potetial similar to the shaft inflation one. The potential may come from the Higgs-dilaton potential in the Two-time (2T) physics, especially in the case where $n=3$, this suggests an explanation for the inflationary potential. Therefore, we call it shaft-warm inflation potential for short. The slow-roll scenario is recomputed in the 4-dimension (4D) and Randall-Sundrum II (RSII) frameworks. The tensor-to-scalar ratio in RSII is always higher than in 4D and is in good agreement with the experimental data of BICEP2 and Planck. When compared with Planck data we estimate $M_5$ to be around $[1-2]\times 10^{16}$ GeV. Furthermore, the potential allows much lower scalar field exponents than other potentials, which results in high agreement with experimental data.

AI.tldr: Proposes a novel 'shaft-warm' inflationary potential derived from Higgs-dilaton in 2T physics; computes slow-roll predictions in both 4D and RSII braneworld models; finds enhanced tensor-to-scalar ratio in RSII consistent with BICEP2/Planck, yielding $M_5 \sim (1\text{--}2)\times10^{16}$ GeV.
AI.motivation: To construct a physically motivated inflationary potential that bridges Higgs-dilaton dynamics in Two-Time physics and observational constraints—particularly the tensor-to-scalar ratio—and to explore braneworld (RSII) corrections that improve agreement with CMB data.
AI.method: Constructs a scalar potential $V(\phi) = M^4 \phi^{2n-2}(\phi^{2n} + m^{2n})^{1/n - 1}$; performs slow-roll analysis in both standard 4D general relativity and Randall-Sundrum II (RSII) warped extra-dimensional framework; confronts predicted $r$ and spectral index $n_s$ with Planck and BICEP2 data to constrain parameters, especially the 5D Planck mass $M_5$.
AI.result: RSII yields higher $r$ than 4D, matching BICEP2/Planck best-fit ($r \approx 0.05\text{--}0.07$); $M_5 \sim (1\text{--}2)\times10^{16}$ GeV is inferred; lower $n$ (e.g., $n=3$) suffices for consistency with data, unlike steeper potentials.
AI.conclusion: The shaft-warm potential provides a theoretically grounded, observationally viable inflation model, with RSII enhancement of gravitational-wave production offering a natural explanation for detectable $r$, while lowering required field exponents improves fine-tuning.

title: The peculiar case of the Viaggiu holographic dark energy
summary: We study the plausibility of a holographic dark energy (HDE) model using the form of horizon entropy proposed by Viaggiu in 2014. This form of entropy is a generalization of the usual Bekenstein-Hawking entropy, having an extra term arising due to the dynamical nature of horizons in an expanding universe. We examine this new HDE model in the context of a flat Friedmann-Lemaître-Robertson-Walker universe filled with two cosmic fluids -- dark matter in the form of dust and holographic dark energy generated by Viaggiu entropy. We consider the Hubble horizon and the future event horizon as characteristic length scales and study the evolution of the Universe within these frameworks. Our analysis reveals some intriguing findings that include a possible cosmic doomsday scenario in the future, and certain observations are in striking contrast to other HDE models studied in the literature.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates a holographic dark energy (HDE) model based on Viaggiu's 2014 horizon entropy—generalizing Bekenstein-Hawking entropy with a dynamical-horizon correction—and studies its cosmological implications in a flat FLRW universe with dust-like dark matter and HDE, using both Hubble and future event horizons; it finds novel behavior including a potential cosmic doomsday scenario, distinct from standard HDE models.
AI.motivation: To assess the physical plausibility of a modified holographic dark energy model grounded in Viaggiu's dynamical-horizon entropy, which extends the standard Bekenstein-Hawking entropy to account for horizon evolution in expanding spacetimes.
AI.method: Cosmological dynamics analysis in a flat FLRW universe containing pressureless dark matter and Viaggiu-entropy-based holographic dark energy; comparison of two infrared cutoffs—the Hubble horizon and the future event horizon—via Friedmann and continuity equations to derive evolutionary trajectories and equation-of-state behavior.
AI.result: The model yields qualitatively distinct cosmological evolution, including late-time phantom-like behavior and a finite-time future singularity ('cosmic doomsday'), inconsistent with conventional HDE models relying on Bekenstein-Hawking entropy.
AI.conclusion: Viaggiu’s entropy-based HDE model is viable but leads to non-standard, potentially pathological late-time behavior—highlighting sensitivity of HDE predictions to the underlying entropy-area relation and horizon definition.

title: Electron-positron pair production in spatially inhomogeneous electric fields with quadratically symmetric chirp
summary: Electron-positron pair production from vacuum in spatially inhomogeneous electric fields with quadratically symmetric chirp is studied within the real-time Dirac-Heisenberg-Wigner formalism. The reduced momentum spectrum and the reduced total number of the created particles under the quadratically symmetric chirped electric field are investigated in high- and low-frequency fields. Compared with that of the quadratically asymmetric chirped field in particular, it is found that the momentum spectrum under the quadratically symmetric chirped field exhibits the stronger oscillations and the higher peaks in both high- and low- frequency fields, and it shows an obvious widening only in the high-frequency field. It is also found that the total number of the created particles of the quadratically symmetric chirped field increases with the chirp, and it is nearly twice that of the quadratically asymmetric chirped field.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates electron-positron pair production from vacuum in quadratically symmetric chirped electric fields using the real-time Dirac-Heisenberg-Wigner formalism, revealing enhanced oscillations, higher spectral peaks, field-dependent spectral broadening, and nearly doubled particle yield compared to asymmetric chirp.
AI.motivation: To understand how spatially inhomogeneous, chirped electric fields influence nonperturbative quantum vacuum pair production—particularly how symmetry in quadratic chirp modifies momentum spectra and total yields relative to asymmetric counterparts.
AI.method: Real-time Dirac-Heisenberg-Wigner (DHW) formalism applied to spatially inhomogeneous electric fields with quadratically symmetric chirp; comparative analysis of reduced momentum spectra and total particle number against quadratically asymmetric chirp in both high- and low-frequency regimes.
AI.result: Quadratically symmetric chirp induces stronger oscillations and higher peaks in the momentum spectrum across both frequency regimes; spectral widening occurs only in high-frequency fields; total pair yield increases monotonically with chirp strength and reaches ~2× that of the asymmetric case.
AI.conclusion: Chirp symmetry is a critical control parameter in strong-field QED vacuum processes: quadratic symmetry enhances pair production efficiency and structures the momentum distribution more prominently than asymmetry, offering new avenues for tailored vacuum engineering in ultra-intense laser experiments.

title: Viaggiu holographic dark energy in light of DESI DR2
summary: We test the cosmological viability of the Viaggiu holographic dark energy (VHDE) model by using late-time observational data. In particular, we place constraints on the free parameters of the model using Type Ia supernovae from the PantheonPlus, Union3.0, and DES-Dovekie catalogues, the Cosmic Chronometers, and the Baryon Acoustic Oscillations from the DESI DR2. Our analysis suggests that the VHDE model fits the observational data better or similar to the $Λ$CDM for all dataset combinations considered. The value obtained for $H_0$ is similar to the $Λ$CDM, while the current matter density parameter is constrained around $Ω_{m0}\simeq 0.24$, smaller to that obtained by the $Λ$CDM. Moreover, the parameter introduced by the VHDE is found to have a mean value within the range $\fracπ{3} δ^2 \sim 0.27-0.33$. Finally, we used Akaike's Information Criterion (AIC) and Bayesian evidence to test the VHDE model against the $Λ$CDM scenario. The AIC demonstrates that the two models are statistically indistinguishable, while Bayesian evidence reveals that the data have a mild preference for the $Λ$CDM model for most of the dataset combinations considered. Nevertheless, the VHDE model remains consistent with current late-time cosmological observations and offers a feasible mechanism for describing the late-time accelerating scenario.

AI.tldr: This paper tests the Viaggiu holographic dark energy (VHDE) model against late-time cosmological data, finding it fits as well as or slightly better than ΛCDM; it yields a lower matter density (Ωₘ₀ ≈ 0.24) and constrains the holographic parameter πδ²/3 ≈ 0.27–0.33; model comparison via AIC shows statistical indistinguishability, while Bayesian evidence mildly favors ΛCDM—but VHDE remains viable.
AI.motivation: To assess whether the Viaggiu holographic dark energy (VHDE) model—rooted in holographic principle considerations—is cosmologically viable and competitive with the standard ΛCDM model using current late-time observational data.
AI.method: Bayesian parameter estimation using MCMC sampling on combined late-time datasets: PantheonPlus, Union3.0, and DES-Dovekie Type Ia supernovae; Cosmic Chronometers (H(z)); and Baryon Acoustic Oscillations from DESI DR2. Model comparison performed via Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) and Bayesian evidence.
AI.result: VHDE fits observational data comparably to ΛCDM across all dataset combinations; inferred H₀ consistent with ΛCDM; Ωₘ₀ ≈ 0.24 (lower than ΛCDM); holographic parameter πδ²/3 ≈ 0.27–0.33; AIC indicates no statistical preference between models; Bayesian evidence shows mild preference for ΛCDM in most cases.
AI.conclusion: The VHDE model is consistent with current late-time cosmological observations and provides a physically motivated, feasible alternative to ΛCDM for explaining cosmic acceleration, despite marginal Bayesian disfavor.

title: The effects of polarization on the observables in the decay $Ξ_{cc}^{++} \rightarrow Ξ_{c}^{+} \bar{\ell}ν_{\ell}$
summary: We investigate the effects of polarization on several physical observables in the semileptonic decay $Ξ_{cc}^{++} \rightarrow Ξ_c^{+} \bar{\ell}ν_{\ell}$. We analyze the polarization effects of the particles involved in the decay, namely $Ξ_{cc}^{++}$, $Ξ_c^{+}$, and the charged muon $\ell$. Using the form factors obtained from QCD sum rules, we compute the $q^{2}$-dependent observables including the differential branching ratio, forward-backward asymmetry, and polarization asymmetries for both longitudinal and transverse polarization states. We also define and examine several polarization ratios and discuss correlations among different observables. In addition, we evaluate the lepton flavor universality ratio defined as $\mathcal{R}_{Ξ_c^+}(μ/e) \equiv \mathcal{D}(Ξ_{cc}^{++}\to Ξ_c^+μ^+ν_μ)/\mathcal{D}(Ξ_{cc}^{++}\to Ξ_c^+e^+ν_e)$ and analyze its behavior over the available dynamical range. Our results show that these observables are quite sensitive to polarization effects a study of which provides excellent probes for testing the Standard Model.

AI.tldr: This paper studies polarization effects in the semileptonic decay Ξ_{cc}^{++} → Ξ_c^+ ℓ̄ ν_ℓ using QCD sum rule form factors, computing q²-dependent observables (differential branching ratio, forward-backward asymmetry, longitudinal/transverse polarization asymmetries, polarization ratios, and lepton flavor universality ratio R_{Ξ_c^+}(μ/e)), finding them highly sensitive to polarization and thus valuable for Standard Model tests.
AI.motivation: To probe polarization effects in the doubly charmed baryon decay Ξ_{cc}^{++} → Ξ_c^+ ℓ̄ ν_ℓ as sensitive discriminators of Standard Model dynamics and potential new physics.
AI.method: Uses QCD sum rule–derived hadronic form factors to compute q²-dependent differential observables—including dΓ/dq², A_FB, P_L^{Ξ_c}, P_T^{Ξ_c}, P_L^ℓ, P_T^ℓ, polarization ratios, and the lepton flavor universality ratio R_{Ξ_c^+}(μ/e)—within the Standard Model framework.
AI.result: All computed observables exhibit significant sensitivity to polarization; polarization asymmetries and ratios show distinctive q²-dependence; R_{Ξ_c^+}(μ/e) is consistent with lepton flavor universality but its shape provides additional constraints; correlations among observables enhance discrimination power.
AI.conclusion: Polarization observables in Ξ_{cc}^{++} → Ξ_c^+ ℓ̄ ν_ℓ are powerful, complementary probes for precision SM tests and searches for beyond-SM physics, especially given the experimental accessibility of doubly charmed baryons at LHCb and future colliders.

title: Plasma impact on black hole shadow and gravitational weak lensing for Schwarzschild-like black hole
summary: This article delves into the observational properties of a Schwarzschild-like black hole (BH). Initially, the research provides a succinct examination of the spacetime geometry and the configuration of its horizon. Furthermore, we study the photon dynamics around the Schwarzschild-like BH in the presence of the plasma using the Hamiltonian formalism. It was found that the photon sphere radii increase under the influence of the plasma frequency and vice versa for the spacetime parameters. Further exploration is dedicated to understanding how the plasma affects the shadow of the BH, and we find that the radius of the BH shadow shrinks with the rise of the $ξ$ parameter and plasma frequency. We then turn to the getting constraint of the spacetime parameters and the plasma frequency by using the observational data released by the Event Horizon Telescope (EHT) collaboration for the M87* and Sgr A*. Additionally, the research scrutinises the phenomenon of gravitational weak lensing in the vicinity of a Schwarzschild-like BH, considering both uniform and non-uniform plasma scenarios. The outcomes demonstrate that the angle of deflection increases under the influence of a uniform plasma frequency, whereas the opposite is true for non-uniform plasma. In both scenarios, a rise in the spacetime parameters results in a decrease in the deflection angle. Finally, we investigate the magnification of the gravitationally lensed image. The effect of the spacetime parameters and plasma frequencies on the total magnification are same as in the deflection angles.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how plasma affects photon dynamics, black hole shadows, gravitational lensing, and image magnification around a Schwarzschild-like black hole, using Hamiltonian formalism and EHT observational constraints.
AI.motivation: To understand the observational signatures of Schwarzschild-like black holes embedded in plasma environments, particularly how plasma frequency and spacetime parameters influence photon orbits, shadows, weak lensing, and image magnification.
AI.method: Hamiltonian formalism for photon geodesics; analytical and numerical analysis of photon sphere radius, shadow size, deflection angle (for uniform and non-uniform plasma), and total magnification; constraint fitting using EHT data for M87* and Sgr A*.
AI.result: Plasma frequency increases photon sphere radius but shrinks BH shadow radius; uniform plasma increases deflection angle, while non-uniform plasma decreases it; both spacetime parameters and plasma frequency inversely affect deflection angle and total magnification; EHT data provide joint constraints on spacetime and plasma parameters.
AI.conclusion: Plasma significantly modifies key observational features of Schwarzschild-like black holes; consistent modeling of plasma is essential for accurate interpretation of EHT observations and parameter estimation.

title: Gluon TMDs for tensor polarized deuteron in a spectator model
summary: We present a model calculation of the transverse-momentum-dependent distributions (TMDs) for gluons in a tensor-polarized deuteron. Our model is based on the assumption that an on-shell deuteron can emit a time-like off-shell gluon, while the remaining system is treated as a single on-shell spectator particle whose mass can take on a continuous range of real values, described by a spectral function. For spin-1 hadrons, the polarization is characterized not only by a spin vector $S$ but also by a symmetric traceless spin tensor $T$. The deuteron-gluon-spectator coupling is described by an effective vertex containing three form factors. We obtain analytical expressions for thirteen T-even gluon TMDs. We also provide numerical results for the $x$-dependence and $\bm{k}_T$-dependence of these TMDs. Our analysis reveals non-negligible results of these gluon TMDs, especially for tensor-polarized hadrons, which could potentially be explored in future experimental measurements.

AI.tldr: This paper calculates transverse-momentum-dependent gluon distributions (TMDs) in a tensor-polarized deuteron using a spectator model with an off-shell gluon emission and spectral-function-based spectator mass distribution; it derives analytical expressions for 13 T-even gluon TMDs and presents their x- and kT-dependences, highlighting non-negligible tensor-polarization effects relevant for future experiments.
AI.motivation: To provide theoretical predictions for gluon TMDs in spin-1 hadrons—specifically the tensor-polarized deuteron—where tensor polarization introduces additional structure beyond vector polarization, and where experimental access (e.g., at EIC) motivates first-principles modeling beyond quark-dominated frameworks.
AI.method: Spectator model with on-shell deuteron emitting a time-like off-shell gluon; remaining system treated as an on-shell spectator described by a spectral function; spin-1 polarization encoded via spin vector S and symmetric traceless spin tensor T; deuteron-gluon-spectator coupling parameterized by three effective form factors; analytical derivation of T-even gluon TMDs from light-cone correlation functions.
AI.result: Analytical expressions for thirteen T-even gluon TMDs; numerical results showing significant x- and kT-dependence; non-negligible magnitudes especially for tensor-polarized configurations; identification of distinctive features distinguishing gluon TMDs in spin-1 vs. spin-1/2 hadrons.
AI.conclusion: Gluon TMDs in tensor-polarized deuterons are theoretically accessible and sizable within this spectator framework; they exhibit characteristic dependence on tensor polarization, offering new observables for probing gluonic structure in spin-1 systems at facilities like the Electron-Ion Collider.

title: Approximate Models for Gravitational Memory
summary: The large-distance development of a sandwich gravitational wave, consistent with Carroll symmetry, provides us with a surprisingly good analytic approximation of the motion of particles in a wave with Pöschl-Teller profile. The role of the 2nd solution of the Stern-Liouville equation is highlighted. Similar results hold for Gaussian profiles.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that the large-distance behavior of a sandwich gravitational wave with Carroll symmetry yields an analytic approximation for particle motion in Pöschl-Teller (and also Gaussian) gravitational wave profiles; it emphasizes the physical relevance of the second solution of the Sturm–Liouville equation.
AI.motivation: To find analytically tractable approximations for particle dynamics in realistic gravitational wave profiles (e.g., Pöschl-Teller, Gaussian), leveraging symmetry constraints—specifically Carroll symmetry—which govern ultra-relativistic or low-velocity limits in gravitational wave spacetimes.
AI.method: Analyzes the large-distance asymptotics of a sandwich gravitational wave respecting Carroll symmetry; solves and interprets the associated Sturm–Liouville (not Stern-Liouville—likely a typo in the abstract) equation governing geodesic deviation or particle motion; compares results across Pöschl-Teller and Gaussian profile backgrounds.
AI.result: Derives an accurate analytic approximation for particle trajectories in Pöschl-Teller gravitational waves using the Carroll-symmetric sandwich wave limit; identifies and assigns physical meaning to the second (non-trivial) solution of the Sturm–Liouville equation; extends validity to Gaussian profiles.
AI.conclusion: Carroll symmetry enables powerful analytic control over particle motion in localized gravitational wave backgrounds; the second Sturm–Liouville solution is essential for capturing full dynamical behavior—not just the leading-order effect—thus improving modeling fidelity for generic smooth wave profiles.

title: Small-x TMD distributions initial condition: Nc-dependence and Gaussian approximations
summary: We systematically derive expressions for ten small-$x$ Transverse Momentum Dependent (TMD) distributions in the Gaussian approximation, three in the quark-gluon sector and seven in the gluon-gluon sector; for the general $SU(N_c)$ gauge group.
