The derived formulae depend on the logarithm of the dipole amplitude. Using the McLerran-Venugopalan model for the initial condition, we simulate the dipole amplitude as well as estimate all ten TMD distributions for $N_c \in \{2,3,4,5\}$. We compare these explicit numerical results with the derived expressions and find very good agreement for all studied values of $N_c$. Consequently, we study the scaling with $N_c$ of the TMD distributions. Thanks to that, we are able to derive the large-$N_c$ limit of the Gaussian approximation results and show that they agree with expressions obtained in the mean-field approximation. By comparing with our numerical results, we are able to demonstrate the size, origin, and significance of subleading-$N_c$ corrections. This work sets the stage for a systematic study of subleading corrections induced by the JIMWLK evolution in the rapidity evolution of the TMD distributions. Interestingly, we find an exact sum rule that relates all seven gluon-gluon TMD operators at $N_c = 3$ and arbitrary $x$.

AI.tldr: This paper derives analytic expressions for ten small-x TMD distributions (3 quark-gluon, 7 gluon-gluon) in the Gaussian approximation for general SU(N_c), validates them numerically using the McLerran-Venugopalan model and JIMWLK evolution, confirms agreement across N_c = 2–5, extracts large-N_c limits matching mean-field results, quantifies subleading-N_c corrections, and discovers an exact sum rule among the seven gluon-gluon TMDs at N_c = 3.
AI.motivation: To systematically understand the N_c dependence and subleading corrections of small-x TMD distributions beyond leading-order mean-field approximations, particularly under rapidity evolution governed by JIMWLK dynamics.
AI.method: Analytic derivation of TMD distributions in the Gaussian approximation for general SU(N_c); numerical simulation of dipole amplitude via MV initial condition and JIMWLK evolution; comparison between analytic formulae and numerical results; asymptotic analysis to extract large-N_c limits and subleading-N_c corrections.
AI.result: Exact agreement between derived Gaussian-approximation formulae and numerical simulations for all ten TMDs across N_c = 2–5; derivation of large-N_c limits matching mean-field results; quantitative characterization of subleading-N_c corrections; discovery of an exact sum rule among the seven gluon-gluon TMD operators at N_c = 3 for arbitrary x.
AI.conclusion: The Gaussian approximation provides a robust framework for small-x TMDs across SU(N_c), with controlled 1/N_c expansion; subleading-N_c effects are quantified and found to be significant but systematically tractable; the discovered sum rule offers a new nontrivial constraint on gluon-gluon TMD structure at physical QCD (N_c = 3).

title: Direct Waves in Black-Hole Binary Mergers: Insights from the Backwards One Body Model
summary: The merger-ringdown radiation from a black hole binary merger is accurately modeled by a sum of linear quasinormal modes (QNMs). Recently, a non-QNM ``direct wave" component of the radiation, associated with prompt emission from a plunging perturber, has been identified. Motivated by the behavior of null geodesics perturbed from the remnant light ring, the Backwards One Body (BOB) approach has been shown to model the full merger-ringdown radiation to high accuracy, while using only a minimal number of parameters. In this work, using the Pöschl--Teller potential, we first show how the BOB amplitude evolution can be recovered from the QNM pole contributions. We then apply rational filters to isolate the non-QNM content in BOB and numerical relativity waveforms. We show that BOB naturally captures the direct wave component of the merger radiation, explaining its accuracy near the waveform peak. Finally, we use BOB to show that the direct wave frequency is largely uncorrelated with the horizon frequency, even for high spin remnants, and instead tracks the News frequency at the time of the peak News amplitude.

AI.tldr: This paper explains how the Backwards One Body (BOB) model captures both quasinormal mode (QNM) and non-QNM 'direct wave' components of black hole merger-ringdown gravitational waveforms, using the Pöschl–Teller potential and rational filtering; it reveals that the direct wave frequency correlates with peak News frequency—not horizon frequency—even for highly spinning remnants.
AI.motivation: To understand why the BOB model accurately reproduces merger-ringdown waveforms, particularly near the peak, by identifying and isolating the recently discovered non-QNM 'direct wave' component associated with prompt plunging emission and linking it to light-ring geodesic behavior.
AI.method: Analytical derivation using the Pöschl–Teller potential to recover BOB amplitude evolution from QNM pole sums; application of rational filters to separate QNM and non-QNM contributions in BOB and numerical relativity waveforms; comparison of direct wave frequency with remnant horizon frequency and News frequency at peak News amplitude.
AI.result: BOB inherently encodes the direct wave component, explaining its high fidelity near waveform peak; the direct wave frequency is uncorrelated with horizon frequency (even for high-spin remnants) but closely tracks the News frequency evaluated at the time of peak News amplitude.
AI.conclusion: The BOB framework provides a physically grounded, parameter-efficient description of merger-ringdown radiation that unifies QNM and direct wave physics; the direct wave is governed by null geodesic dynamics near the light ring—not horizon properties—making News frequency the relevant physical scale.

title: Glimpses of the X17 from coherent elastic neutrino nucleus scattering
summary: We show that the process of Coherent Elastic neutrino (v) Nucleus Scattering (CEvNS) at nuclear reactor experiments has significant sensitivity to the so-called X17 particle, which has been invoked to explain the ATOMKI anomaly, wherein electron-positron pairs emerging from a nuclear transition of excited Be-8, He-4 and C-12 nuclei are studied. Such a new state has potentially been identified as a spin-1 object, with axial-vector couplings and a mass around 16.7 MeV, hence, in the kinematic range accessible by the aforementioned experimental settings. Specifically, we fit CONUS+ and Dresden-II data and show that a robust statistical analysis renders these more compatible with the X17 hypothesis, in turn interfering with the Standard Model, than with that of the latter alone. The same stays true when also adding COHERENT data from pi+ decays at rest, singling out two regions of preferred couplings of the X17 to electron and muon neutrinos as well as nuclei.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the sensitivity of coherent elastic neutrino-nucleus scattering (CEvNS) experiments—CONUS+, Dresden-II, and COHERENT—to the hypothetical X17 boson (16.7 MeV, spin-1, axial-vector), proposed to explain the ATOMKI anomaly; statistical fits favor the X17 hypothesis over the Standard Model alone and identify preferred coupling regions to νₑ, ν_μ, and nuclei.
AI.motivation: To test whether CEvNS experiments at nuclear reactors and spallation sources can probe the X17 particle—a proposed explanation for the anomalous e⁺e⁻ excesses observed in nuclear transitions of Be-8, He-4, and C-12 at ATOMKI.
AI.method: Statistical analysis (likelihood fitting) of CEvNS data from CONUS+, Dresden-II, and COHERENT (π⁺ decay-at-rest) under two hypotheses: Standard Model-only vs. SM + X17-mediated contribution; X17 modeled as a light axial-vector boson with couplings to neutrinos and nuclei.
AI.result: The X17 hypothesis yields significantly better fits to CONUS+ and Dresden-II data than the SM alone; inclusion of COHERENT data further constrains and identifies two distinct preferred regions in the (g_νₑ, g_ν_μ, g_nucleus) coupling parameter space.
AI.conclusion: Reactor and stopped-pion CEvNS experiments provide competitive, model-independent sensitivity to the X17 particle; current data moderately favor its existence, urging dedicated searches and refined theoretical interpretation of nuclear form factors and backgrounds.

title: A Minimal Realization of Radiative Dirac Neutrino Masses via a Non-Invertible Fusion Rule
summary: We propose a minimal one-loop radiative framework for Dirac neutrino mass matrix. As a consequence, the Yukawa hierarchies among the SM fermions are alleviated, and radiative type-I seesaw framework is realized. To regulate divergent loop contributions, we introduce an effective cutoff scale $Λ\sim 100 \, {\rm TeV}$. By introducing a scalar leptoquark and imposing appropriate assignments of ising fusion rule to the particle content, we successfully realize a minimal construction. Furthermore, the presence of the leptoquark leads to rich phenomenology, including semi-leptonic decays, neutral meson mixing, lepton flavor violations and lepton $g-2$, thereby rendering the model experimentally testable. After formulating each sector of our model, we perform a comprehensive numerical analysis, taking into account all relevant experimental constraints for both normal and inverted hierarchies of neutrino masses. Our analysis reveals characteristic tendencies within the viable parameter space.

AI.tldr: Proposes a minimal one-loop radiative Dirac neutrino mass model using a scalar leptoquark and Ising fusion rules, alleviating Yukawa hierarchies, realizing radiative type-I seesaw, and yielding testable phenomenology (e.g., LFV, meson mixing, g−2), with numerical analysis under current experimental constraints for both neutrino mass orderings.
AI.motivation: To construct a minimal, natural, and testable framework for Dirac neutrino masses that avoids fine-tuned Yukawa couplings and explains small neutrino masses radiatively without introducing right-handed neutrinos in the conventional seesaw sense.
AI.method: Introduces a scalar leptoquark transforming under an Ising-type discrete symmetry; employs one-loop radiative generation of Dirac neutrino mass; imposes cutoff scale Λ ∼ 100 TeV to regulate UV sensitivity; performs full numerical scan over parameter space subject to flavor, collider, and precision observables.
AI.result: Identifies viable parameter regions consistent with neutrino oscillation data (both NH and IH), LFV bounds (e.g., μ → eγ), B-meson decays (e.g., B → K(*)νν), meson mixing (K⁰–K̄⁰, Bₛ–B̄ₛ), and (g−2)ₑ,μ; reveals correlations among leptoquark couplings and mass scale; shows hierarchical suppression of off-diagonal neutrino entries arises naturally from symmetry and loop dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The model provides a predictive, minimally extended SM framework for Dirac neutrinos with rich, falsifiable low-energy signatures; it demonstrates that leptoquarks under discrete symmetries can simultaneously address neutrino mass generation and flavor anomalies while remaining compatible with stringent experimental limits.

title: Effects of equilibrium coexisting phases in the first-order chiral transition within the Linear sigma model with quarks
comment: 14pages, 10 figures. Accepted for publication in Physical Review C
summary: The first order chiral phase transition for quark matter with flavor imbalance is studied using the Linear sigma model with quarks, also known as Quark-meson model. Special attention is paid to the role of the scalar isovector meson. The general consensus presently is that the chiral transition changes from a smooth crossover to first-order at low temperatures. This transition is assumed to be discontinuous, with unstable or metastable intermediate states. However, if multiple charges are simultaneously conserved the system could undergo a continuous change through a coexistence of equilibrium states. Under such assumption the bulk properties are analyzed and several remarkable effects for the speed of sound and the susceptibilities are stressed.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the chiral phase transition in flavor-imbalanced quark matter using the Quark-meson model, highlighting the role of the scalar isovector meson; it challenges the standard first-order/discontinuous picture by considering multi-charge conservation, leading to continuous transitions via coexisting equilibrium phases, with notable implications for speed of sound and thermodynamic susceptibilities.
AI.motivation: To re-examine the nature of the chiral phase transition in flavor-imbalanced quark matter—specifically whether it must be first-order and discontinuous, or can instead be continuous under simultaneous conservation of multiple charges (e.g., baryon number, isospin), which permits phase coexistence.
AI.method: Uses the Linear Sigma Model with quarks (Quark-meson model) to compute thermodynamic properties; incorporates the scalar isovector meson explicitly; analyzes phase structure, speed of sound, and generalized susceptibilities under constraints of multiple conserved charges.
AI.result: Demonstrates that enforcing simultaneous conservation of multiple charges allows for continuous (rather than discontinuous) chiral transitions through equilibrium phase coexistence; identifies anomalous non-monotonic behavior in the speed of sound and divergent/cusp-like structures in higher-order susceptibilities near the transition.
AI.conclusion: The assumption of multiple exact charge conservations qualitatively alters the chiral transition paradigm: it enables continuous transitions with coexisting phases, invalidating the generic expectation of metastability and hysteresis; this has critical implications for heavy-ion collision phenomenology and neutron star interiors where flavor asymmetry and charge conservation are essential.

title: Why Quarks and Leptons Demand Different Symmetries: A Systematic Z3 Froggatt-Nielsen Analysis
summary: We present a systematic analysis of a minimal Z_3 discrete flavor symmetry as a solution to the fermion mass hierarchy problem. Using a Froggatt-Nielsen mechanism with generation-dependent Z_3 charges assigned to the right-handed fermions, we show that a single expansion parameter epsilon ~ 0.015 structurally accounts for the hierarchical pattern of quark and charged lepton mass ratios with O(1) Yukawa couplings. A Monte Carlo scan over 10^5 random O(1) coefficient sets confirms that adjacent-generation mass ratios generically fall within the experimentally measured ranges. By contrast, the CKM mixing angles, while reproducible with specific O(1) coefficient choices (chi^2/dof ~ 1.6), are not structurally predicted by the symmetry. When the same framework is extended to neutrinos within a type-I seesaw, it fails decisively on two fronts. First, the mass spectrum is far too hierarchical: the model predicts Delta m^2_{21}/Delta m^2_{31} < 10^{-4}, at least two orders of magnitude below the observed ratio of 0.030. Second, the PMNS mixing angles are generically O(1) random, consistent with Haar-distributed unitaries. When M_R carries the Z_3 charge structure dictated by the correct Majorana charge algebra, the mass spectrum failure deepens catastrophically through a pseudo-Dirac mechanism. These results motivate a sectorial view of flavor where different fermion sectors arise from distinct symmetry mechanisms.

AI.tldr: This paper explores a minimal Z_3 discrete flavor symmetry with Froggatt-Nielsen mechanism to explain fermion mass hierarchies; it successfully reproduces quark and charged lepton mass ratios via a single small parameter ε ≈ 0.015, but fails for neutrino masses and mixings—predicting excessive hierarchy (Δm²₂₁/Δm²₃₁ < 10⁻⁴ vs observed 0.030) and random PMNS angles—suggesting sector-specific flavor symmetries.
AI.motivation: To address the fermion mass hierarchy problem using the simplest possible discrete flavor symmetry (Z_3), testing whether a unified symmetry mechanism can simultaneously explain quark, charged lepton, and neutrino mass patterns.
AI.method: Implements a Froggatt-Nielsen mechanism with generation-dependent Z_3 charges on right-handed fermions; uses a single expansion parameter ε ≈ 0.015; performs Monte Carlo sampling of O(1) Yukawa coefficients (10⁵ points); extends model to neutrinos via type-I seesaw, enforcing consistent Z_3 charge assignments for Dirac and Majorana mass matrices.
AI.result: Quark and charged lepton mass ratios are robustly reproduced within experimental bounds; CKM angles require fine-tuned O(1) coefficients (χ²/dof ≈ 1.6) but are not structurally predicted; neutrino sector fails catastrophically: predicted Δm²₂₁/Δm²₃₁ < 10⁻⁴ (vs observed ~0.030), and PMNS angles are Haar-random; imposing correct Z_3 charge algebra on M_R triggers a pseudo-Dirac mechanism worsening the hierarchy.
AI.conclusion: A universal Z_3 flavor symmetry cannot simultaneously accommodate charged fermion and neutrino data; the failure motivates a sectorial flavor paradigm—distinct symmetry mechanisms for quark/charged-lepton vs. neutrino sectors.

title: Probing the neutrino mass through semileptonic meson decays
summary: We argue that a detailed analysis of semileptonic decays can test the possibility of a massive neutrino. The key observable, related to the forward-backward asymmetry, is exactly zero for a massless neutrino but becomes non-zero if the neutral lepton is heavy and interacts with Standard Model fields via left-handed operators. For right-handed interactions, this quantity differs significantly from zero even for a massless right-handed neutrino. We demonstrate this explicitly using the example of a pseudoscalar meson decaying into another pseudoscalar meson. A similar discussion applies to decays into a vector meson, with an additional subtlety addressed in this work.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using forward-backward asymmetry in semileptonic decays as a probe for neutrino mass and chirality, showing it vanishes for massless left-handed neutrinos but is non-zero for massive or right-handed neutrinos.
AI.motivation: To test whether neutrinos have non-zero mass and to distinguish between left-handed and right-handed neutrino interactions with Standard Model fields via precision analysis of semileptonic decays.
AI.method: Analytical calculation of the forward-backward asymmetry in semileptonic decays of pseudoscalar (and vector) mesons, assuming left- or right-handed effective operators coupling neutrinos to SM fields.
AI.result: The forward-backward asymmetry is identically zero for massless left-handed neutrinos, but becomes non-zero for massive left-handed neutrinos or for right-handed neutrinos—even if massless—enabling discrimination between mass and chirality scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Semileptonic decay asymmetries provide a clean, model-independent observable to constrain neutrino mass and chiral structure, complementary to oscillation and direct mass experiments.

title: QCD-driven dark matter: AQNs formation and observational tests
comment: Submitted to SciPost Physics Lecture Notes, Les Houches Summer School 2025 "Dark Universe"
summary: The nature of dark energy remains a central problem in cosmology. A compelling possibility is that dark matter is macroscopic, consisting of composite objects formed in the early Universe. We introduce the QCD-AQN framework, a well-motivated scenario in which dark matter is composed of dense aggregates of quarks and antiquarks matter stabilised by axion domain walls. The framework proposes a unified explanation for both dark matter and the observed matter-antimatter asymmetry. Particular emphasis is placed on existing observational constraints and on observational tests. Finally, we explore a possible QCD-based scenario for dark energy.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes the QCD-AQN framework, where dark matter consists of macroscopic quark-antiquark aggregates stabilized by axion domain walls, offering a unified explanation for dark matter and baryon asymmetry; it reviews observational constraints and suggests a QCD-based mechanism for dark energy.
AI.motivation: To address the nature of dark energy and provide a unified explanation for dark matter and the matter-antimatter asymmetry using well-motivated particle physics (QCD + axions).
AI.method: Theoretical construction of the QCD-AQN framework—macroscopic, dense quark-antiquark nuggets stabilized by axion domain walls—and analysis of its phenomenological consequences, including compatibility with existing astrophysical and cosmological constraints.
AI.result: The QCD-AQN framework is shown to be consistent with current observational bounds; it naturally accounts for dark matter abundance and baryon asymmetry, and hints at a QCD-origin for dark energy.
AI.conclusion: The QCD-AQN framework is a viable, testable, and theoretically grounded candidate for explaining dark matter and baryogenesis; its extension to dark energy remains speculative but motivates new observational probes.
